title.en: The Book of the Dead: Aaru's Gate

preview.en: A young embalmer’s assistant inherits a forbidden set of funerary spells and learns that the gate to Aaru opens not for the pure, but for the properly named.
preview.id: Seorang asisten pembalsem muda mewarisi kumpulan mantra pemakaman terlarang dan menyadari bahwa gerbang Aaru terbuka bukan bagi yang ‘paling suci’, melainkan bagi yang namanya disebut dengan benar.

title.en: The Name That Must Not Break

content.en:
The day the river withdrew, the city pretended it was a normal drought. Merchants complained about barges. Priests argued about offerings. Scribes counted grain as if numbers could intimidate the sky.

But the embalmers knew the truth first.

When the Nile shrank, graves rose.

Old tomb mouths appeared along the banks like stones remembering they once had voices, and the air carried a smell that was not only mud but time.

In the House of Purification, where bodies were washed and made ready for eternity, work became frantic. Not because more people died—people always died—but because death began arriving with a new impatience.

Khaem was seventeen and had hands that smelled permanently of natron and cedar resin. He was an assistant, not a master, which meant he was allowed to touch everything and decide nothing.

He cleaned knives and bowls. He heated oils. He held linen while older hands wrapped the dead with the careful tenderness of men who feared mistakes more than grief.

The master embalmer, Hori, rarely spoke during work. Words were risky in a place where names determined whether a soul would be recognized or lost.

On the morning Chapter 1 begins, they received a body with no family.

The dead man arrived on a reed stretcher, face covered, wrists bound by twine that had been cut and retied too many times.

No priest accompanied him.

No mourners followed.

Only a clay tag hung from his ankle with a single mark that was not a name.

Hori stared at the tag longer than normal. Then he told Khaem to lock the doors.

Khaem obeyed, heart beating too loud in the sudden hush.

Hori pulled the cloth from the dead man’s face.

The man was not old. His skin had the gray cast of those who drowned, but there was no water in his lungs. His lips were stained black, as if he had bitten ink.

Khaem flinched.

Hori did not.

He leaned close and whispered, almost respectfully, 'Someone tried to erase you.'

Khaem did not understand.

Hori opened the dead man’s mouth and slid two fingers beneath the tongue.

He withdrew a thin roll of papyrus, slick with saliva and darkened by something that was not rot.

Khaem’s stomach turned.

Hori set the papyrus on the table as if setting down a serpent.

There was writing.

Not the tidy script of a scribe.

These glyphs were cramped, urgent, and wrong in their angles, as if they had been carved by a hand that did not believe it had time.

Khaem recognized the first words with a jolt.

They were funerary words.

Spells.

Not the approved prayers sung in public processions, but the private formulas that spoke directly to gates, to guardians, to names that were older than the temples.

Hori watched Khaem’s eyes widen and said, 'Do you know what you are looking at?'

Khaem answered honestly: 'I know what it resembles.'

Hori nodded. 'Then you know why it cannot be seen.'

He moved to a hidden niche behind the linen racks and pulled out a wooden box sealed with pitch.

He broke the seal with a knife tip.

Inside lay a bundle of papyri wrapped in black cloth.

Khaem felt the room cool, though the sun outside was hot.

Hori said, 'This city thinks the afterlife is a reward. The priests teach that the good will be carried. But the old truth is harsher and more practical.'

He tapped the papyri.

'The dead do not float into eternity. They navigate it.'

Khaem swallowed.

Hori continued: 'And navigation requires words that are exact. Names. Titles. Confessions. Passwords. If a name breaks, a soul breaks with it.'

Khaem looked at the dead man again and understood what had frightened Hori.

Someone had sent this body without a name.

And someone had hidden these words under his tongue, like a key concealed in a wound.

Hori told Khaem to read the first line aloud.

Khaem hesitated. Speaking spells felt like lighting a lamp in a tomb.

Hori’s eyes hardened. 'If you cannot read, you cannot help. And if you cannot help, you will eventually be the one with the tag that has no name.'

Khaem read.

His voice shook, but the words were clear.

As he spoke, the lamps in the room flickered—not as miracle, but as if the air itself had to adjust to make room for a language it remembered.

The papyrus under the dead man’s tongue quivered.

Khaem stopped.

Hori said, quietly, 'Do not fear the trembling. Fear the silence.'

Khaem looked down at the line he had read.

It did not ask for mercy.

It did not praise any god.

It said only: I am named. Therefore I may pass.

Khaem felt a chill that had nothing to do with water.

If the afterlife could be gated by language, then morality was not enough.

A good man could be lost if his name was stolen.

A cruel man could pass if his words were correct.

Hori closed the box and pushed it toward Khaem.

'You will carry this,' he said.

Khaem’s hands jerked back. 'Me?'

Hori answered, 'Yes. Because you still believe names are only labels. You haven’t learned yet how easily a person can be turned into a blank.'

Khaem wanted to refuse.

But refusal did not exist in a room full of dead who could not refuse anything.

Hori told him the task in three parts.

First: prepare the nameless body properly, even without family, because neglect would be a second erasure.

Second: find out who the dead man was before sundown, because the longer a name is missing, the easier it is for the world to accept the absence.

Third: do not show the box to temple priests, to city scribes, or to anyone who profits from controlling the story of what happens after death.

Khaem asked the question he could not hold back: 'Why would anyone erase a name?'

Hori’s answer was simple.

'Because a name is a passport,' he said. 'And passports are power.'

Outside, the sun climbed.

Inside, Khaem washed his hands, but the smell of natron did not leave.

He looked at the nameless dead man and felt, for the first time, that death was not the end of a life.

It was the beginning of a legal dispute.

Chapter 1 ends with Khaem tying the black cloth bundle beneath his tunic and stepping out of the House of Purification into the city—carrying a book of words that could open Aaru, and realizing that the first gate he must face is not in the Duat.

It is in the living world, where people decide whose names deserve to remain intact.
content.id:
Pada hari ketika sungai surut, kota pura-pura itu hanya kekeringan biasa. Pedagang mengeluh soal perahu. Pendeta berdebat soal persembahan. Juru tulis menghitung gandum seolah angka bisa mengintimidasi langit.

Namun para pembalsem lebih dulu tahu kebenarannya.

Saat Nil menyusut, kubur bangkit.

Mulut-mulut makam lama muncul di tepi sungai seperti batu yang mengingat pernah punya suara, dan udara membawa bau yang bukan hanya lumpur, melainkan waktu.

Di Rumah Pemurnian, tempat tubuh dicuci dan disiapkan untuk keabadian, pekerjaan menjadi panik. Bukan karena lebih banyak orang mati—orang selalu mati—melainkan karena kematian datang dengan ketidaksabaran baru.

Khaem berusia tujuh belas dan tangannya berbau natron dan resin aras yang menempel selamanya. Ia asisten, bukan ahli, artinya ia boleh menyentuh segalanya tetapi tak boleh memutuskan apa pun.

Ia membersihkan pisau dan mangkuk. Ia memanaskan minyak. Ia menahan linen sementara tangan-tangan lebih tua membungkus orang mati dengan kelembutan hati-hati dari orang yang lebih takut salah daripada sedih.

Ahli pembalsem, Hori, jarang berbicara saat bekerja. Kata-kata berbahaya di tempat di mana nama menentukan apakah jiwa dikenali atau hilang.

Pada pagi ketika Chapter 1 dimulai, mereka menerima jenazah tanpa keluarga.

Orang mati itu datang di tandu ilalang, wajah tertutup, pergelangan tangan terikat tali yang sudah dipotong dan diikat ulang terlalu sering.

Tidak ada pendeta yang mengantar.

Tidak ada pelayat yang mengikuti.

Hanya sebuah label tanah liat tergantung di pergelangan kaki dengan satu tanda yang bukan nama.

Hori menatap label itu lebih lama dari biasa. Lalu ia menyuruh Khaem mengunci pintu.

Khaem patuh, jantung berdetak terlalu keras dalam senyap mendadak.

Hori menarik kain dari wajah jenazah.

Pria itu tidak tua. Kulitnya abu-abu seperti orang tenggelam, tetapi tidak ada air di paru-parunya. Bibirnya ternoda hitam, seolah ia menggigit tinta.

Khaem tersentak.

Hori tidak.

Ia mendekat dan berbisik, hampir hormat, 'Seseorang mencoba menghapusmu.'

Khaem tidak paham.

Hori membuka mulut jenazah dan menyelipkan dua jari di bawah lidah.

Ia menarik selembar gulungan papirus tipis, licin oleh liur dan menghitam oleh sesuatu yang bukan busuk.

Perut Khaem berbalik.

Hori meletakkan papirus itu di meja seolah meletakkan ular.

Ada tulisan.

Bukan tulisan rapi juru tulis.

Hieroglifnya sempit, tergesa, dan salah sudut, seolah dipahat tangan yang tidak percaya ia punya waktu.

Khaem mengenali kata pertama dan terkejut.

Itu kata-kata pemakaman.

Mantra.

Bukan doa resmi yang dinyanyikan dalam prosesi umum, melainkan formula privat yang berbicara langsung pada gerbang, penjaga, dan nama-nama yang lebih tua dari kuil.

Hori melihat mata Khaem membesar dan bertanya, 'Kau tahu apa yang kau lihat?'

Khaem menjawab jujur: 'Aku tahu apa yang mirip dengannya.'

Hori mengangguk. 'Kalau begitu kau tahu kenapa itu tidak boleh terlihat.'

Ia membuka ceruk tersembunyi di balik rak linen dan mengeluarkan kotak kayu yang disegel ter.

Ia memecah segel itu dengan ujung pisau.

Di dalamnya ada ikatan papirus dibungkus kain hitam.

Khaem merasakan ruangan mendingin, padahal matahari di luar panas.

Hori berkata, 'Kota ini mengira akhirat itu hadiah. Para pendeta mengajar yang baik akan dibawa. Tapi kebenaran lama lebih keras dan lebih praktis.'

Ia mengetuk papirus.

'Orang mati tidak mengapung ke keabadian. Mereka menavigasinya.'

Khaem menelan ludah.

Hori melanjutkan: 'Dan navigasi butuh kata-kata yang presisi. Nama. Gelar. Pengakuan. Kata sandi. Kalau nama retak, jiwa retak bersamanya.'

Khaem menatap jenazah itu lagi dan mengerti apa yang menakutkan Hori.

Seseorang mengirim tubuh ini tanpa nama.

Dan seseorang menyembunyikan kata-kata ini di bawah lidahnya, seperti kunci yang diselipkan di luka.

Hori menyuruh Khaem membaca baris pertama keras-keras.

Khaem ragu. Mengucap mantra terasa seperti menyalakan lampu di makam.

Mata Hori mengeras. 'Kalau kau tak bisa baca, kau tak bisa menolong. Dan kalau kau tak bisa menolong, suatu hari kau yang akan punya label tanpa nama.'

Khaem membaca.

Suaranya gemetar, tetapi kata-katanya jelas.

Saat ia mengucap, lampu-lampu ruangan berkelip—bukan mukjizat, melainkan seolah udara harus menyesuaikan diri memberi ruang untuk bahasa yang ia ingat.

Papirus di bawah lidah jenazah bergetar.

Khaem berhenti.

Hori berkata pelan, 'Jangan takut getarannya. Takutlah pada diam.'

Khaem menatap baris yang baru ia baca.

Baris itu tidak meminta belas kasih.

Baris itu tidak memuji dewa mana pun.

Baris itu hanya berkata: Aku bernama. Maka aku boleh lewat.

Dingin menyusup ke Khaem tanpa air.

Jika akhirat bisa digembok oleh bahasa, maka moral saja tidak cukup.

Orang baik bisa hilang jika namanya dicuri.

Orang kejam bisa lewat jika kata-katanya benar.

Hori menutup kotak dan mendorongnya ke arah Khaem.

'Kau akan membawa ini,' katanya.

Tangan Khaem tersentak mundur. 'Aku?'

Hori menjawab, 'Ya. Karena kau masih percaya nama hanya label. Kau belum belajar betapa mudah seseorang diubah menjadi kosong.'

Khaem ingin menolak.

Namun penolakan tidak ada di ruangan penuh orang mati yang tak bisa menolak apa pun.

Hori memberi tugas dalam tiga bagian.

Pertama: siapkan jenazah tanpa nama itu dengan benar, meski tanpa keluarga, karena pengabaian adalah penghapusan kedua.

Kedua: cari tahu siapa dia sebelum matahari terbenam, karena semakin lama nama hilang, semakin mudah dunia menerima ketiadaan itu.

Ketiga: jangan tunjukkan kotak itu pada pendeta kuil, juru tulis kota, atau siapa pun yang diuntungkan oleh mengendalikan cerita tentang apa yang terjadi setelah mati.

Khaem bertanya hal yang tak bisa ia tahan: 'Kenapa orang menghapus nama?'

Jawaban Hori sederhana.

'Karena nama itu paspor,' katanya. 'Dan paspor itu kuasa.'

Di luar, matahari naik.

Di dalam, Khaem mencuci tangan, tetapi bau natron tidak pergi.

Ia menatap jenazah tanpa nama dan merasakan, untuk pertama kalinya, bahwa kematian bukan akhir kehidupan.

Kematian adalah awal sengketa hukum.

Chapter 1 berakhir ketika Khaem menyelipkan bungkusan kain hitam di balik tuniknya dan melangkah keluar dari Rumah Pemurnian menuju kota—membawa kitab kata-kata yang bisa membuka Aaru, dan sadar bahwa gerbang pertama yang harus ia hadapi bukan di Duat.

Gerbang pertama ada di dunia hidup, tempat orang memutuskan nama siapa yang pantas tetap utuh.

content.en:
Khaem reads the first funerary line aloud, careful with every consonant as if the air itself might misfile a syllable.

The lamps flicker.

Then the dead man exhales.

Not a breath of life—an empty release, like a jar unsealed.

Hori’s hand snaps to Khaem’s wrist, hard enough to bruise. 'Do not move,' he whispers.

The dead man’s lips part.

A voice emerges that is not fully sound. It is closer to writing—dry, thin, insistent.

It speaks one sentence: 'My name is not missing. It was taken.'

Khaem’s heart pounds. Hori’s face tightens with fury and fear.

The voice continues, each word scraping the room: 'If you wrap me without my name, you will bury me twice.'

Khaem looks to Hori for permission, but Hori has no permission to give. The House of Purification does not have rules for the dead interrupting their own paperwork.

The voice forces a choice: treat it as taboo and remain safe, or treat it as testimony and become dangerous.

Hori grips Khaem’s shoulder and says, 'Names do not speak. People speak.'

The dead man’s jaw trembles as if laughing. 'People lie,' the voice answers.

Khaem realizes the papyrus is not merely hidden. It is anchored.

Someone wrote spells into a body to keep a message from being burned.

Hori orders Khaem to stop reading.

Khaem disobeys for the first time in his life.

He reads the second line.

The voice strengthens.

The dead man’s eyes do not open, but the room feels watched.

The voice gives Khaem a clue: a nickname only the living would use, and a location where the name was stolen.

Then the jaw relaxes.

The body returns to stillness.

Hori releases Khaem and says, quietly, 'Now you understand. This is not a body. It is a complaint.'

Chapter 1 ends with Khaem stepping out into the city with a new terror: he is not carrying a forbidden book.

He is carrying a witness that cannot testify again unless Khaem risks making the dead speak twice.
content.id:
Khaem membaca baris pertama kata-kata pemakaman keras-keras, hati-hati pada setiap konsonan seolah udara sendiri bisa salah mengarsip satu suku kata.

Lampu-lampu berkelip.

Lalu jenazah itu mengembus.

Bukan napas hidup—melainkan pelepasan kosong, seperti kendi yang segelnya dibuka.

Tangan Hori menyambar pergelangan Khaem, keras sampai memar. 'Jangan bergerak,' bisiknya.

Bibir jenazah itu terbuka.

Suara keluar yang tidak sepenuhnya bunyi. Lebih dekat ke tulisan—kering, tipis, memaksa.

Ia mengucapkan satu kalimat: 'Namaku bukan hilang. Namaku diambil.'

Jantung Khaem berdebar. Wajah Hori mengeras oleh marah dan takut.

Suara itu lanjut, tiap kata menggesek ruangan: 'Kalau kau membungkusku tanpa namaku, kau menguburku dua kali.'

Khaem menatap Hori minta izin, tetapi Hori tak punya izin untuk memberi. Rumah Pemurnian tidak punya aturan untuk orang mati yang menyela urusan berkasnya sendiri.

Suara itu memaksa pilihan: anggap tabu agar tetap aman, atau anggap kesaksian agar menjadi berbahaya.

Hori mencengkeram bahu Khaem dan berkata, 'Nama tidak bicara. Orang yang bicara.'

Rahang jenazah bergetar seolah tertawa. 'Orang bohong,' jawab suara itu.

Khaem sadar papirus itu bukan sekadar disembunyikan. Ia ditambatkan.

Seseorang menulis mantra ke tubuh untuk mencegah pesan dibakar.

Hori menyuruh Khaem berhenti membaca.

Untuk pertama kali seumur hidup, Khaem tidak patuh.

Ia membaca baris kedua.

Suara itu menguat.

Mata jenazah tidak membuka, tetapi ruangan terasa sedang ditatap.

Suara itu memberi petunjuk: nama panggilan yang hanya dipakai orang hidup, dan lokasi tempat namanya dicuri.

Lalu rahang itu mengendur.

Tubuh kembali diam.

Hori melepas Khaem dan berkata pelan, 'Sekarang kau paham. Ini bukan jenazah. Ini pengaduan.'

Chapter 1 berakhir saat Khaem melangkah keluar ke kota dengan teror baru: ia bukan sedang membawa kitab terlarang.

Ia sedang membawa saksi yang tak bisa bersaksi lagi kecuali Khaem berani membuat orang mati bicara dua kali.

content.en:
Hori shows Khaem the sealed box of funerary papyri and speaks about names as passports.

Khaem believes him.

He believes because Hori’s hands are steady and his voice is controlled, the way authority is supposed to look.

That night, after the House of Purification closes, Khaem returns quietly to fetch forgotten linen.

He hears a sound from Hori’s workroom—paper sliding.

Khaem pauses at the crack of the door and sees Hori alone, not embalming, but sorting clay tags.

Name tags.

Dozens.

Hori lines them up beside small rolls of papyrus, comparing marks, making substitutions with the calm of a man balancing accounts.

Khaem’s mouth goes dry.

Hori notices him without turning, as if he has always known Khaem would be curious.

He says, 'You wanted to know why anyone would erase a name.'

Khaem tries to speak but can only breathe.

Hori continues, 'Because the afterlife is crowded. Because the living pay to secure passage. Because priests sell purity, and embalmers sell certainty.'

Khaem whispers, 'You’re stealing names.'

Hori corrects him gently: 'I’m redistributing them. Names are wasted on the poor. They die without families to pay for ink.'

Khaem feels nausea. Hori is using charity as a mask.

Hori offers Khaem a deal: stay silent and become his successor. Learn the real economy of eternity.

Khaem’s hands shake. Refusing means danger. Accepting means becoming the mechanism.

He remembers the nameless body on the table.

A man erased.

Khaem says, 'If names are passports, you’re forging borders.'

Hori’s calm cracks for the first time. 'Borders already exist,' he snaps. 'I’m only charging for them.'

Chapter 1 ends with Khaem backing out into the corridor, realizing the forbidden papyri were never meant to save souls.

They were inventory.

And the person who taught him to fear erasure may be the one profiting from it.
content.id:
Hori menunjukkan kotak papirus pemakaman tersegel dan bicara tentang nama sebagai paspor.

Khaem percaya.

Ia percaya karena tangan Hori stabil dan suaranya terkendali—seperti otoritas seharusnya terlihat.

Malam itu, setelah Rumah Pemurnian tutup, Khaem kembali diam-diam untuk mengambil linen yang tertinggal.

Ia mendengar bunyi dari ruang kerja Hori—kertas bergeser.

Khaem berhenti di celah pintu dan melihat Hori sendirian, bukan membalsem, melainkan menyortir label tanah liat.

Label nama.

Puluhan.

Hori menyusunnya sejajar dengan gulungan papirus kecil, membandingkan tanda, menukar satu dengan yang lain dengan tenang seperti orang menyeimbangkan buku kas.

Mulut Khaem kering.

Hori menyadari kehadirannya tanpa menoleh, seolah ia sudah tahu Khaem akan penasaran.

Hori berkata, 'Kau ingin tahu kenapa orang menghapus nama.'

Khaem mencoba bicara, tetapi hanya bisa bernapas.

Hori melanjutkan: 'Karena akhirat padat. Karena orang hidup membayar untuk memastikan jalan. Karena pendeta menjual kemurnian, dan pembalsem menjual kepastian.'

Khaem berbisik, 'Kau mencuri nama.'

Hori mengoreksi lembut: 'Aku mendistribusikannya ulang. Nama terbuang untuk orang miskin. Mereka mati tanpa keluarga yang bisa membayar tinta.'

Khaem mual. Hori memakai amal sebagai topeng.

Hori menawarkan kesepakatan: diam dan jadi penerusnya. Belajar ekonomi keabadian yang sebenarnya.

Tangan Khaem gemetar. Menolak berarti bahaya. Menerima berarti menjadi mesin.

Ia teringat jenazah tanpa nama di meja.

Seorang manusia yang dihapus.

Khaem berkata, 'Kalau nama itu paspor, kau sedang memalsukan perbatasan.'

Ketenangan Hori retak untuk pertama kalinya. 'Perbatasan sudah ada,' bentaknya. 'Aku cuma menarik biaya.'

Chapter 1 berakhir saat Khaem mundur ke koridor, sadar papirus terlarang itu tidak pernah dimaksudkan menyelamatkan jiwa.

Papirus itu stok.

Dan orang yang mengajarinya takut pada penghapusan mungkin justru orang yang untung dari penghapusan itu.

content.en:
Hori orders Khaem to read the first line aloud so the papyrus can be confirmed.

Khaem’s eyes track the cramped glyphs.

He recognizes the structure but not the hand.

He misreads one mark.

It is tiny—an angle, a feather stroke, the difference between two consonants that sound similar in a tired mouth.

The lamps flicker.

Then the air thickens.

Not cold—bureaucratic.

As if an unseen clerk has leaned over the room and begun writing a correction into the world.

Hori’s face goes pale.

He snatches the papyrus and hisses, 'Stop.'

Khaem freezes, ashamed.

The dead man’s chest does not rise.

But the clay tag on his ankle cracks.

A hairline fracture runs through the mark that is not a name.

Khaem hears something like distant footsteps in sand.

Hori whispers, 'You just called something by the wrong address.'

Khaem tries to apologize.

Hori shuts his mouth with two fingers and says, 'Never apologize to a gate. Gates don’t forgive. They only redirect.'

The room’s shadows lengthen toward the body.

For one breath, Khaem sees a shape at the edge of vision—tall, thin, with a jackal’s stillness.

It is not fully present.

It is checking.

Hori grabs a bowl of natron and throws a ring around the table.

He speaks a counter-line with brutal precision, correcting Khaem’s error by declaring the misread name invalid.

The pressure eases.

The shadows retract.

Khaem’s knees nearly give.

Hori stares at him and says, 'Now you understand why priests prefer morality. Morality can be vague. Names cannot.'

Khaem realizes the mission is worse than he thought.

He is not carrying poetry.

He is carrying addresses.

And one wrong syllable can summon the wrong judge.

Chapter 1 ends with Khaem leaving the House of Purification with shaking hands, knowing that he can doom a soul not through malice, but through a single tired mistake.
content.id:
Hori menyuruh Khaem membaca baris pertama keras-keras untuk memastikan papirusnya benar.

Mata Khaem mengikuti hieroglif sempit itu.

Ia mengenali strukturnya, tetapi tidak mengenali tangan penulisnya.

Ia salah membaca satu tanda.

Kecil sekali—sudut, gores bulu, beda dua konsonan yang terdengar mirip di mulut lelah.

Lampu berkelip.

Lalu udara menebal.

Bukan dingin—melainkan birokratis.

Seolah ada juru tulis tak terlihat yang menunduk ke ruangan dan mulai menulis koreksi ke dalam dunia.

Wajah Hori memucat.

Ia menyambar papirus dan mendesis, 'Berhenti.'

Khaem membeku, malu.

Dada jenazah tidak naik.

Namun label tanah liat di pergelangan kakinya retak.

Retak rambut menjalar melewati tanda yang bukan nama.

Khaem mendengar sesuatu seperti langkah kaki jauh di pasir.

Hori berbisik, 'Kau baru saja memanggil sesuatu ke alamat yang salah.'

Khaem mencoba minta maaf.

Hori menutup mulutnya dengan dua jari dan berkata, 'Jangan pernah minta maaf pada gerbang. Gerbang tidak memaafkan. Gerbang hanya mengalihkan.'

Bayangan ruangan memanjang ke arah tubuh.

Sesaat Khaem melihat bentuk di tepi penglihatan—tinggi, kurus, dengan diamnya serigala-padang.

Ia tidak sepenuhnya hadir.

Ia sedang memeriksa.

Hori meraih semangkuk natron dan menaburkan lingkaran mengitari meja.

Ia mengucapkan baris penangkal dengan presisi kejam, membatalkan kesalahan Khaem dengan menyatakan nama yang salah itu tidak sah.

Tekanan mengendur.

Bayangan menarik diri.

Lutut Khaem nyaris runtuh.

Hori menatapnya dan berkata, 'Sekarang kau paham kenapa pendeta lebih suka moral. Moral bisa kabur. Nama tidak bisa.'

Khaem sadar misinya lebih buruk dari yang ia kira.

Ia bukan membawa puisi.

Ia membawa alamat.

Dan satu suku kata yang salah bisa memanggil hakim yang salah.

Chapter 1 berakhir saat Khaem meninggalkan Rumah Pemurnian dengan tangan gemetar, sadar ia bisa menenggelamkan jiwa bukan karena jahat, melainkan karena satu kesalahan lelah.

content.en:
Hori seals the black-cloth bundle and pushes it toward Khaem.

Before Khaem can hide it, a knock strikes the outer door.

Three sharp taps.

Official taps.

Hori’s eyes narrow. He signals Khaem to stay still.

The knock repeats, then a voice calls out a temple title—high enough to be dangerous.

Hori opens the door a fraction.

A priest stands outside with two acolytes and a scribe holding a reed pen. The priest smiles like a man who already owns the answer.

He says the temple has heard of an 'irregular corpse' delivered without a name.

He says the House of Purification must surrender any unusual writings found with the body for 'doctrinal safety.'

Khaem feels the bundle under his tunic like a heartbeat.

Hori replies politely that no such writings exist.

The priest’s smile stays.

The scribe begins writing anyway.

Hori understands: denial will be recorded as guilt.

The priest steps forward, eyes scanning the room with practiced entitlement.

Khaem’s panic rises.

Then Hori does something that shocks him.

Hori lies.

He says the writings were already burned, as required.

The priest’s smile falters for the first time.

Burned means lost control.

The priest asks who gave the order.

Hori answers, 'You did. In your last letter.'

The priest turns to his scribe, confused.

The scribe hesitates.

Hori has created a bureaucratic trap: if the priest denies the letter, he admits he does not control his own records.

If he accepts the letter, he accepts the burning.

The priest chooses anger. He declares an inspection.

Hori bows and says inspections require a docket.

He hands the priest a stack of mundane receipts and forces the temple party into paperwork.

While the priest argues about procedure, Hori whispers to Khaem: 'Go. Now. Keep your head down. Remember: in this city, paper is a weapon. Use it as cover.'

Khaem slips out through a back passage, bundle tight against his ribs.

Behind him, the priest’s voice rises.

In front of him, the city opens like a maze.

Chapter 1 ends with Khaem running not from gods or monsters, but from administration—and realizing the first guardian of Aaru’s Gate is not Anubis.

It is the temple that wants to control who gets called by name.
content.id:
Hori menyegel bungkusan kain hitam dan mendorongnya ke arah Khaem.

Sebelum Khaem sempat menyembunyikannya, ketukan menghantam pintu luar.

Tiga ketukan tajam.

Ketukan resmi.

Mata Hori menyipit. Ia memberi isyarat Khaem untuk diam.

Ketukan terulang, lalu sebuah suara menyebut gelar kuil—cukup tinggi untuk berbahaya.

Hori membuka pintu sedikit.

Seorang pendeta berdiri di luar bersama dua akolit dan seorang juru tulis yang memegang pena buluh. Pendeta itu tersenyum seperti orang yang sudah memiliki jawabannya.

Ia berkata kuil mendengar ada 'jenazah tidak beres' yang datang tanpa nama.

Ia berkata Rumah Pemurnian harus menyerahkan tulisan apa pun yang tidak biasa demi 'keselamatan doktrin.'

Khaem merasakan bungkusan di balik tunik seperti detak.

Hori menjawab sopan bahwa tidak ada tulisan seperti itu.

Senyum pendeta tidak hilang.

Juru tulis mulai menulis juga.

Hori paham: penyangkalan akan dicatat sebagai rasa bersalah.

Pendeta melangkah masuk, mata menyapu ruangan dengan hak milik yang terlatih.

Panik Khaem naik.

Lalu Hori melakukan sesuatu yang mengejutkan.

Hori berbohong.

Ia berkata tulisannya sudah dibakar, sesuai aturan.

Senyum pendeta retak untuk pertama kali.

Dibakar berarti hilang kendali.

Pendeta bertanya siapa yang memerintah.

Hori menjawab, 'Anda. Dalam surat Anda yang terakhir.'

Pendeta menoleh ke juru tulisnya, bingung.

Juru tulis ragu.

Hori membuat jebakan birokrasi: jika pendeta menyangkal surat itu, ia mengakui ia tidak mengendalikan catatannya sendiri.

Jika ia menerima surat itu, ia menerima pembakaran.

Pendeta memilih marah. Ia memerintahkan inspeksi.

Hori membungkuk dan berkata inspeksi butuh docket.

Ia menyodorkan tumpukan kuitansi biasa dan memaksa rombongan kuil terjerat dalam berkas.

Saat pendeta berdebat soal prosedur, Hori berbisik pada Khaem: 'Pergi. Sekarang. Jangan menonjol. Ingat: di kota ini, kertas itu senjata. Pakai kertas sebagai kamuflase.'

Khaem menyelinap keluar lewat lorong belakang, bungkusan menekan rusuk.

Di belakangnya suara pendeta meninggi.

Di depannya, kota terbuka seperti labirin.

Chapter 1 berakhir dengan Khaem berlari bukan dari dewa atau monster, melainkan dari administrasi—dan menyadari penjaga pertama Gerbang Aaru bukan Anubis.

Penjaga pertama adalah kuil yang ingin mengendalikan siapa yang boleh dipanggil dengan nama.

preview.en: Khaem searches for the nameless dead man’s identity and discovers a hidden economy where names are bought, altered, and stolen—because the Duat has paperwork too.
preview.id: Khaem mencari identitas jenazah tanpa nama dan menemukan ekonomi tersembunyi: nama bisa dibeli, diubah, dan dicuri—karena Duat pun punya urusan berkas.

content.en:
Khaem did not run.

He walked.

He walked the way Hori had taught him to walk when carrying anything fragile: steady, ordinary, unmemorable.

Under his tunic the black-cloth bundle pressed his ribs like a second heartbeat. He could feel it whenever he inhaled, and he hated how quickly his body began to treat that pressure as normal.

The city outside the House of Purification was louder than the embalming rooms, but it had the same rule: everything important was done quietly.

Khaem moved through streets where sellers shouted about fish and figs, where children chased each other between baskets, and where priests in clean linen walked as if the ground owed them apologies.

He told himself he was only doing what Hori ordered: find the dead man’s name before sundown.

But the more he repeated the instruction, the more it sounded like a curse.

A name before sundown.

As if the sun itself was an official who would close a ledger.

Khaem started where the nameless dead had entered the world of the living for the last time: the riverbank.

The Nile’s withdrawal had exposed old stones and new shame. Men who had never been seen near tombs now pried at half-revealed doorways. Women who sold bread also sold gossip about what the river had uncovered.

Khaem asked carefully, without using the word 'body.'

He asked if anyone had seen a man carried on a reed stretcher.

Most people shook their heads.

One fisherman spat and said, 'Too many. That drought is a god with a knife.'

Khaem followed rumor instead of certainty. Rumor had better legs.

A washerwoman pointed him toward a side street where scribes took payments for letters the poor could not write.

A boy there, thin as a reed himself, offered Khaem information for copper.

Khaem hesitated.

He had little money.

The boy smiled as if he could smell scarcity.

He said, 'If you want a name, you pay. If you want it clean, you pay more.'

Khaem asked where names could be bought.

The boy pointed to an alley behind a granary and said, 'Not in the sun.'

Khaem’s stomach tightened. He understood that he was being guided, and that being guided was another kind of being handled.

Still, he went.

The alley smelled of grain dust and old ink. A door stood there that looked like any storeroom door, except for the mark scratched near its hinge: a small looped symbol Khaem recognized from the papyrus.

A gate-mark.

He knocked.

A slit opened.

A single eye assessed him.

The door opened just enough for Khaem to slip inside.

The room beyond was not a room. It was a workshop.

Tables held reed pens, palettes, seal stones, and rolls of papyrus stacked like loaves.

Men and women worked in silence, copying lists with hands that moved too fast to be holy.

On the far wall hung a board of clay tags.

Names.

Some complete.

Some smeared.

Some scratched out and rewritten.

Khaem felt his mouth go dry.

A woman approached him, older, with ink-stained fingertips and a calm that did not ask permission.

She asked his business.

Khaem did not speak the dead man’s problem directly. He had learned that explicit truth invited theft.

He said he needed to restore a name that had been 'misplaced.'

The woman’s lips curved slightly.

'Names do not misplace themselves,' she said.

Khaem asked if she was a scribe.

She answered, 'A scribe writes what is ordered. I write what is needed.'

Khaem noticed her neck: a small scar shaped like a cord had once tightened there.

He understood something without asking.

This woman had once been punished by the temple.

Now she made her living selling what the temple claimed to control.

She told Khaem her name was Nebet.

Then she added, 'And I keep it close. That is why I still have it.'

Nebet led Khaem to a table where a clay tag lay cracked in two.

'This one belonged to a man whose family paid,' she said.

'It still broke. Because ink is not protection. Ink is negotiation.'

Khaem asked what she meant.

Nebet pointed to a shelf of seal stones.

'Every seal is a promise,' she said. 'And every promise is for sale.'

She asked Khaem which authority he served.

Khaem lied in the smallest way possible.

He said, 'An embalmer.'

Nebet studied him. Then she nodded, as if that was credible enough.

She asked if he had the tag.

Khaem thought of the clay mark on the dead man’s ankle.

He did not have it.

He felt shame rise. He should have taken it.

Nebet saw the hesitation and sighed.

'You came without the broken thing,' she said. 'That means you came with the living fear instead.'

Khaem asked how to find a name without a tag.

Nebet answered, 'You find who profited.'

She explained the trade as if reciting weather.

Some names were stolen to erase debts.

Some were stolen to prevent inheritance.

Some were stolen because a priest wanted a soul to fail its own weighing and be reborn as labor.

Khaem felt anger flare.

He had been taught the weighing of the heart was sacred.

Nebet called it administration.

Khaem demanded proof.

Nebet did not argue.

She pulled out a thin papyrus strip and showed him a list of payments—copper, silver, grain—beside short notations.

Not names.

Descriptions.

'Widow. Dockworker. Scribe’s son. Foreman’s rival.'

Khaem realized the point.

In this market, people were reduced before they died.

A name was not identity.

It was leverage.

Nebet asked Khaem again what he wanted.

Khaem said, 'I want the name that was taken from a man delivered to the embalmers without family.'

Nebet’s calm finally shifted.

Just a fraction.

She said, 'That is not a common theft.'

Khaem asked why.

Nebet answered, 'Because erasing a man completely is expensive. It requires cooperation: the river, the priests, the clerks, the neighbors who look away.'

Khaem felt the city tilt.

He had imagined a single thief.

Nebet described a system.

She leaned closer and lowered her voice.

'If you keep asking,' she said, 'you will discover that Aaru’s Gate has a back door. And the back door is staffed.'

Khaem’s throat tightened.

He thought of Hori’s box.

He thought of the papyrus under the tongue.

He thought of how the lamps had flickered when he spoke the line.

Aaru’s Gate was real.

And the living were managing it.

Nebet gave Khaem a choice.

She could sell him a replacement name—clean, believable, stamped.

Or she could help him hunt the original.

The first option would save the dead man.

The second would endanger the living.

Khaem looked at Nebet’s ink-stained fingers and understood she had asked this question before.

Not to customers.

To herself.

He answered, 'I want the original.'

Nebet’s eyes narrowed, not in suspicion, but in recognition.

She slid a small scrap of papyrus across the table.

On it was a location: the temple granary office.

And beneath it, a symbol Khaem had only seen once before.

The same looped gate-mark.

Nebet said, 'Go at dusk. Clerks change shifts. People stop watching for a moment. That moment is your only mercy.'

Khaem folded the scrap and tucked it into his sleeve.

He stood to leave.

Nebet stopped him with one sentence.

'If you find the name,' she said, 'do not say it aloud in the street.'

Khaem frowned.

Nebet explained, 'A stolen name is like stolen gold. The moment it shines, someone else will claim it.'

Khaem left the workshop and reentered the sunlight.

The city looked the same.

But now every sign felt like a label.

Every priest felt like an accountant.

Every scribe felt like a guard.

Under his tunic, the black bundle pressed his ribs.

Khaem understood Chapter 2’s lesson: the dead travel by language, but the living control language by economy.

If Aaru’s Gate was a gate, then someone owned the keys.

And Khaem had just stepped into the market where keys were traded.
content.id:
Khaem tidak berlari.

Ia berjalan.

Ia berjalan seperti Hori mengajarinya saat membawa sesuatu yang rapuh: stabil, biasa, tidak mudah diingat.

Di balik tuniknya, bungkusan kain hitam menekan rusuk seperti detak kedua. Ia merasakannya setiap menarik napas, dan ia benci betapa cepat tubuhnya mulai menganggap tekanan itu normal.

Kota di luar Rumah Pemurnian lebih berisik daripada ruang pembalseman, tetapi punya aturan yang sama: hal paling penting dikerjakan diam-diam.

Khaem melewati jalan-jalan tempat penjual berteriak soal ikan dan ara, tempat anak-anak berkejaran di antara keranjang, dan tempat pendeta berlinen bersih berjalan seolah tanah berutang permintaan maaf pada mereka.

Ia mengulang pada diri sendiri bahwa ia hanya melakukan perintah Hori: menemukan nama si mati sebelum matahari terbenam.

Namun semakin sering ia mengulang, semakin perintah itu terdengar seperti kutukan.

Nama sebelum matahari terbenam.

Seolah matahari adalah pejabat yang akan menutup buku kas.

Khaem mulai dari tempat si tanpa nama terakhir kali masuk dunia hidup: tepi sungai.

Surutnya Nil menelanjangi batu lama dan malu baru. Lelaki yang tak pernah terlihat dekat makam kini mengungkit pintu yang setengah terbuka. Perempuan penjual roti juga menjual gosip tentang apa yang dibuka sungai.

Khaem bertanya hati-hati, tanpa menyebut kata 'jenazah.'

Ia bertanya apakah ada yang melihat seorang pria dibawa di tandu ilalang.

Kebanyakan menggeleng.

Seorang nelayan meludah dan berkata, 'Kebanyakan. Kekeringan itu dewa yang membawa pisau.'

Khaem mengejar rumor, bukan kepastian. Rumor punya kaki lebih bagus.

Seorang tukang cuci menunjuk ke gang tempat juru tulis menerima bayaran untuk surat yang orang miskin tak bisa tulis.

Seorang bocah kurus di sana menawarkan informasi untuk tembaga.

Khaem ragu.

Uangnya sedikit.

Bocah itu tersenyum seakan mencium kelangkaan.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau kau mau nama, kau bayar. Kalau mau yang bersih, bayar lebih.'

Khaem bertanya di mana nama bisa dibeli.

Bocah itu menunjuk ke gang di belakang lumbung dan berkata, 'Bukan di bawah matahari.'

Perut Khaem menegang. Ia mengerti ia sedang diarahkan, dan diarahkan adalah cara lain untuk dipegang.

Namun ia tetap pergi.

Gang itu berbau debu gandum dan tinta tua. Ada pintu yang tampak seperti pintu gudang biasa, kecuali satu tanda kecil dekat engselnya: simbol melingkar yang Khaem kenal dari papirus.

Tanda gerbang.

Ia mengetuk.

Sebuah celah terbuka.

Satu mata menilainya.

Pintu terbuka cukup untuk Khaem menyelinap masuk.

Ruang di dalam bukan ruang. Itu bengkel.

Meja-meja berisi pena buluh, palet, batu segel, gulungan papirus ditumpuk seperti roti.

Lelaki dan perempuan bekerja dalam sunyi, menyalin daftar dengan tangan yang terlalu cepat untuk disebut suci.

Di dinding jauh ada papan berisi label tanah liat.

Nama.

Ada yang lengkap.

Ada yang tersmear.

Ada yang dicoret lalu ditulis ulang.

Mulut Khaem kering.

Seorang perempuan mendekat, lebih tua, ujung jarinya bernoda tinta dan ketenangannya tidak meminta izin.

Ia bertanya urusannya.

Khaem tidak menyebut masalah si mati secara langsung. Ia belajar bahwa kebenaran yang eksplisit mengundang pencurian.

Ia berkata ia perlu memulihkan nama yang 'terselip.'

Bibir perempuan itu naik sedikit.

'Nama tidak terselip sendiri,' katanya.

Khaem bertanya apakah ia juru tulis.

Ia menjawab, 'Juru tulis menulis sesuai perintah. Aku menulis sesuai kebutuhan.'

Khaem melihat lehernya: bekas luka kecil seperti pernah dicekik tali.

Ia mengerti tanpa bertanya.

Perempuan ini pernah dihukum kuil.

Sekarang ia hidup dari menjual apa yang kuil klaim kendalikan.

Perempuan itu menyebut namanya Nebet.

Lalu ia menambahkan, 'Dan aku menjaganya dekat. Itu sebabnya aku masih punya.'

Nebet membawa Khaem ke meja tempat sebuah label tanah liat terbelah dua.

'Ini milik seorang pria yang keluarganya membayar,' katanya.

'Labelnya tetap retak. Karena tinta bukan perlindungan. Tinta negosiasi.'

Khaem bertanya maksudnya.

Nebet menunjuk rak batu segel.

'Batu segel itu janji,' katanya. 'Dan setiap janji bisa dijual.'

Nebet bertanya Khaem melayani otoritas mana.

Khaem berbohong dengan cara sekecil mungkin.

Ia berkata, 'Seorang pembalsem.'

Nebet menelitinya. Lalu ia mengangguk, seolah itu cukup masuk akal.

Nebet bertanya apakah Khaem membawa labelnya.

Khaem teringat tanda tanah liat di pergelangan kaki si mati.

Ia tidak membawanya.

Malu naik. Seharusnya ia mengambilnya.

Nebet melihat keraguan itu dan menghela napas.

'Kau datang tanpa benda yang retak,' katanya. 'Itu berarti kau datang membawa takut orang hidup sebagai gantinya.'

Khaem bertanya bagaimana mencari nama tanpa label.

Nebet menjawab, 'Cari siapa yang untung.'

Ia menjelaskan perdagangan itu seperti mengulang cuaca.

Ada nama dicuri untuk menghapus utang.

Ada nama dicuri untuk mencegah warisan.

Ada nama dicuri karena pendeta ingin jiwa gagal penimbangan lalu lahir ulang jadi tenaga.

Marah menyala di dada Khaem.

Ia diajar bahwa penimbangan hati itu suci.

Nebet menyebutnya administrasi.

Khaem menuntut bukti.

Nebet tidak berdebat.

Ia mengeluarkan selembar papirus tipis berisi daftar pembayaran—tembaga, perak, gandum—dengan catatan pendek.

Bukan nama.

Melainkan deskripsi.

'Janda. Buruh dermaga. Anak juru tulis. Rival mandor.'

Khaem mengerti intinya.

Di pasar ini, orang sudah direduksi sebelum mati.

Nama bukan identitas.

Nama adalah tuas.

Nebet bertanya lagi apa yang Khaem inginkan.

Khaem menjawab, 'Aku ingin nama yang diambil dari seorang pria yang dikirim ke pembalsem tanpa keluarga.'

Ketenangan Nebet akhirnya bergeser.

Sedikit.

Ia berkata, 'Itu bukan pencurian biasa.'

Khaem bertanya kenapa.

Nebet menjawab, 'Karena menghapus seseorang sepenuhnya itu mahal. Butuh kerja sama: sungai, pendeta, juru tulis, tetangga yang menoleh.'

Dunia terasa miring bagi Khaem.

Ia membayangkan pencuri tunggal.

Nebet menggambarkan sistem.

Nebet mencondongkan badan dan merendahkan suara.

'Kalau kau terus bertanya,' katanya, 'kau akan tahu Gerbang Aaru punya pintu belakang. Dan pintu belakang itu dijaga orang.'

Tenggorokan Khaem menegang.

Ia teringat kotak Hori.

Ia teringat papirus di bawah lidah.

Ia teringat lampu yang berkelip saat ia mengucap baris.

Gerbang Aaru nyata.

Dan orang hidup sedang mengelolanya.

Nebet memberi Khaem pilihan.

Ia bisa menjual nama pengganti—bersih, masuk akal, bercap.

Atau ia bisa membantu memburu yang asli.

Pilihan pertama menyelamatkan si mati.

Pilihan kedua membahayakan yang hidup.

Khaem menatap jari Nebet yang bernoda tinta dan mengerti Nebet pernah menanyakan pertanyaan itu.

Bukan pada pelanggan.

Pada dirinya.

Khaem berkata, 'Aku mau yang asli.'

Mata Nebet menyempit, bukan curiga, melainkan mengenali.

Nebet menyelipkan secarik papirus ke atas meja.

Di sana tertulis lokasi: kantor lumbung kuil.

Dan di bawahnya, simbol yang Khaem baru pernah lihat sekali.

Tanda gerbang yang sama.

Nebet berkata, 'Pergi saat senja. Pergantian shift. Orang lengah sebentar. Sebentar itu satu-satunya belas kasihmu.'

Khaem melipat kertas itu dan menyelipkannya ke lengan baju.

Ia berdiri hendak pergi.

Nebet menghentikannya dengan satu kalimat.

'Kalau kau menemukan namanya,' katanya, 'jangan ucapkan di jalan.'

Khaem mengernyit.

Nebet menjelaskan, 'Nama curian itu seperti emas curian. Begitu ia berkilau, orang lain akan mengklaim.'

Khaem meninggalkan bengkel dan kembali ke matahari.

Kota tampak sama.

Namun kini setiap papan terasa seperti label.

Setiap pendeta terasa seperti akuntan.

Setiap juru tulis terasa seperti penjaga.

Di balik tuniknya, bungkusan hitam menekan rusuk.

Khaem menangkap pelajaran Chapter 2: orang mati berjalan dengan bahasa, tetapi orang hidup mengendalikan bahasa lewat ekonomi.

Jika Gerbang Aaru adalah gerbang, maka ada yang memiliki kuncinya.

Dan Khaem baru saja melangkah ke pasar tempat kunci diperdagangkan.

content.en:
Nebet offers Khaem two paths: buy a replacement name or hunt the original.

Khaem surprises himself by choosing the replacement.

Not because he stops caring about the dead man.

Because he is seventeen, and fear is practical.

He asks for a name that will pass.

Nebet does not smile. She simply opens a drawer of blank tags and pulls out one that has already been aged—edges nicked, clay rubbed with oil, a history manufactured.

She writes a name with a steady hand.

Then she asks for the price.

Khaem offers copper.

Nebet shakes her head. 'Copper buys ink. It doesn’t buy silence.'

She names a different payment: a favor.

If temple clerks come asking questions, Khaem must answer in a way that points away from her workshop.

Khaem agrees too quickly.

Nebet stamps the tag with a seal that looks official enough to survive casual inspection.

Khaem leaves with the tag hidden in his sleeve.

He returns to the House of Purification and does what he told himself he would never do.

He attaches the new name to the nameless body.

The room does not protest.

The lamps do not flicker.

It works.

That is the horror.

Hori looks at the tag and knows instantly it is not original.

He says nothing, but his silence is heavier than anger.

Later, as Khaem recites the funerary lines, he feels a subtle wrongness—like wearing someone else’s clothes at a funeral.

The spells open a path.

But Khaem wonders what exactly is walking through it.

Chapter 2 ends with Khaem realizing that saving one soul by counterfeit may condemn many others by proving the system can be cheated—and that the Duat does not punish lies.

It punishes consequences.
content.id:
Nebet menawarkan dua jalan: beli nama pengganti atau memburu yang asli.

Khaem mengejutkan dirinya sendiri dengan memilih nama pengganti.

Bukan karena ia berhenti peduli pada si mati.

Karena ia tujuh belas, dan takut itu praktis.

Ia meminta nama yang pasti lolos.

Nebet tidak tersenyum. Ia hanya membuka laci berisi label kosong dan menarik satu yang sudah dibuat tampak tua—pinggirnya sedikit tergerus, tanah liatnya digosok minyak, riwayatnya dipalsukan.

Ia menulis sebuah nama dengan tangan stabil.

Lalu ia menyebut harganya.

Khaem menawarkan tembaga.

Nebet menggeleng. 'Tembaga membeli tinta. Tidak membeli diam.'

Ia meminta bayaran lain: sebuah jasa.

Jika juru tulis kuil datang bertanya, Khaem harus menjawab dengan cara yang mengalihkan jejak dari bengkelnya.

Khaem setuju terlalu cepat.

Nebet mengecap label itu dengan segel yang cukup resmi untuk lolos inspeksi kasual.

Khaem pergi dengan label disembunyikan di lengan bajunya.

Ia kembali ke Rumah Pemurnian dan melakukan hal yang ia bilang tak akan ia lakukan.

Ia menggantung nama baru pada tubuh tanpa nama.

Ruangan tidak memprotes.

Lampu tidak berkelip.

Itu berhasil.

Itulah horornya.

Hori melihat label itu dan langsung tahu itu bukan asli.

Ia tidak berkata apa-apa, tetapi diamnya lebih berat daripada marah.

Belakangan, saat Khaem melafalkan baris pemakaman, ia merasakan ada yang salah halus—seperti memakai baju orang lain di pemakaman.

Mantra membuka jalan.

Namun Khaem bertanya apa sebenarnya yang berjalan melewatinya.

Chapter 2 berakhir ketika Khaem sadar menyelamatkan satu jiwa dengan palsu bisa mengutuk banyak jiwa lain dengan membuktikan sistem bisa dicurangi—dan Duat tidak menghukum bohong.

Duat menghukum akibat.

content.en:
Nebet listens to Khaem’s request with careful stillness.

She does not interrupt when he says the dead man arrived without family.

She does not blink when he says the name was taken.

Then she asks him to repeat one detail: where the body was found.

Khaem hesitates, but answers.

Nebet nods and offers help.

She gives him the dusk instruction, the shift-change window, the location of the temple granary office.

Khaem thinks he has found an ally.

He leaves.

Nebet waits until his footsteps fade.

Then she turns to the back wall and knocks twice.

A hidden panel opens.

Behind it is a narrow passage smelling of incense, not ink.

A temple acolyte steps out, face tight with obedience.

Nebet hands him a note.

On it is not Khaem’s name.

It is a description.

Young. Embalmer’s assistant. Carrying something under his tunic.

The acolyte asks, 'Do we take him tonight?'

Nebet pauses.

Her jaw works as if chewing something bitter.

She says, 'Not at the granary office. There are too many eyes there. Take him on the way.

And be careful. He is not a thief.

He is worse.

He is honest.'

Chapter 2 ends with Khaem walking toward dusk believing he is hunting a stolen name, unaware he is being funneled into a corridor where the temple’s true trade happens.

And Nebet, alone in her workshop, staring at her ink-stained fingers, unable to tell whether she has just saved herself—or sold another person into erasure.
content.id:
Nebet mendengar permintaan Khaem dengan tenang yang terlalu hati-hati.

Ia tidak memotong saat Khaem berkata jenazah datang tanpa keluarga.

Ia tidak berkedip saat Khaem berkata namanya diambil.

Lalu ia meminta Khaem mengulang satu detail: tubuh itu ditemukan di mana.

Khaem ragu, tetapi menjawab.

Nebet mengangguk dan menawarkan bantuan.

Ia memberi instruksi senja, celah pergantian shift, lokasi kantor lumbung kuil.

Khaem mengira ia menemukan sekutu.

Ia pergi.

Nebet menunggu sampai langkah Khaem hilang.

Lalu ia menoleh ke dinding belakang dan mengetuk dua kali.

Sebuah panel tersembunyi terbuka.

Di baliknya ada lorong sempit yang berbau dupa, bukan tinta.

Seorang akolit kuil keluar, wajahnya tegang oleh patuh.

Nebet menyerahkan catatan.

Isinya bukan nama Khaem.

Isinya deskripsi.

Muda. Asisten pembalsem. Membawa sesuatu di balik tunik.

Akolit bertanya, 'Kita ambil dia malam ini?'

Nebet diam.

Rahangnya bergerak seolah mengunyah sesuatu yang pahit.

Ia berkata, 'Bukan di kantor lumbung. Terlalu banyak mata di sana. Ambil dia di jalan.

Dan hati-hati. Dia bukan pencuri.

Dia lebih buruk.

Dia jujur.'

Chapter 2 berakhir saat Khaem berjalan menuju senja dengan keyakinan ia sedang memburu nama curian, tanpa sadar ia sedang diarahkan ke koridor tempat perdagangan asli kuil berlangsung.

Dan Nebet, sendirian di bengkelnya, menatap jari-jari bernoda tinta, tak tahu apakah ia baru saja menyelamatkan dirinya—atau menjual orang lain ke penghapusan.

content.en:
Khaem notices the looped gate-mark scratched near the workshop door hinge.

He recognizes it from the papyrus under the dead man’s tongue.

At first he assumes it is a sign: a symbol for those who know.

But when he leaves the alley, he feels a pressure at the base of his throat.

A subtle tug.

As if a cord has been tied to his shadow.

He reaches under his tunic and touches the black cloth bundle.

The pressure sharpens.

Khaem stops near a water jar and catches his reflection.

For a blink, a faint looped mark appears on the skin of his neck—like ink beneath flesh.

Then it vanishes.

Khaem’s stomach drops.

The gate-mark is not only an identifier.

It is a tracker.

Someone has designed a symbol that sticks to people who carry forbidden words.

A way to make contraband visible to the right eyes.

Khaem returns to Nebet and demands to know what she placed on him.

Nebet looks genuinely startled.

She swears she did nothing.

Then she turns pale, not with guilt but with realization.

She says, 'It’s in the ink.'

The market does not only sell names.

It sells surveillance.

Khaem understands that every gate has two uses: passage and capture.

He runs—only this time, he runs.

Because walking is for people who are not being followed by a symbol.

Chapter 2 ends with Khaem realizing he cannot simply avoid the temple granary office.

If the mark is on him, the city itself is the office.

Every eye can become an inspector.

And the Duat’s first pursuit has begun in the living world.
content.id:
Khaem memperhatikan tanda gerbang melingkar yang digores dekat engsel pintu bengkel.

Ia mengenalinya dari papirus di bawah lidah jenazah.

Awalnya ia mengira itu tanda: simbol untuk orang dalam.

Namun saat ia keluar dari gang, ia merasakan tekanan di dasar tenggorokan.

Tarikan halus.

Seolah ada tali yang diikat pada bayangannya.

Ia meraba bungkusan kain hitam di balik tunik.

Tekanan itu menajam.

Khaem berhenti di dekat kendi air dan menangkap pantulannya.

Sesaat, tanda melingkar samar muncul di kulit lehernya—seperti tinta di bawah daging.

Lalu lenyap.

Perut Khaem jatuh.

Tanda gerbang itu bukan hanya penanda.

Itu pelacak.

Seseorang merancang simbol yang menempel pada orang yang membawa kata-kata terlarang.

Cara membuat barang selundupan terlihat bagi mata yang tepat.

Khaem kembali ke Nebet dan menuntut tahu apa yang ia taruh pada dirinya.

Nebet tampak sungguh terkejut.

Ia bersumpah tidak melakukan apa-apa.

Lalu wajahnya pucat, bukan oleh salah, melainkan oleh sadar.

Ia berkata, 'Itu ada di tintanya.'

Pasar itu bukan hanya menjual nama.

Pasar itu menjual pengawasan.

Khaem paham setiap gerbang punya dua fungsi: lewat dan menangkap.

Ia berlari—kali ini ia benar-benar berlari.

Karena berjalan itu untuk orang yang tidak sedang diikuti oleh simbol.

Chapter 2 berakhir saat Khaem sadar ia tidak bisa sekadar menghindari kantor lumbung kuil.

Kalau tanda itu sudah menempel, kota itu sendiri adalah kantor.

Setiap mata bisa jadi inspektur.

Dan pengejaran pertama Duat dimulai di dunia hidup.

content.en:
Nebet gives Khaem the location of the temple granary office.

Before he leaves, an old man at the far table clears his throat.

The room goes still, not with reverence, but with calculation.

The old man says, 'If you go to the granary office, you will be seen.'

Nebet’s eyes flick toward him, warning.

The old man ignores it.

He gestures for Khaem to come closer.

Khaem hesitates, then steps forward.

The old man’s hands are scarred. Not from labor.

From breaking clay.

He lifts a small pouch and pours out cracked tag fragments like teeth.

He says, 'They pay me to break names cleanly. So cleanly that people can pretend it was always blank.'

Khaem’s skin prickles.

He asks why the old man admits it.

The old man answers, 'Because you’re asking for an original. That means you’re not here to participate. You’re here to interfere.'

He tells Khaem something Nebet did not: names are not only erased.

They are reassigned.

A stolen name can be sold to someone else who needs a clean afterlife.

Khaem asks who buys them.

The old man smiles without humor.

He says, 'The ones who fear judgment.

And the ones who perform judgment.'

He offers Khaem a tool: a small chisel wrapped in cloth.

'If you find the registry slab,' he says, 'you can’t steal a name back by writing.

You must break the seal that holds it.

And if you break it wrong, you will break the soul attached.'

Khaem takes the chisel, hands trembling.

Nebet watches, expression unreadable.

As Khaem turns to go, the old man adds one final instruction: 'If anyone asks who gave you that, say the river did.'

Chapter 2 ends with Khaem stepping into the street carrying not only a forbidden bundle of spells, but a tool designed for erasure.

He understands the irony: to restore a name, he may have to learn the craft of breaking.
content.id:
Nebet memberi Khaem lokasi kantor lumbung kuil.

Sebelum Khaem pergi, seorang lelaki tua di meja jauh berdehem.

Ruangan mendadak sunyi, bukan karena hormat, melainkan karena hitung-hitungan.

Lelaki tua itu berkata, 'Kalau kau ke kantor lumbung, kau akan terlihat.'

Mata Nebet melirik ke arahnya, memperingatkan.

Lelaki tua itu mengabaikannya.

Ia memberi isyarat Khaem mendekat.

Khaem ragu, lalu melangkah.

Tangan lelaki tua itu penuh bekas luka. Bukan bekas kerja kasar.

Bekas memecahkan tanah liat.

Ia mengangkat kantong kecil dan menuangkan serpihan label retak seperti gigi.

Ia berkata, 'Mereka membayarku untuk memecahkan nama dengan rapi. Serapi itu sampai orang bisa pura-pura sejak awal memang kosong.'

Bulu kuduk Khaem berdiri.

Khaem bertanya kenapa ia mengaku.

Lelaki tua itu menjawab, 'Karena kau cari yang asli. Itu berarti kau tidak datang untuk ikut dagang. Kau datang untuk mengganggu.'

Ia memberitahu Khaem sesuatu yang tidak dikatakan Nebet: nama bukan hanya dihapus.

Nama dipindah-tangankan.

Nama curian bisa dijual ke orang lain yang butuh akhirat bersih.

Khaem bertanya siapa pembelinya.

Lelaki tua tersenyum tanpa lucu.

Ia berkata, 'Mereka yang takut diadili.

Dan mereka yang pura-pura mengadili.'

Ia menawarkan alat: pahat kecil dibungkus kain.

'Kalau kau menemukan batu registrinya,' katanya, 'kau tidak bisa mengambil nama kembali hanya dengan menulis.

Kau harus memecahkan segel yang menahannya.

Dan kalau kau memecah salah, kau akan memecahkan jiwa yang terikat.'

Khaem mengambil pahat itu, tangan gemetar.

Nebet menatap, ekspresinya tak terbaca.

Saat Khaem berbalik pergi, lelaki tua itu menambah satu instruksi terakhir: 'Kalau ada yang tanya siapa yang memberimu itu, bilang sungai.'

Chapter 2 berakhir ketika Khaem melangkah ke jalan membawa bukan hanya bungkusan mantra terlarang, tetapi juga alat yang dirancang untuk penghapusan.

Ia menangkap ironinya: untuk memulihkan nama, mungkin ia harus belajar keterampilan memecahkan.

preview.en: Khaem infiltrates the temple granary office at dusk, discovers how names are registered like grain, and learns the first rule of the Duat’s bureaucracy: what gets stamped becomes real.
preview.id: Khaem menyusup ke kantor lumbung kuil saat senja, menemukan bagaimana nama dicatat seperti gandum, dan belajar aturan pertama birokrasi Duat: yang sudah dicap akan dianggap nyata.

content.en:
Dusk did not arrive gently.

It fell.

The city cooled the way a person cools when they decide to become careful. Lamps appeared in doorways. Conversations lowered. Even the river, dark and narrow in its drought, seemed to hold its breath.

Khaem moved toward the temple granary office as Nebet instructed, keeping his pace unremarkable. He had learned the city’s first survival skill: panic is visible.

The granary complex sat behind the temple proper, a place less beautiful than sacred—brick walls, sealed doors, guards who looked bored because boredom was the mask of authority.

Khaem did not approach the main entrance.

He circled.

He found a side corridor where clerks exited in pairs, rubbing their eyes, talking about ink shortages and aching hands.

Shift change.

People stopped watching for a moment.

That moment was the only mercy.

Khaem waited behind stacked reed baskets until two clerks passed, then slipped into the corridor as if he belonged there.

He kept his head down.

He carried nothing in his hands.

In this place, hands that carried nothing looked like hands that carried permission.

Inside, the granary office smelled of dust and papyrus—dryness preserved into a profession. Lamps burned with oil that had been measured, not poured.

A long room opened before him.

Desks.

Shelves.

Clay seals.

And, at the far end, a stone slab ledger mounted on a stand like an altar.

Khaem’s throat tightened.

A ledger slab.

Not for grain.

For names.

He recognized it by the gate-mark carved at its corner: the same looped symbol he had seen on the workshop door and on the papyrus under the dead man’s tongue.

The temptation was immediate: run to it, read it, find the stolen name.

Khaem did not move.

He watched.

Two officials stood near the slab.

One read from a papyrus roll.

The other pressed a seal into wet clay, then stamped the clay onto a tag.

Khaem realized what he was seeing.

This was not writing.

This was assignment.

A name being attached to a person the way a label is attached to a jar.

A clerk approached the officials holding a basket of tags. The tags were blank—ready to receive.

Khaem’s stomach turned.

Somewhere in the House of Purification, a body lay with a tag that was not a name.

Here, blank tags waited to become someone.

Khaem moved along the wall, step by step, toward a shelf where unused papyrus strips lay in bundles.

He needed a pretext.

He needed to look like a clerk.

He picked up a bundle of strips and held it like a task.

No one stopped him.

That was the second rule of bureaucracy: if you look busy, you become invisible.

He reached a side desk where a young clerk sat alone, copying numbers.

The clerk looked up and frowned.

Khaem’s pulse spiked.

Before the clerk could speak, Khaem placed the papyrus bundle down and said the simplest lie he could manage.

'I’m delivering replacements. The scribe-master requested them.'

The clerk hesitated.

Then nodded, already too tired to fight a plausible sentence.

Khaem leaned closer as if sharing routine information.

He whispered, 'Where are the name assignments recorded?'

The clerk’s eyes sharpened.

Not with suspicion.

With fear.

'We don’t call them that,' the clerk murmured. 'We call them allocations.'

Allocations.

The word tasted like grain.

Khaem forced himself to breathe.

He asked where the allocations for the last week were stored.

The clerk glanced toward the officials near the slab, then pointed—barely—with his chin toward a locked cabinet.

Khaem thanked him and withdrew.

He understood he had seconds.

Shift change was not a window.

It was a blink.

He walked toward the cabinet.

A heavy lock hung from it.

Khaem’s hand slid into his sleeve and touched the small chisel wrapped in cloth—if he had taken it.

Or, failing that, he touched the edge of his knife, the one tool his apprenticeship allowed him to carry.

He did not break the lock.

Breaking would be loud.

He needed something quieter.

He looked around.

On a nearby desk lay a tray of fresh clay seals—soft, glistening, ready.

A seal was a promise.

And every promise had a weakness: it depended on being believed.

Khaem took one clay seal—just one—pressed it gently between his fingers, and smeared it.

He ruined it.

Then he carried the ruined seal toward the officials.

A clerk’s greatest weapon was inconvenience.

He approached with a bowed head and said, 'My seal is flawed. I need it reissued.'

The official nearest him scowled.

But scowling was easier than investigating.

The official gestured impatiently toward a side bench where defective seals were replaced.

Khaem bowed again and moved to the bench.

At the bench sat an older clerk with a box of seal stones.

The clerk did not look up.

He held out his hand for the defective clay.

Khaem placed it there.

The older clerk pressed a new seal into fresh clay with mechanical precision.

Then, without looking up, he said, 'Name?'

Khaem froze.

The question was not personal.

It was procedural.

But procedure can kill.

Khaem answered with the only safe truth.

'Apprentice to Hori,' he said.

The older clerk grunted and stamped the clay.

Khaem took the new seal and walked away, heart pounding.

He had gained something.

A valid stamp.

Now he needed a document to stamp.

He returned to the locked cabinet.

He watched the young clerk again.

The young clerk had a small key on a cord beneath his collar.

Khaem’s mind raced.

Steal the key?

Impossible.

Ask for the key?

Suicidal.

Then Khaem noticed something else.

The clerk’s hand kept rubbing his wrist.

A rash.

Ink rash.

Natron burned.

Khaem understood: the clerk was new to the work, and his skin was reacting.

A clerk with pain is a clerk with distraction.

Khaem moved to the desk and placed the sealed clay on it.

He said softly, 'Your wrist is infected. If you keep scratching, it will split.'

The clerk stiffened.

Khaem continued, voice low, practical. 'There’s natron in the purification house. It will dry it. You need to wash with clean water first.'

The clerk stared at him.

Khaem took the risk. 'Let me fetch water. You unlock the cabinet so I can take the allocation strips. I’ll return them and no one will know you moved.'

The clerk hesitated.

Then he did something human.

He believed relief.

He unlocked the cabinet.

Khaem’s fingers moved fast.

Inside were bundled strips labeled by day.

He grabbed the most recent.

He did not read names.

He read descriptions.

Widow.

Dockworker.

Foreman’s rival.

The same language Nebet had shown him.

Then he found one entry that made his blood turn cold:

Delivered without kin.

Tag: blank.

Allocation: reassigned.

Khaem could not see the name.

It was written in shorthand, coded, protected.

But beside it was a seal impression.

A temple seal.

And beneath that seal: a second mark.

The looped gate-mark.

This was not random theft.

This was sanctioned.

Khaem swallowed and slipped the strip into his tunic.

He returned the bundle to the cabinet and stepped back.

The clerk relocked it, hands shaking—not from suspicion, but from the dawning realization that he had just opened something that should not open.

Khaem left the office the way he entered: ordinary.

He carried no basket.

No tray.

Only one thin strip of papyrus pressed against his ribs beside the black-cloth book.

Outside, the city’s lamps burned.

Khaem walked fast but did not run.

He understood Chapter 3’s lesson: a stolen name is not simply missing.

It has been processed.

And once a name is processed—once it is stamped into existence—undoing it requires more than truth.

It requires power strong enough to invalidate a stamp.

As the sun’s last light died behind the temple walls, Khaem realized he had found his first solid evidence.

But evidence was also an invitation.

Somewhere, the people who staffed Aaru’s back door would soon learn that an embalmer’s apprentice had looked into the ledger.

And in a city where names were passports, curiosity was an illegal crossing.
content.id:
Senja tidak datang pelan.

Ia jatuh.

Kota mendingin seperti orang yang memutuskan menjadi hati-hati. Lampu muncul di ambang pintu. Percakapan merendah. Bahkan sungai, gelap dan sempit dalam kekeringan, seakan menahan napas.

Khaem bergerak menuju kantor lumbung kuil seperti instruksi Nebet, menjaga langkahnya tetap tak mencolok. Ia belajar keterampilan pertama bertahan hidup di kota: panik itu terlihat.

Kompleks lumbung berdiri di belakang kuil utama, tempat yang kurang indah daripada suci—tembok bata, pintu tersegel, penjaga yang tampak bosan karena bosan adalah topeng otoritas.

Khaem tidak mendekati pintu utama.

Ia memutar.

Ia menemukan koridor samping tempat para juru tulis keluar berpasangan, mengucek mata, bicara soal kekurangan tinta dan tangan pegal.

Pergantian shift.

Orang lengah sebentar.

Sebentar itu satu-satunya belas kasih.

Khaem menunggu di balik tumpukan keranjang ilalang sampai dua juru tulis lewat, lalu menyelinap ke koridor seolah ia memang milik tempat itu.

Ia menunduk.

Tangannya kosong.

Di tempat ini, tangan kosong tampak seperti tangan yang membawa izin.

Di dalam, kantor lumbung berbau debu dan papirus—kekeringan yang diawetkan jadi profesi. Lampu menyala dengan minyak yang ditakar, bukan dituang.

Ruang panjang terbuka.

Meja.

Rak.

Segel tanah liat.

Dan, di ujung, sebuah lempeng batu buku kas terpasang di dudukan seperti altar.

Tenggorokan Khaem menegang.

Buku kas lempeng.

Bukan untuk gandum.

Untuk nama.

Ia mengenalinya dari tanda gerbang di sudut: simbol melingkar yang sama seperti di pintu bengkel dan di papirus bawah lidah.

Godaan itu langsung: berlari, membaca, menemukan nama curian.

Khaem tidak bergerak.

Ia mengamati.

Dua pejabat berdiri dekat lempeng.

Satu membaca dari gulungan papirus.

Satu lagi menekan segel ke tanah liat basah, lalu mengecap tanah liat itu ke sebuah label.

Khaem sadar apa yang ia lihat.

Ini bukan menulis.

Ini penetapan.

Nama ditempelkan ke orang seperti label ditempelkan ke kendi.

Seorang juru tulis mendekat membawa keranjang label. Label-label itu kosong—siap menerima.

Perut Khaem berbalik.

Di Rumah Pemurnian, sebuah tubuh terbaring dengan label yang bukan nama.

Di sini, label kosong menunggu menjadi seseorang.

Khaem merapat ke dinding, selangkah demi selangkah, menuju rak berisi pita papirus kosong.

Ia butuh alasan.

Ia harus tampak seperti juru tulis.

Ia mengambil satu ikat pita dan memegangnya seperti tugas.

Tak ada yang menghentikan.

Itu aturan kedua birokrasi: kalau kau tampak sibuk, kau jadi tak terlihat.

Ia sampai di meja samping tempat seorang juru tulis muda duduk sendirian, menyalin angka.

Juru tulis itu menatap dan mengernyit.

Nadi Khaem melonjak.

Sebelum juru tulis itu bicara, Khaem meletakkan ikat papirus dan mengucap bohong paling sederhana.

'Aku mengantar pengganti. Ahli juru tulis minta.'

Juru tulis itu ragu.

Lalu mengangguk—terlalu lelah untuk melawan kalimat yang masuk akal.

Khaem mencondong seolah berbagi informasi rutin.

Ia berbisik, 'Di mana penetapan nama dicatat?'

Mata juru tulis menajam.

Bukan curiga.

Takut.

'Kami tidak menyebutnya begitu,' gumamnya. 'Kami menyebutnya alokasi.'

Alokasi.

Kata itu terasa seperti gandum.

Khaem memaksa bernapas.

Ia bertanya di mana alokasi minggu terakhir disimpan.

Juru tulis melirik pejabat dekat lempeng, lalu menunjuk tipis dengan dagu ke arah lemari terkunci.

Khaem mundur.

Ia paham ia punya detik.

Pergantian shift bukan jendela.

Itu kedipan.

Ia berjalan ke lemari.

Gembok berat menggantung.

Tangan Khaem menyentuh pahat kecil terbungkus kain di lengan bajunya—jika ia membawanya.

Atau, kalau tidak, ujung pisaunya—satu-satunya alat yang diizinkan magang.

Ia tidak memecahkan gembok.

Memecahkan berarti bunyi.

Ia butuh yang lebih sunyi.

Ia menoleh.

Di meja dekatnya ada nampan segel tanah liat baru—lunak, mengilap, siap.

Segel adalah janji.

Dan setiap janji punya kelemahan: ia bergantung pada dipercaya.

Khaem mengambil satu segel—hanya satu—menekannya pelan di antara jari, lalu mengoleskannya.

Ia merusaknya.

Lalu ia membawa segel rusak itu ke para pejabat.

Senjata terbaik juru tulis adalah merepotkan.

Ia membungkuk dan berkata, 'Segelku cacat. Perlu diterbitkan ulang.'

Pejabat terdekat mengernyit.

Tapi mengernyit lebih mudah daripada menyelidik.

Pejabat memberi isyarat kesal ke bangku samping tempat segel cacat diganti.

Khaem pindah.

Di sana duduk juru tulis tua dengan kotak batu segel.

Juru tulis itu tidak menoleh.

Ia mengulurkan tangan minta tanah liat.

Khaem memberikannya.

Juru tulis tua menekan segel baru ke tanah liat segar dengan presisi mekanis.

Lalu tanpa menoleh ia bertanya, 'Nama?'

Khaem membeku.

Pertanyaan itu bukan personal.

Itu prosedural.

Namun prosedur bisa membunuh.

Khaem menjawab dengan kebenaran paling aman.

'Magang Hori,' katanya.

Juru tulis tua mendengus dan mengecap.

Khaem mengambil segel baru dan pergi dengan jantung menghantam.

Ia mendapat sesuatu.

Cap sah.

Sekarang ia butuh dokumen untuk dicap.

Ia kembali ke lemari.

Ia mengamati juru tulis muda lagi.

Di bawah kerah juru tulis itu ada kunci kecil di tali.

Pikiran Khaem berputar.

Curi kunci?

Mustahil.

Minta kunci?

Bunuh diri.

Lalu Khaem melihat hal lain.

Juru tulis itu terus menggosok pergelangan.

Ruam.

Ruam tinta.

Natron perih.

Khaem mengerti: juru tulis itu baru, kulitnya bereaksi.

Juru tulis yang sakit adalah juru tulis yang terdistraksi.

Khaem mendekat dan meletakkan segel tanah liat sah di meja.

Ia berkata pelan, 'Pergelanganmu infeksi. Kalau kau garuk terus, kulitnya pecah.'

Juru tulis itu kaku.

Khaem lanjut, praktis: 'Ada natron di Rumah Pemurnian. Itu mengeringkan. Tapi harus cuci dulu dengan air bersih.'

Juru tulis itu menatap.

Khaem mengambil risiko. 'Biarkan aku ambilkan air. Kau buka lemari supaya aku bisa ambil pita alokasi. Aku kembalikan. Tak ada yang tahu kau memindah.'

Juru tulis ragu.

Lalu ia melakukan hal manusia.

Ia percaya pada lega.

Ia membuka lemari.

Jari Khaem bergerak cepat.

Di dalam ada ikatan pita berlabel hari.

Ia mengambil yang paling baru.

Ia tidak membaca nama.

Ia membaca deskripsi.

Janda.

Buruh dermaga.

Rival mandor.

Bahasa yang sama seperti yang Nebet tunjukkan.

Lalu ia menemukan satu entri yang membuat darahnya dingin:

Dikirim tanpa kerabat.

Label: kosong.

Alokasi: dipindah.

Khaem tidak bisa melihat nama.

Ditulis singkat, berkode, diproteksi.

Namun di sampingnya ada bekas segel.

Segel kuil.

Dan di bawah segel itu: tanda kedua.

Tanda gerbang melingkar.

Ini bukan pencurian acak.

Ini disahkan.

Khaem menelan ludah dan menyelipkan pita itu ke tuniknya.

Ia mengembalikan ikatan ke lemari dan mundur.

Juru tulis mengunci lagi, tangannya gemetar—bukan curiga, melainkan sadar ia baru saja membuka sesuatu yang tidak seharusnya terbuka.

Khaem keluar seperti ia masuk: biasa.

Ia tidak membawa keranjang.

Tidak membawa nampan.

Hanya satu pita papirus tipis menekan rusuknya di samping kitab kain hitam.

Di luar, lampu kota menyala.

Khaem berjalan cepat tapi tidak berlari.

Ia menangkap pelajaran Chapter 3: nama yang dicuri bukan sekadar hilang.

Nama itu sudah diproses.

Dan sekali nama diproses—sekali ia dicap jadi nyata—membatalkannya butuh lebih dari kebenaran.

Butuh kuasa yang cukup besar untuk membatalkan sebuah cap.

Saat cahaya terakhir mati di belakang tembok kuil, Khaem sadar ia baru saja menemukan bukti padat pertamanya.

Namun bukti juga undangan.

Di suatu tempat, orang-orang yang menjaga pintu belakang Aaru akan segera tahu bahwa seorang magang pembalsem telah mengintip buku kas.

Dan di kota tempat nama adalah paspor, rasa ingin tahu adalah penyebrangan ilegal.

content.en:
Khaem plays the role of a tired messenger and slips into the granary office during shift change.

He repeats the simplest lie: replacement strips, scribe-master requested.

The young clerk frowns, then looks closer.

His eyes flick to Khaem’s hands.

To the faint residue of natron at the nail beds.

To the way Khaem’s fingers rest—trained around blades and linen, not reed pens.

The clerk whispers, 'House of Purification.'

It isn’t a question.

Khaem’s pulse spikes.

The clerk rubs his ink rash, then says, very quietly, 'My sister died there.

Her tag came back with the wrong name.'

Khaem freezes.

The clerk’s voice hardens into a kind of exhausted hatred that has been waiting for a listener.

He says, 'If you came to steal, steal carefully. If you came to fix, fix fast. Because they don’t erase names once.

They erase the people who notice.'

Then he does something dangerous.

He slips the cabinet key from under his collar and places it on the desk—just for a heartbeat.

Khaem understands the offer: one chance.

He takes the key, opens the cabinet, and finds not only allocation strips but a second hidden bundle: error corrections.

Corrections are where guilt is stored.

Among them he finds a line that matches his nameless corpse: delivered without kin, tag blank, allocation reassigned.

But beside it is another note: 'Observer present. Remove.'

Khaem’s blood goes cold.

The system has already noticed him.

He returns the key.

The clerk takes it back and forces his face blank.

When guards pass, the clerk does not look at Khaem.

Khaem leaves with the strip and a new understanding: some clerks do not enforce the gate.

Some clerks are trapped inside it.

Chapter 3 ends with Khaem stepping into the dusk knowing he now has an ally who is also a liability—and that saving one stolen name may require exposing the wrong name already placed on another dead body.
content.id:
Khaem memainkan peran kurir lelah dan menyelinap ke kantor lumbung saat pergantian shift.

Ia mengulang bohong paling sederhana: pita pengganti, diminta ahli juru tulis.

Juru tulis muda mengernyit, lalu menatap lebih dekat.

Matanya jatuh ke tangan Khaem.

Ke sisa natron tipis di sela kuku.

Ke cara jari Khaem beristirahat—terlatih memegang pisau dan linen, bukan pena buluh.

Juru tulis itu berbisik, 'Rumah Pemurnian.'

Itu bukan pertanyaan.

Nadi Khaem melonjak.

Juru tulis itu menggosok ruam tintanya, lalu berkata sangat pelan, 'Adikku mati di sana.

Labelnya kembali dengan nama yang salah.'

Khaem membeku.

Suara juru tulis itu mengeras menjadi benci letih yang lama menunggu pendengar.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau kau datang mencuri, curilah dengan hati-hati. Kalau kau datang memperbaiki, perbaiki cepat. Karena mereka tidak menghapus nama sekali.

Mereka menghapus orang yang sadar.'

Lalu ia melakukan hal berbahaya.

Ia menyelipkan kunci lemari dari balik kerah dan meletakkannya di meja—hanya sekejap.

Khaem mengerti tawarannya: satu kesempatan.

Khaem mengambil kunci itu, membuka lemari, dan menemukan bukan hanya pita alokasi tetapi satu ikatan tersembunyi lagi: koreksi kesalahan.

Koreksi adalah tempat rasa bersalah disimpan.

Di antara koreksi itu, ia menemukan baris yang cocok dengan jenazah tanpa nama: dikirim tanpa kerabat, label kosong, alokasi dipindah.

Namun di sampingnya ada catatan lain: 'Pengamat hadir. Singkirkan.'

Darah Khaem dingin.

Sistem sudah memperhatikannya.

Ia mengembalikan kunci.

Juru tulis mengambilnya kembali dan memaksa wajahnya datar.

Saat penjaga lewat, juru tulis itu tidak menatap Khaem.

Khaem keluar membawa pita dan satu pemahaman baru: tidak semua juru tulis menegakkan gerbang.

Sebagian juru tulis terperangkap di dalamnya.

Chapter 3 berakhir ketika Khaem melangkah ke senja dengan pengetahuan ia punya sekutu yang sekaligus beban—dan bahwa menyelamatkan satu nama curian mungkin mengharuskan membongkar nama salah yang sudah ditempelkan pada jenazah lain.

content.en:
Khaem ruins a clay seal on purpose to create a problem small enough to be solved without questions.

The tactic works.

At the defect bench, the older clerk asks, 'Name?'

Khaem answers, 'Apprentice to Hori.'

The clerk grunts and presses a new seal.

But as Khaem turns away, the clerk’s hand slips—only slightly.

The fresh clay stamp lands not on a tag.

It lands on Khaem’s wrist.

The clay is cool, wet, and heavy.

Khaem freezes.

The older clerk finally looks up.

His eyes widen—not with guilt, but with calculation.

A stamp on skin is not an accident.

It is a claim.

The clerk whispers, 'Don’t wash it.'

Khaem tries to scrape the clay off, but the seal has already bitten into the world.

The impression remains faintly, like ink under flesh.

Suddenly Khaem understands why the office feels like a temple.

Stamps here are not symbols.

They are bindings.

He moves through the corridor and feels eyes shift toward him as if the mark emits a scent.

Two guards turn their heads in unison.

Not because he looks suspicious.

Because he looks registered.

Khaem realizes the danger: if he is stamped into the ledger’s logic, the system can now 'process' him.

A living person can be allocated too.

He leaves with the stolen strip, but the strip is no longer the only evidence.

His wrist is.

Chapter 3 ends with Khaem in an alley scrubbing at his skin until it bleeds, only to see the impression persist—and understanding the Duat’s bureaucracy has reached across the threshold.
