It has begun to treat him as a file.
content.id:
Khaem merusak satu segel tanah liat dengan sengaja untuk menciptakan masalah kecil yang bisa diselesaikan tanpa pertanyaan.

Taktiknya berhasil.

Di bangku cacat, juru tulis tua bertanya, 'Nama?'

Khaem menjawab, 'Magang Hori.'

Juru tulis itu mendengus dan mengecap segel baru.

Namun saat Khaem berbalik, tangan juru tulis itu terpeleset—hanya sedikit.

Cap tanah liat segar jatuh bukan ke label.

Cap itu jatuh ke pergelangan tangan Khaem.

Tanah liatnya dingin, basah, berat.

Khaem membeku.

Juru tulis tua akhirnya menatap.

Matanya membesar—bukan oleh salah, melainkan oleh hitung-hitungan.

Cap di kulit bukan kecelakaan.

Itu klaim.

Juru tulis itu berbisik, 'Jangan cuci.'

Khaem mencoba mengerik tanah liat, tetapi segel itu sudah menggigit dunia.

Bekasnya tinggal samar, seperti tinta di bawah daging.

Mendadak Khaem paham kenapa kantor itu terasa seperti kuil.

Cap di sini bukan simbol.

Cap adalah ikatan.

Ia berjalan melewati koridor dan merasakan mata-mata bergeser ke arahnya seolah tanda itu mengeluarkan bau.

Dua penjaga menoleh bersamaan.

Bukan karena Khaem terlihat mencurigakan.

Karena Khaem terlihat terdaftar.

Khaem menyadari bahayanya: kalau ia sudah dicap ke dalam logika buku kas, sistem kini bisa 'memproses' dirinya.

Orang hidup pun bisa dialokasikan.

Ia keluar membawa pita curian, tetapi pita itu bukan lagi satu-satunya bukti.

Pergelangannya juga bukti.

Chapter 3 berakhir saat Khaem di gang menggosok kulitnya sampai berdarah, hanya untuk melihat bekas cap tetap ada—dan mengerti birokrasi Duat sudah menjulur melewati ambang.

Birokrasi itu mulai memperlakukannya sebagai berkas.

content.en:
Khaem reaches the granary office and sees the registry slab like an altar.

He remembers the old man’s warning: to restore a name, sometimes you must break what holds it.

He tells himself he will only look.

Only copy.

But then he watches an allocation happen.

A blank tag receives a name.

A seal is pressed.

A person is reassigned as casually as grain moved between bins.

Something in Khaem snaps.

He drifts closer to the slab, fingers already in his sleeve.

The small chisel touches his palm.

He waits for the blink of the room—an official turning, a clerk coughing.

Then he strikes.

Not hard.

Precisely.

A hairline crack opens at the corner where the looped gate-mark is carved.

The sound is tiny.

But the air reacts as if a law has been broken.

Lamps flare.

Ink wells tremble.

Every clerk looks up at the same time, not because they heard the crack, but because they felt it.

A registry slab is not stone.

It is an anchor.

And Khaem has loosened it.

The officials rush to the slab.

One touches the crack and goes pale.

He shouts for guards.

Khaem does not run.

He knows running would confirm guilt.

Instead he does the only thing he can do inside a bureaucracy.

He speaks procedure.

He raises the stamped clay seal and says, loudly, 'Defect reported. Replacement required.'

The officials hesitate.

For one breath, the machine tries to treat sabotage as paperwork.

That breath is Khaem’s escape.

He slips away as guards push into the room.

Outside, his hands shake.

He has not retrieved the dead man’s name.

He has created a larger problem.

A crack in the system’s altar.

Chapter 3 ends with Khaem realizing that power does not only live in seals and names.

It lives in the objects that make seals and names feel permanent.

And once you crack permanence, the Duat notices.
content.id:
Khaem sampai di kantor lumbung dan melihat lempeng registri seperti altar.

Ia teringat peringatan lelaki tua: untuk memulihkan nama, kadang kau harus memecahkan yang menahannya.

Ia berkata pada diri sendiri ia hanya akan melihat.

Hanya menyalin.

Namun ia melihat penetapan terjadi.

Label kosong menerima nama.

Segel ditekan.

Seseorang dipindah-tangankan santai seperti gandum dipindah antar bak.

Ada sesuatu di dalam Khaem yang patah.

Ia mendekat ke lempeng, jari sudah masuk ke lengan.

Pahat kecil menyentuh telapak.

Ia menunggu ruangan berkedip—pejabat menoleh, juru tulis batuk.

Lalu ia memukul.

Tidak keras.

Presisi.

Retak rambut membuka di sudut tempat tanda gerbang melingkar dipahat.

Bunyinya kecil.

Namun udara bereaksi seolah hukum retak.

Lampu menyala tajam.

Tinta bergetar.

Semua juru tulis menatap bersamaan, bukan karena mendengar retak, melainkan karena merasakannya.

Lempeng registri bukan batu.

Lempeng itu jangkar.

Dan Khaem baru saja mengendurkannya.

Para pejabat menyerbu.

Salah satu menyentuh retak dan pucat.

Ia berteriak memanggil penjaga.

Khaem tidak berlari.

Ia tahu berlari mengonfirmasi salah.

Maka ia melakukan satu-satunya hal yang bisa dilakukan di dalam birokrasi.

Ia bicara prosedur.

Ia mengangkat segel tanah liat bercap dan berkata keras, 'Cacat dilaporkan. Perlu penggantian.'

Para pejabat ragu.

Sesaat mesin mencoba memperlakukan sabotase sebagai berkas.

Sesaat itu adalah pelarian Khaem.

Ia menyelinap keluar saat penjaga masuk.

Di luar, tangannya gemetar.

Ia belum mendapatkan nama si mati.

Ia menciptakan masalah lebih besar.

Retak di altar sistem.

Chapter 3 berakhir saat Khaem sadar kuasa tidak hanya hidup di segel dan nama.

Kuasa hidup di benda-benda yang membuat segel dan nama terasa permanen.

Dan sekali kau meretakkan yang permanen, Duat akan sadar.

content.en:
Khaem exits the granary office with the thin strip hidden under his tunic.

He keeps his face ordinary, his shoulders relaxed, his breathing quiet.

He turns one corner.

And sees Nebet.

She is not hiding.

She stands under a lamp as if lamps cannot accuse her.

Khaem’s first thought is betrayal.

His second is worse: protection.

Nebet steps closer and says, 'Show me what you took.'

Khaem refuses.

Nebet nods as if expecting it.

Then she does something that changes the air.

She speaks the looped gate-mark aloud.

Not the symbol.

The sound behind the symbol.

A name for the mark.

Khaem feels the bundle under his tunic tighten, like a knot being pulled.

Nebet says, 'That mark means your strip is already owned.

If you keep it, they will come.

If you give it to me, they will come anyway.

But at least you won’t be alone.'

Khaem realizes Nebet is risking herself by standing here.

Either she is brave.

Or she is already condemned.

Nebet tells him what she could not say in the workshop: the temple granary office is only the front ledger.

The back ledger is kept in a mortuary.

A place where tags are swapped before bodies ever reach embalmers.

Khaem’s stomach drops.

The theft of the nameless man began before death was even official.

Nebet offers Khaem a route out of the district through a water-seller’s corridor.

But she demands one thing: Khaem must promise he will not use the recovered name to buy purity.

He must use it to expose the trade.

Khaem looks at her ink-stained fingers and hears the cost inside the demand.

He nods.

They move together into the shadows.

Behind them, guards’ voices rise.

Chapter 3 ends with Khaem understanding that the only way to carry truth in a city like this is to share its weight—and that the gate-mark is not a mystery anymore.

It is a leash, and someone is tugging.
content.id:
Khaem keluar dari kantor lumbung dengan pita tipis tersembunyi di balik tunik.

Ia menjaga wajah biasa, bahu rileks, napas tenang.

Ia berbelok satu sudut.

Dan melihat Nebet.

Nebet tidak bersembunyi.

Ia berdiri di bawah lampu seolah lampu tidak bisa menuduhnya.

Pikiran pertama Khaem: pengkhianatan.

Pikiran kedua lebih buruk: perlindungan.

Nebet mendekat dan berkata, 'Tunjukkan apa yang kau ambil.'

Khaem menolak.

Nebet mengangguk seolah sudah menduga.

Lalu Nebet melakukan sesuatu yang mengubah udara.

Ia mengucapkan tanda gerbang melingkar itu keras-keras.

Bukan simbolnya.

Melainkan bunyi di balik simbol.

Nama untuk tanda itu.

Khaem merasakan bungkusan di balik tunik mengencang, seperti simpul ditarik.

Nebet berkata, 'Tanda itu artinya pitamu sudah dimiliki.

Kalau kau simpan, mereka akan datang.

Kalau kau berikan padaku, mereka tetap datang.

Tapi setidaknya kau tidak sendirian.'

Khaem sadar Nebet mempertaruhkan dirinya dengan berdiri di sini.

Entah Nebet berani.

Atau Nebet sudah dihukum sejak lama.

Nebet memberitahu hal yang tak bisa ia katakan di bengkel: kantor lumbung kuil hanya buku kas depan.

Buku kas belakang disimpan di rumah mayat.

Tempat label ditukar sebelum tubuh sampai ke pembalsem.

Perut Khaem jatuh.

Pencurian si tanpa nama dimulai sebelum kematian bahkan resmi.

Nebet menawarkan jalan keluar dari distrik lewat koridor penjual air.

Tapi ia menuntut satu hal: Khaem harus berjanji ia tidak akan memakai nama yang didapat untuk membeli kemurnian.

Ia harus memakainya untuk membongkar perdagangan.

Khaem menatap jari Nebet yang bernoda tinta dan mendengar biaya di balik tuntutan itu.

Ia mengangguk.

Mereka bergerak bersama ke bayangan.

Di belakang mereka suara penjaga meninggi.

Chapter 3 berakhir ketika Khaem paham satu-satunya cara membawa kebenaran di kota seperti ini adalah berbagi bebannya—dan tanda gerbang itu bukan misteri lagi.

Tanda itu tali kekang, dan seseorang sedang menariknya.

preview.en: Khaem and Nebet trace the gate-mark to a mortuary where tags are swapped before the dead are declared dead, and Khaem learns the Duat’s most brutal rule: identity can be reassigned while you are still breathing.
preview.id: Khaem dan Nebet menelusuri tanda gerbang ke rumah mayat tempat label ditukar sebelum kematian ‘diresmikan’, dan Khaem belajar aturan paling brutal Duat: identitas bisa dipindah bahkan saat kau masih bernapas.

content.en:
Nebet led Khaem through corridors the city pretended did not exist.

They passed a water-seller’s lane where jars lined up like silent witnesses. They crossed a narrow bridge of planks over a canal that smelled of algae and secrets. They moved with the discipline of people who had learned that speed is not safety.

Khaem kept one hand near his ribs, feeling the thin strip from the granary office and the black-cloth bundle beneath it. Two pieces of paper, two kinds of danger.

Nebet did not speak until they reached the district of the mortuaries.

Here, the city’s noise changed texture. Fewer shouts. More murmurs. Doors were painted with protective eyes, but the paint had chipped from being trusted too often.

Nebet stopped in front of a building that looked like any storage house except for the smell.

Natron.

Cedar.

And something metallic that made Khaem think of knives washed too many times.

A small sign above the door declared the place an official holding house for the unclaimed.

Khaem felt his throat tighten.

Unclaimed.

A word that sounded like property.

Nebet said, 'This is where names disappear before anyone is allowed to notice they were taken.'

They waited.

A cart arrived carrying two bodies wrapped in rush matting. A clerk walked beside it with a bored face and a fresh set of tags.

Khaem watched the tags.

They were clean.

Too clean.

No family wrote those names.

The cart rolled into the holding house.

Nebet moved.

She approached the clerk like a colleague, not like a thief. She spoke in the tone of a person delivering routine inconvenience.

'Your docket is missing the second seal,' she said.

The clerk frowned automatically and looked down, already defensive against procedure.

Nebet pointed at the papers.

'If the temple inspects this week, they will blame you,' she added.

The clerk cursed softly.

He stepped aside, looking for a stamp.

Nebet’s hand slid into his tag basket and lifted one tag with the smoothness of habit.

Khaem’s breath stopped.

Nebet pressed the tag into Khaem’s palm without looking.

It was still damp.

New clay.

New identity.

She whispered, 'Hold it. Feel the weight. This is what they sell.'

Khaem felt sick.

A human life reduced to a rectangle of clay.

They slipped inside while the clerk hunted for a stamp.

The holding house was dim and cool. Rows of bodies lay on shelves, wrapped and labeled like inventory.

A man in priestly linen walked between the shelves with a reed pen, checking tags against a ledger.

Not a funeral priest.

An administrative priest.

He was not praying.

He was auditing.

Nebet guided Khaem behind a hanging curtain into a back room.

Here the smell of ink was stronger than the smell of death.

A table held clay tags in stacks and a shallow bowl of water where old ink could be softened and scraped away.

Khaem stared.

This was the workshop.

Not Nebet’s.

The temple’s.

A young worker sat at the table, scraping a name off a tag with a small blade, face blank with concentration.

The worker did not look like a criminal.

He looked like an apprentice.

Like Khaem.

Khaem’s anger rose.

He stepped forward.

Nebet caught his sleeve, warning.

But the worker looked up.

His eyes widened.

He recognized Khaem’s natron-stained hands.

He whispered, 'Purification house.'

The same word.

Khaem realized there was a corridor of apprentices across the city—young hands trained to obey, moved between institutions like tools.

The worker’s gaze flicked to Nebet, then to the door.

He said, barely audible, 'You shouldn’t be here.'

Nebet replied, 'Neither should you.'

The worker swallowed.

Then he said the sentence that turned Khaem’s blood to ice.

'We assign the tag before they die,' he whispered.

Khaem stared.

The worker explained quickly, as if confessing might buy him a smaller punishment.

Some people were marked as unclaimed while still alive, so that when they died, no one would be allowed to claim them.

Their families would be told the body had been lost.

Or that disease required immediate burial.

Or that the river took what it took.

Khaem felt the Duat’s shadow fall across the living world.

This was not theft after death.

This was premeditated erasure.

Nebet asked a single question.

'Who orders it?'

The worker’s eyes flicked upward, not toward the ceiling, but toward the temple.

He said, 'The granary office stamps it. The mortuary makes it true.'

Khaem remembered his strip: Delivered without kin. Tag: blank. Allocation: reassigned.

The strip was the middle of the story.

This room was the beginning.

Khaem asked about the man with ink-stained lips.

The worker hesitated.

Then he nodded.

He said that body had arrived with instructions: no name, no inquiry, immediate transfer.

A back-door passage.

Aaru’s Gate, staffed.

Khaem demanded the original tag.

The worker shook his head. 'It’s not here anymore.'

Nebet asked where.

The worker pointed to a small metal box on a high shelf, sealed with a temple cord.

'Rejected tags,' he whispered. 'Names that were removed but not destroyed. They keep them like they keep grain—so they can reassign later.'

Khaem’s hands trembled.

He understood the logic.

If a name was a passport, then removed passports became a reserve.

Nebet moved first.

She reached for the shelf.

The worker flinched and whispered, 'If you open it, the cord will record you.'

Nebet answered, 'Good.'

Khaem heard the defiance and realized Nebet was not only helping him.

She was choosing a side.

Nebet cut the cord.

The sound was tiny.

But the air in the room changed.

Khaem felt, briefly, like the lamps had in Chapter 1: as if the world made room for an old language.

Only now the language was not spell.

It was crime.

Nebet opened the metal box.

Inside were tags wrapped in linen, each one with a name that had been scraped away from someone else.

Khaem’s vision blurred.

He was looking at stolen afterlives.

Nebet lifted one tag and showed Khaem the back.

A mark had been pressed there.

The looped gate-mark.

Khaem realized the mark was not only a symbol of passage.

It was a brand of ownership.

Footsteps sounded in the outer room.

The auditing priest had finished his rounds.

Nebet shoved the tags back into the box and pressed it into Khaem’s hands.

Khaem recoiled.

Nebet hissed, 'If you want the original, you carry the evidence. Evidence is heavier when you try to hand it off.'

Khaem clutched the box against his ribs.

It felt like holding a beating heart made of metal.

They slipped out through the curtain.

The auditing priest turned.

His eyes landed on Khaem.

Not with recognition.

With appraisal.

Like a clerk seeing a new file.

He said, softly, 'You are not registered here.'

Khaem forced his face empty.

Nebet spoke first, calm as ink.

'He is delivering replacements to the purification house,' she said.

The priest’s gaze moved to the damp tag in Khaem’s hand.

Khaem realized he was still holding it.

The priest smiled slightly.

'A tag for the living,' he murmured. 'How efficient.'

Khaem’s blood chilled.

The priest reached out.

Nebet stepped between them.

'You can have him later,' she said.

The priest’s smile did not change, but his eyes narrowed.

'Later is a promise,' he said. 'Promises are stamped.'

And he lifted his seal.

Chapter 4 ends with Nebet grabbing Khaem’s wrist and pulling him into the aisle of wrapped bodies, using death as cover.

Behind them, the priest’s seal hovered like a quiet threat.

Khaem understood the chapter’s lesson with sick clarity: in this city, you can be processed before you are dead.

And once you are processed, the world will treat your disappearance as orderly.

Somewhere behind the mortuary walls, Aaru’s Gate waited.

But the gate they faced now was smaller, closer, and staffed by men who called theft 'allocation.'
content.id:
Nebet menuntun Khaem melewati koridor yang pura-pura tidak ada.

Mereka melewati gang penjual air, kendi berjajar seperti saksi yang diam. Mereka menyeberangi jembatan papan sempit di atas kanal yang berbau ganggang dan rahasia. Mereka bergerak dengan disiplin orang yang sudah belajar: cepat bukan berarti aman.

Khaem menahan satu tangan dekat rusuk, merasakan pita tipis dari kantor lumbung dan bungkusan kain hitam di bawahnya. Dua kertas, dua jenis bahaya.

Nebet baru bicara ketika mereka sampai di distrik rumah mayat.

Di sini, kebisingan kota berubah tekstur. Lebih sedikit teriak. Lebih banyak gumam. Pintu-pintu dicat mata pelindung, tetapi catnya mengelupas karena terlalu sering dipercaya.

Nebet berhenti di depan bangunan yang tampak seperti gudang biasa kecuali baunya.

Natron.

Resin aras.

Dan sesuatu yang metalik, membuat Khaem teringat pisau yang sudah terlalu sering dicuci.

Papan kecil di atas pintu menyatakan tempat itu rumah penahanan resmi bagi jenazah tak bertuan.

Tenggorokan Khaem menegang.

Tak bertuan.

Kata yang terdengar seperti milik.

Nebet berkata, 'Di sinilah nama menghilang sebelum siapa pun boleh sadar namanya diambil.'

Mereka menunggu.

Sebuah gerobak datang membawa dua tubuh dibungkus tikar. Seorang juru tulis berjalan di sampingnya dengan wajah bosan dan set baru label.

Khaem menatap labelnya.

Label itu bersih.

Terlalu bersih.

Tidak ada keluarga menulis nama itu.

Gerobak masuk.

Nebet bergerak.

Ia mendekati juru tulis seperti rekan kerja, bukan seperti pencuri. Nadanya seperti orang yang mengantar kerepotan rutin.

'Docket-mu kurang segel kedua,' katanya.

Juru tulis mengernyit otomatis dan menatap kertasnya.

Nebet menunjuk.

'Kalau kuil inspeksi minggu ini, mereka akan salahkan kau,' tambahnya.

Juru tulis mengumpat pelan.

Ia menyingkir mencari cap.

Tangan Nebet menyelinap ke keranjang label dan mengambil satu label dengan kelancaran kebiasaan.

Napas Khaem berhenti.

Nebet menekan label itu ke telapak Khaem tanpa menoleh.

Tanah liatnya masih lembap.

Identitas baru.

Nebet berbisik, 'Pegang. Rasakan beratnya. Inilah yang mereka jual.'

Khaem mual.

Hidup manusia direduksi jadi persegi tanah liat.

Mereka menyelinap masuk saat juru tulis mencari cap.

Di dalam, rumah penahanan itu redup dan dingin. Barisan tubuh terbungkus di rak, diberi label seperti stok.

Seorang pria berjubah linen pendeta berjalan di antara rak dengan pena buluh, memeriksa label terhadap buku kas.

Bukan pendeta pemakaman.

Pendeta administrasi.

Ia tidak berdoa.

Ia mengaudit.

Nebet mengantar Khaem ke balik tirai gantung menuju ruang belakang.

Di sini bau tinta lebih kuat daripada bau mati.

Meja berisi tumpukan label dan baskom air dangkal tempat tinta lama dilunakkan lalu dikikis.

Khaem menatap.

Ini bengkel.

Bukan bengkel Nebet.

Bengkel kuil.

Seorang pekerja muda duduk, mengikis nama dari label dengan pisau kecil, wajahnya kosong oleh fokus.

Ia tidak tampak kriminal.

Ia tampak seperti magang.

Seperti Khaem.

Marah Khaem naik.

Ia melangkah.

Nebet menarik lengan bajunya, memperingatkan.

Namun pekerja itu menatap.

Matanya membesar.

Ia mengenali tangan Khaem yang bernoda natron.

Ia berbisik, 'Rumah Pemurnian.'

Kata yang sama.

Khaem sadar ada koridor para magang di seluruh kota—tangan muda dilatih patuh, dipindah antar lembaga seperti alat.

Pandangan pekerja itu melirik Nebet, lalu pintu.

Ia berbisik, 'Kalian tak seharusnya di sini.'

Nebet menjawab, 'Kau juga.'

Pekerja itu menelan ludah.

Lalu ia mengucapkan kalimat yang membuat darah Khaem beku.

'Kami menetapkan label sebelum mereka mati,' bisiknya.

Khaem menatap.

Pekerja itu menjelaskan cepat, seolah mengaku bisa membeli hukuman lebih kecil.

Sebagian orang ditandai 'tak bertuan' saat masih hidup, agar ketika mati, tak ada yang diizinkan mengakui.

Keluarganya akan diberitahu tubuhnya hilang.

Atau bahwa penyakit menuntut kubur cepat.

Atau bahwa sungai mengambil apa yang ia ambil.

Bayang Duat jatuh ke dunia hidup.

Ini bukan pencurian setelah mati.

Ini penghapusan yang direncanakan.

Nebet bertanya satu hal.

'Siapa yang memerintah?'

Mata pekerja itu melirik ke atas, bukan langit-langit, melainkan kuil.

'Kantor lumbung mengecapnya. Rumah mayat membuatnya nyata,' katanya.

Khaem teringat pitanya: Dikirim tanpa kerabat. Label: kosong. Alokasi: dipindah.

Pita itu tengah cerita.

Ruang ini awalnya.

Khaem bertanya tentang pria berbibir tinta.

Pekerja ragu.

Lalu mengangguk.

Ia berkata tubuh itu datang dengan instruksi: tanpa nama, tanpa tanya, transfer segera.

Jalur pintu belakang.

Gerbang Aaru, dijaga.

Khaem menuntut label asli.

Pekerja menggeleng. 'Sudah bukan di sini.'

Nebet bertanya di mana.

Pekerja menunjuk kotak metal kecil di rak tinggi, disegel tali kuil.

'Label ditolak,' bisiknya. 'Nama yang dicabut tapi tidak dimusnahkan. Mereka simpan seperti gandum—untuk dipindah lagi nanti.'

Tangan Khaem gemetar.

Ia mengerti logikanya.

Kalau nama itu paspor, maka paspor yang dicabut jadi cadangan.

Nebet bergerak dulu.

Ia meraih rak.

Pekerja itu meringis dan berbisik, 'Kalau kau buka, talinya akan mencatatmu.'

Nebet menjawab, 'Bagus.'

Khaem mendengar penentangan itu dan sadar Nebet tidak hanya menolongnya.

Nebet sedang memilih sisi.

Nebet memotong tali.

Bunyinya kecil.

Tapi udara ruangan berubah.

Khaem merasakan seperti lampu di Chapter 1: dunia memberi ruang bagi bahasa lama.

Tapi kini bahasanya bukan mantra.

Bahasanya kejahatan.

Nebet membuka kotak metal.

Di dalam ada label-label dibungkus linen, masing-masing menyimpan nama yang pernah dikikis dari orang lain.

Pandangan Khaem kabur.

Ia menatap akhirat yang dicuri.

Nebet mengangkat satu label dan menunjukkan bagian belakang.

Ada tanda tercap.

Tanda gerbang melingkar.

Khaem sadar tanda itu bukan hanya simbol lewat.

Itu merek kepemilikan.

Langkah kaki terdengar di ruang luar.

Pendeta audit selesai.

Nebet menyuruh Khaem memegang kotak itu.

Khaem tersentak.

Nebet mendesis, 'Kalau kau mau yang asli, kau bawa buktinya. Bukti lebih berat kalau kau coba menyerahkannya.'

Khaem memeluk kotak itu di dada.

Rasanya seperti memegang jantung metal.

Mereka menyelinap keluar dari tirai.

Pendeta audit menoleh.

Matanya jatuh pada Khaem.

Bukan mengenali.

Menilai.

Seperti juru tulis melihat berkas baru.

Ia berkata pelan, 'Kau tidak terdaftar di sini.'

Khaem memaksa wajah kosong.

Nebet bicara dulu, tenang seperti tinta.

'Dia mengantar pengganti ke Rumah Pemurnian,' katanya.

Tatapan pendeta turun ke label lembap di tangan Khaem.

Khaem sadar ia masih memegangnya.

Pendeta tersenyum tipis.

'Label untuk yang hidup,' gumamnya. 'Efisien.'

Darah Khaem dingin.

Pendeta mengulurkan tangan.

Nebet melangkah menutup.

'Kau bisa ambil dia nanti,' kata Nebet.

Senyum pendeta tidak berubah, tapi matanya menyempit.

'Nanti itu janji,' katanya. 'Janji harus dicap.'

Dan ia mengangkat segelnya.

Chapter 4 berakhir saat Nebet menarik pergelangan Khaem masuk ke lorong rak jenazah, memakai kematian sebagai kamuflase.

Di belakang mereka segel pendeta menggantung seperti ancaman sunyi.

Khaem mengerti pelajaran chapter ini dengan mual: di kota ini, kau bisa diproses sebelum mati.

Dan sekali diproses, hilangmu akan disebut tertib.

Di balik dinding rumah mayat, Gerbang Aaru menunggu.

Namun gerbang yang mereka hadapi sekarang lebih kecil, lebih dekat, dan dijaga orang yang menyebut pencurian 'alokasi'.

content.en:
In the aisle of wrapped bodies, Nebet pulls Khaem down until linen brushes his cheek and the air tastes of cedar and silence.

The auditing priest’s seal hovers above them.

Khaem expects the priest to shout.

Instead the priest does what administrators do.

He stamps.

The seal touches Khaem’s damp living tag first—then, with a smoothness that looks like accident and feels like design, it touches Khaem’s forearm.

For a heartbeat the clay is cold.

Then the mark remains, faint as a bruise.

The priest murmurs, 'Now you are registered.'

Nebet’s face tightens.

Khaem realizes the stamp is not a threat of future capture.

It is capture.

Once stamped, he is no longer a person in the room.

He is a file.

Guards will not need to recognize his face.

They will only need to follow the mark.

Nebet makes a decision.

She smears ash and natron onto the stamped skin, grinding until Khaem’s arm burns.

The mark resists.

It is not ink.

It is procedure.

Nebet hisses, 'The only thing stronger than a seal is a contradiction.'

She grabs the damp living tag and stamps it again—over the priest’s impression—using the wrong orientation, reversing the sign.

The air stutters.

For one breath the mark becomes unreadable.

Nebet drags Khaem through a side door into a wash room and plunges his arm into brine.

Khaem shakes, half from pain, half from the realization that he has been put into the same category as the dead.

Outside, the priest’s voice stays calm as he orders a search.

Calm searches are the most dangerous.

Chapter 4 ends with Khaem staring at his reddened arm and understanding that if he cannot undo his registration, he will be 'allocated' like the unclaimed.

Not after death.

Before it.
content.id:
Di lorong rak jenazah, Nebet menarik Khaem turun sampai linen menyentuh pipinya dan udara terasa seperti resin aras dan sunyi.

Segel pendeta audit menggantung di atas mereka.

Khaem mengira pendeta akan berteriak.

Ternyata pendeta melakukan yang biasa dilakukan administrator.

Ia mengecap.

Segel itu menyentuh label lembap di tangan Khaem dulu—lalu, dengan kelancaran yang tampak seperti kecelakaan namun terasa seperti desain, segel itu menyentuh lengan Khaem.

Sesaat tanah liatnya dingin.

Lalu bekasnya tinggal, samar seperti memar.

Pendeta bergumam, 'Sekarang kau terdaftar.'

Wajah Nebet menegang.

Khaem sadar cap itu bukan ancaman penangkapan di masa depan.

Cap itu penangkapan.

Begitu tercap, ia bukan lagi orang di ruangan.

Ia berkas.

Penjaga tidak perlu mengenali wajah.

Mereka hanya perlu mengikuti tanda.

Nebet mengambil keputusan.

Ia menggosok abu dan natron ke kulit bercap, menggerus sampai lengan Khaem panas perih.

Tanda itu melawan.

Itu bukan tinta.

Itu prosedur.

Nebet mendesis, 'Satu-satunya yang lebih kuat dari segel adalah kontradiksi.'

Ia meraih label lembap itu dan mengecap ulang—menimpa bekas pendeta—dengan arah yang salah, membalik tandanya.

Udara tersendat.

Sesaat tanda itu jadi tak terbaca.

Nebet menyeret Khaem ke pintu samping menuju ruang cuci dan mencelupkan lengannya ke air asin.

Khaem gemetar, separuh karena sakit, separuh karena sadar ia baru saja dimasukkan ke kategori yang sama dengan orang mati.

Di luar, suara pendeta tetap tenang saat memerintahkan pencarian.

Pencarian yang tenang adalah yang paling berbahaya.

Chapter 4 berakhir saat Khaem menatap lengannya yang merah dan mengerti: kalau ia tidak bisa membatalkan pendaftarannya, ia akan 'dialokasikan' seperti jenazah tak bertuan.

Bukan setelah mati.

Sebelum itu.

content.en:
Nebet cuts the temple cord and opens the metal box of rejected tags.

Khaem expects erased names.

Loose passports.

Inventory.

Instead he finds one tag that has not been fully scraped.

A name remains.

Not cleanly written.

Pressed deep, as if the clay itself refused to let go.

Khaem reads it silently and feels a pressure behind his eyes.

A memory that isn’t his.

A flash of river light, a woman’s laughter, the sting of a whip against someone else’s back.

Khaem staggers.

Nebet grabs him.

Khaem realizes the worst possibility: a name is not just a label.

It is a tether.

If the person attached to this name still lives, then holding the tag is like holding their throat.

The young mortuary worker sees Khaem’s reaction and goes pale.

He whispers, 'That one is dangerous.'

Nebet demands why.

The worker answers, 'Because he hasn’t died yet.

He’s scheduled.'

Khaem’s stomach turns.

Scheduled.

Death as an appointment.

The auditing priest’s footsteps approach.

Nebet wants to take the whole box.

Khaem refuses.

He takes only the half-scraped tag.

He says, 'If they can schedule death, then finding one stolen name isn’t enough.

We have to stop the next theft before it becomes official.'

They flee into the aisles of bodies.

The priest’s voice remains calm.

Behind calmness is confidence.

Chapter 4 ends with Khaem clutching the half-scraped tag and feeling a stranger’s life tug on his ribs—understanding that the Duat’s paperwork does not begin at death.

It begins at selection.
content.id:
Nebet memotong tali kuil dan membuka kotak metal berisi label-label 'ditolak'.

Khaem mengira ia akan menemukan nama-nama yang sudah terhapus.

Paspor longgar.

Stok.

Ternyata ada satu label yang belum sepenuhnya dikikis.

Satu nama masih ada.

Bukan ditulis rapi.

Melainkan tertekan dalam, seolah tanah liatnya sendiri menolak melepas.

Khaem membacanya dalam hati dan merasakan tekanan di belakang mata.

Ingatan yang bukan miliknya.

Kilat cahaya sungai, tawa seorang perempuan, perih cambuk di punggung orang lain.

Khaem limbung.

Nebet menahannya.

Khaem sadar kemungkinan terburuk: nama bukan sekadar label.

Nama adalah tali.

Kalau orang yang terikat pada nama itu masih hidup, maka memegang labelnya seperti memegang tenggorokannya.

Pekerja muda itu melihat reaksi Khaem dan pucat.

Ia berbisik, 'Yang itu berbahaya.'

Nebet menuntut kenapa.

Pekerja itu menjawab, 'Karena dia belum mati.

Dia dijadwalkan.'

Perut Khaem berbalik.

Dijadwalkan.

Mati sebagai janji temu.

Langkah pendeta audit mendekat.

Nebet ingin membawa seluruh kotak.

Khaem menolak.

Ia hanya mengambil label setengah terkikis.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau mereka bisa menjadwalkan kematian, menemukan satu nama curian saja tidak cukup.

Kita harus menghentikan pencurian berikutnya sebelum menjadi resmi.'

Mereka kabur ke lorong rak jenazah.

Suara pendeta tetap tenang.

Di balik tenang ada percaya diri.

Chapter 4 berakhir saat Khaem memeluk label setengah terkikis dan merasakan hidup orang asing menarik di rusuknya—mengerti bahwa berkas Duat tidak dimulai saat mati.

Berkas itu dimulai saat pemilihan.

content.en:
In the back room, the young worker scrapes a name from a tag with the blank focus of survival.

When Nebet confronts him, he whispers the line that chills Khaem: tags are assigned before death.

Khaem expects the worker to keep cooperating, because cooperation is how apprentices survive.

But then the worker looks at Khaem’s natron-stained hands and something in him shifts.

He sees himself.

He sees a future where his hands keep erasing until they forget what they erased.

He begins to shake.

Nebet asks, 'Who ordered it?'

The worker opens his mouth.

And screams.

Not in fear.

In refusal.

The scream tears through the holding house like a dropped jar.

Bodies on shelves do not move.

But clerks do.

Footsteps rush.

The auditing priest turns, startled for the first time.

The worker shouts words that are not spell and not prayer.

They are a list.

Names.

Names of clerks.

Names of priests.

Names of families who paid.

He points at the water bowl and screams, 'This is where you wash people into blanks!'

Nebet grabs Khaem’s wrist and tries to pull him away.

Khaem hesitates.

Because the worker’s refusal is the first honest thing Khaem has heard in a room designed to make honesty impossible.

Guards arrive.

They seize the worker.

The priest’s calm returns and he says, 'He is contaminated by grief.'

Khaem realizes the trick: labeling truth as sickness.

As the worker is dragged away, he locks eyes with Khaem and shouts one last thing: the location of the back ledger.

Then his voice is cut off.

Nebet and Khaem flee with the metal box half-open.

Chapter 4 ends with Khaem hearing the worker’s scream echo in his skull and understanding that exposure has a cost.

Not everyone can pay it.

But someone already did.
content.id:
Di ruang belakang, pekerja muda itu mengikis nama dari label dengan fokus kosong demi selamat.

Saat Nebet menekannya, ia membisikkan kalimat yang membekukan Khaem: label ditetapkan sebelum mati.

Khaem mengira pekerja itu akan terus bekerja sama, karena kerja sama adalah cara magang bertahan.

Namun pekerja itu menatap tangan Khaem yang bernoda natron dan ada sesuatu bergeser.

Ia melihat dirinya.

Ia melihat masa depan di mana tangannya terus menghapus sampai lupa apa yang ia hapus.

Tubuhnya mulai gemetar.

Nebet bertanya, 'Siapa yang memerintah?'

Pekerja itu membuka mulut.

Dan menjerit.

Bukan karena takut.

Karena menolak.

Jerit itu merobek rumah penahanan seperti kendi yang jatuh.

Jenazah di rak tidak bergerak.

Tapi juru tulis bergerak.

Langkah kaki berlari.

Pendeta audit menoleh, terkejut untuk pertama kali.

Pekerja itu berteriak kata-kata yang bukan mantra dan bukan doa.

Itu daftar.

Nama.

Nama juru tulis.

Nama pendeta.

Nama keluarga yang membayar.

Ia menunjuk baskom air dan berteriak, 'Di sini kalian mencuci orang jadi kosong!'

Nebet menarik pergelangan Khaem dan mencoba menyeret pergi.

Khaem ragu.

Karena penolakan pekerja itu adalah hal jujur pertama yang Khaem dengar di ruangan yang dirancang membuat jujur mustahil.

Penjaga datang.

Mereka menangkap pekerja itu.

Ketenangan pendeta kembali dan ia berkata, 'Dia terkontaminasi duka.'

Khaem menangkap triknya: menamai kebenaran sebagai sakit.

Saat pekerja itu diseret, ia mengunci mata ke Khaem dan berteriak satu hal terakhir: lokasi buku kas belakang.

Lalu suaranya diputus.

Nebet dan Khaem kabur dengan kotak metal yang nyaris terbuka.

Chapter 4 berakhir saat Khaem mendengar jerit itu bergema di kepalanya dan paham bahwa pembongkaran selalu berbiaya.

Tidak semua orang bisa membayarnya.

Tapi seseorang sudah membayar lebih dulu.

content.en:
The auditing priest blocks the aisle, seal raised, expression calm.

Nebet understands the rules of this world: calm means leverage.

She steps forward and lowers her head, performing submission.

Khaem is shocked.

Nebet says, 'You want him. Fine.

Give me ten breaths.'

The priest’s smile flickers.

He expects pleading.

He does not expect negotiation.

He asks why.

Nebet answers, 'Because you can stamp him anytime.

But if you stamp me, you risk questions.

People remember my face.'

The priest considers.

Ten breaths is nothing.

He nods.

Nebet grips Khaem’s wrist and whispers, 'Run when I let go.'

Then she lets go.

And does not run.

Khaem’s feet move on instinct, but his throat locks with horror as he realizes what Nebet is doing.

She has offered herself as the easier file.

Outside the mortuary, Khaem forces himself to keep moving until the city swallows the sounds.

He clutches the metal box and feels its weight turn into guilt.

He wants to go back.

But going back would make Nebet’s bargain meaningless.

Chapter 4 ends with Khaem alone under street lamps, understanding that every gate has a price.

He just watched someone pay it for him.

And he does not yet know whether Nebet’s sacrifice was loyalty—or another form of control.
content.id:
Pendeta audit menutup lorong, segel terangkat, wajah tenang.

Nebet paham aturan dunia ini: tenang berarti punya tuas.

Ia maju dan menundukkan kepala, memerankan patuh.

Khaem terpukul.

Nebet berkata, 'Kau mau dia. Baik.

Beri aku sepuluh napas.'

Senyum pendeta bergetar.

Ia mengira akan ada permohonan.

Ia tidak mengira akan ada tawar.

Pendeta bertanya kenapa.

Nebet menjawab, 'Karena kau bisa mengecap dia kapan pun.

Tapi kalau kau mengecap aku, kau berisiko ditanya.

Orang ingat wajahku.'

Pendeta mempertimbangkan.

Sepuluh napas itu kecil.

Ia mengangguk.

Nebet mencengkeram pergelangan Khaem dan berbisik, 'Lari saat aku lepas.'

Lalu Nebet melepas.

Dan Nebet tidak lari.

Kaki Khaem bergerak refleks, tetapi tenggorokannya mengunci oleh ngeri saat ia sadar apa yang Nebet lakukan.

Nebet menawarkan dirinya sebagai berkas yang lebih mudah.

Di luar rumah mayat, Khaem memaksa diri terus bergerak sampai kota menelan bunyinya.

Ia memeluk kotak metal dan merasakan beratnya berubah jadi rasa bersalah.

Ia ingin kembali.

Tapi kembali akan membuat tawar Nebet sia-sia.

Chapter 4 berakhir saat Khaem sendirian di bawah lampu jalan, mengerti bahwa setiap gerbang punya harga.

Ia baru saja melihat seseorang membayarnya untuk dirinya.

Dan ia belum tahu apakah pengorbanan Nebet adalah setia—atau bentuk kendali lain.

preview.en: With the stolen box of rejected tags, Khaem learns the back ledger is not paper at all but a living registry—and that it can correct the living the same way it corrects the dead.
preview.id: Dengan kotak label ‘ditolak’ yang dicuri, Khaem menyadari buku kas belakang bukan sekadar kertas melainkan registri yang hidup—dan ia bisa ‘mengoreksi’ orang hidup seperti ia mengoreksi orang mati.

content.en:
Khaem did not know where to go.

He only knew where he could not.

He could not return to the House of Purification with a metal box full of stolen afterlives pressed against his ribs.

He could not walk openly with Nebet’s name on his tongue, because names were bait.

So he chose the city’s oldest hiding place.

He went to the dead.

At the edge of the necropolis, beyond the neat tombs of the wealthy, a slope of broken stones led to older chambers—places the river had exposed and the city pretended not to see.

Khaem slipped into one such chamber through a crack in the masonry, scraping his shoulder, breathing dust that tasted like old linen.

Inside, the air was cooler and still.

He set the metal box on a flat stone and stared at it.

The temple cord Nebet had cut lay coiled beside it like a dead snake.

Khaem’s hands shook.

He had not meant to become a thief.

He had meant to become accurate.

Accuracy, he was learning, was another kind of crime.

He opened the box again.

Tags wrapped in linen.

Some scraped clean.

Some half-scraped.

Some with the faint ghost of a name still pressed into the clay.

All branded on the back with the looped gate-mark.

Ownership.

Khaem lifted one tag and held it near the lantern.

The clay felt warm, impossibly warm for something that had been stored among corpses.

He turned it over.

The gate-mark shimmered, not with light but with attention.

Khaem felt a subtle tug behind his eyes.

A sensation like being counted.

He understood then: the mark was not merely a stamp.

It was a link.

A link to the back ledger.

Nebet had called it a leash.

Now Khaem felt the leash tighten.

A sound came from the chamber wall.

Not footsteps.

Scratching.

As if stone was being written on from the other side.

Khaem froze.

The necropolis was supposed to be silent.

Then the scratch resolved into a pattern—short, repeated strokes.

Like a reed pen’s rhythm.

The wall before him—blank stone—began to show faint lines.

Glyphs emerging as if ink seeped up from inside the rock.

Khaem’s mouth went dry.

A ledger.

Not carried.

Embedded.

The back ledger was not stored in a mortuary.

It was distributed into the city’s bones.

Into tomb walls.

Into thresholds.

Into anything that could hold writing long after humans forgot.

The glyphs formed columns.

Allocations.

Corrections.

Reassignments.

Khaem saw the same cold language he had glimpsed in the granary strip.

But here it was older.

Stronger.

As if the city itself had been turned into an archive.

Khaem stepped closer.

The wall’s writing paused.

Then, under the last line, a new entry appeared.

Not in a clerk’s hand.

In a hand that felt like hunger.

SUBJECT: KHAEM.

STATUS: UNREGISTERED.

ACTION: PENDING.

Khaem’s breath stopped.

He had not spoken his name.

He had not offered it.

Yet the ledger had found him.

Because he had touched the branded tags.

Because he had carried the leash.

Khaem staggered back.

His lantern shook.

The light trembled over the walls and for a moment he saw other chambers through the doorway—other stones, other scratches.

The city was full of ledgers.

He was surrounded by files.

Khaem’s first instinct was childish.

Erase it.

He grabbed a cloth, dipped it in his water skin, and scrubbed at the wall.

The glyphs did not smear.

They did not fade.

They only deepened, as if friction fed them.

Khaem felt a flash of anger at himself.

Of course.

You cannot wash bureaucracy.

You can only break it.

He remembered the small chisel—the tool for breaking tags cleanly.

He raised it toward the wall.

Then he stopped.

Breaking stone would not only break writing.

It would break the tomb.

Break the dead.

It would be sacrilege.

Which meant it would also be effective.

A new sound entered the chamber.

Human breath.

Khaem spun.

In the crack of the doorway, a figure stood—thin, hooded, holding no lamp.

Khaem’s heart slammed.

Temple?

Guard?

Then the figure spoke.

It was Nebet.

Her voice was low, steady, alive.

Khaem’s knees nearly gave.

He had been half-convinced she had not escaped.

Nebet stepped in and looked at the wall.

She did not gasp.

She did not pray.

She said, simply, 'So you found the teeth.'

Khaem demanded how she found him.

Nebet pointed at the gate-mark on the tags.

'We are both carrying it now,' she said. 'It pulls toward its own record.'

Khaem asked if the wall was the back ledger.

Nebet nodded.

She said the mortuary kept tags.

The granary kept paperwork.

But the true registry—the one that decided what counted—was older than temples.

It had been carved into the necropolis when the first kings decided that eternity should be managed.

Khaem stared at the entry with his name.

STATUS: UNREGISTERED.

ACTION: PENDING.

He asked what action meant.

Nebet answered without softness.

'Correction,' she said.

Khaem felt cold.

He had seen correction strips.

He had imagined them as clerical repair.

Nebet said correction was removal.

A person could be corrected the way a tag could.

Name scraped.

History softened.

Neighbors trained to forget.

Khaem’s throat tightened.

He realized the city had a mechanism for erasure that did not require murder.

It required consensus.

And consensus could be stamped.

Nebet told him they had one advantage.

The ledger was powerful.

But it was not creative.

It could only rewrite what it could categorize.

So Nebet proposed a plan that sounded like madness.

They would feed it something it could not file.

A contradiction large enough to jam its grammar.

Khaem asked how.

Nebet gestured to the black-cloth bundle.

'Your spells are not prayers,' she said. 'They are addresses.

If you can address a gate, you can address a registry.'

Khaem understood with a sick clarity.

The Book of the Dead was not meant only to guide souls.

It was meant to speak to the administrators of the Duat.

To argue with them.

To force them to record what they did not want.

Khaem opened the bundle with shaking hands.

Papyrus whispered as it unfolded.

He did not read aloud.

Not yet.

Nebet placed one rejected tag on the stone beside the box.

She said, 'Find the original name among these.

But before you return it, you must learn how to say it so the ledger cannot steal it again.'

Khaem looked at her.

'How?' he asked.

Nebet answered, 'By saying it in the Duat’s own language.

Not as a label.

As an accusation.'

Outside the chamber, wind moved over the necropolis like the turning of pages.

Khaem watched the wall.

The ledger’s last line pulsed faintly, as if waiting.

ACTION: PENDING.

Khaem realized Chapter 5’s turn: the enemy was no longer only priests and clerks.

It was the record itself.

A living archive with teeth.

And it had just taken his name into its mouth.
content.id:
Khaem tidak tahu harus pergi ke mana.

Ia hanya tahu ia tidak boleh ke mana.

Ia tidak boleh kembali ke Rumah Pemurnian dengan kotak metal berisi akhirat curian menekan rusuk.

Ia tidak boleh berjalan terbuka dengan nama Nebet di lidah, karena nama adalah umpan.

Maka ia memilih tempat sembunyi tertua kota.

Ia pergi ke orang mati.

Di tepi nekropolis, melewati makam rapi orang kaya, lereng batu pecah mengarah ke ruang-ruang lebih tua—tempat yang dibuka sungai dan pura-pura tidak dilihat kota.

Khaem menyelinap ke salah satu ruang lewat retak batu, bahunya tergores, menghirup debu yang rasanya seperti linen lama.

Di dalam, udara lebih dingin dan diam.

Ia meletakkan kotak metal di atas batu datar dan menatapnya.

Tali kuil yang dipotong Nebet tergulung di sampingnya seperti ular mati.

Tangan Khaem gemetar.

Ia tidak berniat menjadi pencuri.

Ia berniat menjadi akurat.

Ia belajar: akurasi adalah jenis kejahatan lain.

Ia membuka kotak lagi.

Label dibungkus linen.

Ada yang terkikis bersih.

Ada yang setengah terkikis.

Ada yang masih menyisakan hantu nama tertekan di tanah liat.

Semua diberi merek di belakang: tanda gerbang melingkar.

Kepemilikan.

Khaem mengangkat satu label dekat lampu.

Tanah liatnya hangat—tidak masuk akal untuk benda yang disimpan dekat jenazah.

Ia membalik.

Tanda gerbang itu berkilau bukan oleh cahaya, melainkan oleh perhatian.

Khaem merasakan tarikan halus di belakang mata.

Seperti sedang dihitung.

Ia paham: tanda itu bukan sekadar cap.

Itu taut.

Taut ke buku kas belakang.

Nebet menyebutnya tali kekang.

Sekarang Khaem merasakan talinya mengencang.

Ada bunyi dari dinding ruang.

Bukan langkah.

Goresan.

Seolah batu sedang ditulisi dari sisi lain.

Khaem membeku.

Nekropolis seharusnya sunyi.

Lalu goresan itu membentuk pola—pendek, berulang.

Seperti ritme pena buluh.

Dinding di depannya—batu kosong—mulai menampakkan garis samar.

Hieroglif muncul seolah tinta merembes dari dalam batu.

Mulut Khaem kering.

Buku kas.

Bukan dibawa.

Tertanam.

Buku kas belakang bukan disimpan di rumah mayat.

Ia tersebar ke tulang kota.

Ke dinding makam.

Ke ambang.

Ke apa pun yang bisa menyimpan tulisan lama setelah manusia lupa.

Hieroglif membentuk kolom.

Alokasi.

Koreksi.

Pemindahan.

Khaem melihat bahasa dingin yang sama seperti di pita lumbung.

Namun di sini lebih tua.

Lebih kuat.

Seolah kota sendiri dijadikan arsip.

Khaem mendekat.

Tulisan berhenti.

Lalu, di bawah baris terakhir, entri baru muncul.

Bukan tulisan juru tulis.

Tapi tulisan yang terasa seperti lapar.

SUBJEK: KHAEM.

STATUS: BELUM TERDAFTAR.

TINDAKAN: MENUNGGU.

Napas Khaem berhenti.

Ia tidak mengucapkan namanya.

Ia tidak menyerahkannya.

Namun buku kas menemukan.

Karena ia menyentuh label bermerek.

Karena ia membawa tali kekang.

Khaem mundur.

Lampunya berguncang.

Cahaya bergoyang di dinding dan sesaat ia melihat ruang lain di luar—batu lain, goresan lain.

Kota ini penuh buku kas.

Ia dikelilingi berkas.

Naluri pertama Khaem kekanak-kanakan.

Hapus.

Ia meraih kain, membasahinya, dan menggosok dinding.

Hieroglif tidak luntur.

Tidak pudar.

Justru menajam, seolah gesekan memberi makan.

Marah menyambar.

Tentu.

Kau tidak bisa mencuci birokrasi.

Kau hanya bisa mematahkannya.

Ia teringat pahat kecil—alat memecahkan label dengan rapi.

Ia mengangkatnya ke dinding.

Lalu berhenti.

Memecah batu bukan hanya memecah tulisan.

Itu memecah makam.

Memecah orang mati.

Itu penghujatan.

Dan artinya efektif.

Bunyi lain masuk.

Napas manusia.

Khaem berputar.

Di celah pintu, sosok kurus berkerudung berdiri tanpa lampu.

Jantung Khaem menghantam.

Kuil?

Penjaga?

Lalu sosok itu bicara.

Itu Nebet.

Suara Nebet rendah, stabil, hidup.

Lutut Khaem nyaris lepas.

Ia setengah yakin Nebet tidak lolos.

Nebet masuk dan menatap dinding.

Ia tidak terkejut.

Ia tidak berdoa.

Ia hanya berkata, 'Jadi kau menemukan taringnya.'

Khaem menuntut bagaimana Nebet menemukan.

Nebet menunjuk tanda gerbang pada label.

'Kita berdua membawanya sekarang,' katanya. 'Ia menarik ke catatannya sendiri.'

Khaem bertanya apakah dinding itu buku kas belakang.

Nebet mengangguk.

Rumah mayat menyimpan label.

Lumbung menyimpan berkas.

Tapi registri sejati—yang memutuskan apa yang dihitung—lebih tua dari kuil.

Ia dipahat ke nekropolis saat raja pertama memutuskan keabadian harus dikelola.

Khaem menatap entri namanya.

STATUS: BELUM TERDAFTAR.

TINDAKAN: MENUNGGU.

Ia bertanya maksud 'tindakan'.

Nebet menjawab tanpa lembut.

'Koreksi,' katanya.

Dingin menyusup.

Khaem sudah melihat pita koreksi.

Ia mengira itu perbaikan.

Nebet menyebut koreksi itu penghapusan.

Orang bisa dikoreksi seperti label.

Nama dikikis.

Riwayat dilunakkan.

Tetangga dilatih lupa.

Tenggorokan Khaem menegang.

Ia sadar kota punya mesin penghapusan tanpa perlu membunuh.

Yang dibutuhkan adalah konsensus.

Dan konsensus bisa dicap.

Nebet berkata mereka punya satu keunggulan.

Buku kas itu kuat.

Tapi ia tidak kreatif.

Ia hanya bisa menulis ulang yang bisa ia kategorikan.

Maka Nebet mengusulkan rencana yang terdengar gila.

Mereka akan memberi makan sesuatu yang tak bisa ia berkas.

Kontradiksi cukup besar untuk menyumbat tata bahasanya.

Khaem bertanya bagaimana.

Nebet menunjuk bungkusan kain hitam.

'Mantramu bukan doa,' katanya. 'Itu alamat.

Kalau kau bisa mengalamatkan gerbang, kau bisa mengalamatkan registri.'

Khaem paham dengan mual.

Kitab Orang Mati bukan cuma untuk menuntun jiwa.

Kitab itu untuk bicara pada administrator Duat.

Untuk berdebat.

Untuk memaksa mereka mencatat yang tak mereka mau.

Khaem membuka bungkusan dengan tangan gemetar.

Papirus berbisik saat terbentang.

Ia tidak membaca keras.

Belum.

Nebet menaruh satu label ditolak di atas batu.

Ia berkata, 'Cari nama asli di antara ini.

Tapi sebelum kau mengembalikannya, kau harus belajar menyebutnya agar buku kas tak bisa mencurinya lagi.'

Khaem menatap.

'Bagaimana?' tanyanya.

Nebet menjawab, 'Dengan menyebutnya dalam bahasa Duat.

Bukan sebagai label.

Sebagai tuduhan.'

Di luar ruang, angin bergerak di nekropolis seperti halaman yang dibalik.

Khaem menatap dinding.

Baris terakhir berdenyut samar, seolah menunggu.

TINDAKAN: MENUNGGU.

Khaem menangkap titik balik Chapter 5: musuhnya bukan lagi hanya pendeta dan juru tulis.

Musuhnya adalah catatan itu sendiri.

Arsip hidup bertaring.

Dan arsip itu baru saja memasukkan namanya ke dalam mulut.

content.en:
Khaem watches the stone wall write his name as if the city is finally admitting it has always been alive.

SUBJECT: KHAEM.

STATUS: UNREGISTERED.

ACTION: PENDING.

Nebet’s face stays still, but her breathing changes.

Khaem notices before she does.

The wall scratches again.

A second entry forms beneath his.

SUBJECT: NEBET.

STATUS: IRREGULAR.

ACTION: CORRECTION.

Nebet stares at the letters like they are teeth.

She laughs once—dry, humorless.

'It finally remembered me,' she says.

Khaem asks what IRREGULAR means.

Nebet answers: 'It means I escaped the categories long enough to become a problem.'

The wall’s writing deepens.

Nebet’s shadow on the floor shifts, thinning at the edges as if the ledger is already scraping her out of the room.

Khaem reaches for her wrist.

Her skin is warm.

But her name on the wall feels cold.

Nebet tells him the ledger corrects by making witnesses unreliable.

First it removes your name from records.

Then it removes your name from mouths.

Finally it removes your name from your own memory.

Khaem hears the horror: erasure is not death.

It is being alive while nobody can find you—including yourself.

Nebet presses the black-cloth papyri into Khaem’s hands.

She says, 'If it takes me, don’t save me by calling my name.

Save me by making it record what it did.'

Khaem refuses.

Nebet smiles, almost kind.

'Refusal is young,' she says. 'Learn speed.'

She takes one rejected tag from the metal box, flips it to the gate-mark, and presses it against the wall.

The wall shudders.

The writing stutters.

For one breath, the ledger hesitates—confused by a physical contradiction.

Nebet uses that breath to push Khaem toward the tomb crack.

'Run with the accusation,' she whispers.

Khaem escapes into the necropolis wind with the papyri and a new fear.

Behind him the wall keeps writing.

Chapter 5 ends with Khaem realizing the ledger can bite anyone, not only thieves—and that the price of fighting the back ledger may be losing the one ally who taught him how to see it.
content.id:
Khaem menatap dinding batu menulis namanya seolah kota akhirnya mengaku bahwa ia selalu hidup.

SUBJEK: KHAEM.

STATUS: BELUM TERDAFTAR.

TINDAKAN: MENUNGGU.

Wajah Nebet tetap datar, tetapi napasnya berubah.

Khaem menyadarinya lebih dulu.

Dinding menggores lagi.

Entri kedua terbentuk di bawah entri Khaem.

SUBJEK: NEBET.

STATUS: TIDAK TERATUR.

TINDAKAN: KOREKSI.

Nebet menatap huruf-huruf itu seperti taring.

Ia tertawa sekali—kering, tanpa lucu.

'Akhirnya ia ingat aku,' katanya.

Khaem bertanya apa arti TIDAK TERATUR.

Nebet menjawab: 'Artinya aku cukup lama lolos dari kotak-kotaknya sampai aku jadi masalah.'

Tulisan dinding menajam.

Bayangan Nebet di lantai bergeser, menipis di pinggir seolah buku kas sudah mulai mengikisnya keluar dari ruangan.

Khaem meraih pergelangan Nebet.

Kulitnya hangat.

Namun nama Nebet di dinding terasa dingin.

Nebet berkata buku kas mengoreksi dengan membuat saksi jadi tak bisa dipercaya.

Pertama ia menghapus namamu dari catatan.

Lalu ia menghapus namamu dari mulut.

Terakhir ia menghapus namamu dari ingatanmu sendiri.

Khaem menangkap horornya: penghapusan bukan mati.

Penghapusan adalah hidup ketika tak ada yang bisa menemukanmu—termasuk dirimu.

Nebet menekan papirus kain hitam ke tangan Khaem.

Ia berkata, 'Kalau aku diambil, jangan selamatkan aku dengan memanggil namaku.

Selamatkan aku dengan memaksa ia mencatat apa yang ia lakukan.'

Khaem menolak.

Nebet tersenyum, nyaris lembut.

'Penolakan itu muda,' katanya. 'Belajar cepat.'

Nebet mengambil satu label ditolak dari kotak metal, membaliknya ke tanda gerbang, lalu menekankannya ke dinding.

Dinding berguncang.

Tulisan tersendat.

Sesaat buku kas ragu—bingung oleh kontradiksi fisik.

Nebet memakai sesaat itu untuk mendorong Khaem ke retakan makam.

'Lari bawa tuduhannya,' bisiknya.

Khaem lolos ke angin nekropolis membawa papirus dan takut baru.

Di belakangnya dinding terus menulis.

Chapter 5 berakhir saat Khaem sadar buku kas bisa menggigit siapa pun, bukan hanya pencuri—dan harga melawan buku kas belakang mungkin adalah kehilangan satu sekutu yang mengajarinya cara melihatnya.

content.en:
Seeing his name appear on stone breaks something in Khaem’s logic.

He was raised to believe gods can be appeased.

He realizes he has begun treating the registry like a god.

He asks, softly, 'What do you want?'

The wall scratches.

Not an answer.

A category.

Khaem understands the ledger wants inputs.

So he gives it one.

He takes a rejected tag and presses it flat against the stone.

He speaks a funerary line—not to a gate, but to the registry.

'I am named. Therefore I may pass.'

The wall reacts.

The glyphs crawl faster, hungry.

A new entry forms:

OFFERING: ACCEPTED.

ACTION: ACCELERATED.

Nebet’s face tightens.

'You fed it,' she says.

Khaem insists he is negotiating.

Nebet answers, 'You can’t negotiate with a ledger. It only knows increase.'

The stone’s writing deepens like a wound.

Khaem feels the tug behind his eyes sharpen—like a hook sunk into his thoughts.

Suddenly he remembers things that are not his: a clerk’s hands stamping, a priest’s calm smile, a mortuary boy’s blank focus.

The ledger is not listening.

It is indexing.

It is pulling him into its categories by force of attention.

Khaem panics and tries to stop speaking.

But the registry has already recorded the act: OFFERING.

Now it expects more.

Nebet slaps the tag from his hand and throws it back into the metal box.

She says, 'Never teach a system what you are willing to give.'

They run deeper into the chamber as the wall keeps writing.

Chapter 5 ends with Khaem realizing the back ledger cannot be bribed with clay or words.

It can only be starved—or jammed with a contradiction so large it cannot digest.
content.id:
Melihat namanya muncul di batu mematahkan sesuatu di logika Khaem.

Ia dibesarkan dengan keyakinan bahwa dewa bisa ditenangkan.

Ia sadar ia mulai memperlakukan registri seperti dewa.

Ia bertanya pelan, 'Kau mau apa?'

Dinding menggores.

Bukan jawaban.

Kategori.

Khaem mengerti buku kas itu menginginkan masukan.

Maka ia memberi satu.

Ia mengambil satu label ditolak dan menekannya rata ke batu.

Ia mengucap satu baris pemakaman—bukan ke gerbang, melainkan ke registri.

'Aku bernama. Maka aku boleh lewat.'

Dinding bereaksi.

Hieroglif merayap lebih cepat, lapar.

Entri baru terbentuk:

PERSEMBAHAN: DITERIMA.

TINDAKAN: DIPERCEPAT.

Wajah Nebet menegang.

'Kau memberinya makan,' katanya.

Khaem bersikeras ia sedang bernegosiasi.

Nebet menjawab, 'Kau tidak bisa negosiasi dengan buku kas. Ia hanya mengenal kenaikan.'

Tulisan batu menajam seperti luka.

Khaem merasakan tarikan di belakang mata menajam—seperti kail ditancap ke pikirannya.

Tiba-tiba ia mengingat hal-hal yang bukan miliknya: tangan juru tulis mengecap, senyum tenang pendeta, fokus kosong anak rumah mayat.

Buku kas itu bukan mendengar.

Ia mengindeks.

Ia menarik Khaem masuk ke kotaknya lewat perhatian.

Khaem panik dan mencoba berhenti bicara.

Namun registri sudah mencatat tindakannya: PERSEMBAHAN.

Kini ia menuntut lebih.

Nebet menampar label itu dari tangan Khaem dan melemparkannya kembali ke kotak metal.

Nebet berkata, 'Jangan pernah mengajari sistem apa yang kau bersedia berikan.'

Mereka lari lebih dalam sementara dinding terus menulis.

Chapter 5 berakhir saat Khaem sadar buku kas belakang tidak bisa disuap dengan tanah liat atau kata.

Buku kas hanya bisa dilaparkan—atau disumbat dengan kontradiksi yang terlalu besar untuk ia cerna.

title.en: The Spell That Files the Gods

content.en:
Nebet tells Khaem the Book of the Dead is an address book for gates.

Khaem stares at the wall where the registry writes his name.

He realizes a gate is only a gate because something agrees to behave like one.

A registry behaves like a god because everyone treats it like fate.

Khaem opens the black-cloth papyri and finds a line he has never dared to read aloud.

It is written in cramped, ugly glyphs—like the papyrus under the dead man’s tongue.

A working hand.

Not a sacred one.

Khaem speaks it.

The lantern flame bends.

The stone wall shudders.

For the first time, the registry pauses mid-scratch.

A new heading forms above the ledger’s columns:

REGISTRY: IDENTIFIED.

OWNER: UNDECLARED.

OBSTRUCTION: ACTIVE.

Nebet’s breath catches.

The Book did not open the gate.

It named the gate.

And naming is the first act of control.

Khaem reads another line.

The heading expands:

WITNESS: PRESENT.

TESTIMONY: RECORDED.

The wall begins recording itself—its own actions, its own categories, its own hunger.

Khaem realizes the spell is not a weapon.

It is a filing method.

A way to force the Duat’s administration to index the administrators.

But every forced record creates enemies.

Nebet grabs Khaem’s shoulder and says, 'Stop before it learns your voice.'

Too late.

The wall adds a final line:

VOICE PATTERN: CAPTURED.

Khaem feels the leash tighten behind his eyes.

He has won a moment.

And paid with a signature.

Chapter 5 ends with the registry silent at last—temporarily jammed—while Khaem and Nebet flee the chamber carrying the terrifying knowledge that the Book can make the Duat record its own guilt.

But using it even once teaches the system how to find you again.
content.id:
Nebet berkata Kitab Orang Mati adalah buku alamat untuk gerbang.

Khaem menatap dinding tempat registri menulis namanya.

Ia sadar gerbang hanya gerbang karena ada sesuatu yang setuju berperilaku sebagai gerbang.

Registri berperilaku seperti dewa karena semua orang memperlakukannya seperti takdir.

Khaem membuka papirus kain hitam dan menemukan satu baris yang tak pernah ia berani ucapkan.

Baris itu ditulis dalam hieroglif sempit dan jelek—seperti papirus di bawah lidah jenazah.

Tulisan tangan kerja.

Bukan tangan suci.

Khaem mengucapkannya.

Api lampu melengkung.

Dinding batu berguncang.

Untuk pertama kali, registri berhenti di tengah gores.

Judul baru muncul di atas kolom-kolomnya:

REGISTRI: TERIDENTIFIKASI.

PEMILIK: TIDAK DINYATAKAN.

PENGHALANGAN: AKTIF.

Napas Nebet tertahan.

Kitab itu tidak membuka gerbang.

Kitab itu menamai gerbang.

Dan penamaan adalah tindakan pertama kendali.

Khaem membaca baris lain.

Judul melebar:

SAKSI: HADIR.

KESAKSIAN: TERCATAT.

Dinding mulai mencatat dirinya sendiri—tindakannya, kategorinya, kelaparannya.

Khaem sadar mantra itu bukan senjata.

Mantra itu metode pengarsipan.

Cara memaksa administrasi Duat mengindeks para administrator.

Namun setiap catatan paksa menciptakan musuh.

Nebet menggenggam bahu Khaem dan berkata, 'Berhenti sebelum ia belajar suaramu.'

Terlambat.

Dinding menambah satu baris terakhir:

POLA SUARA: TERTANGKAP.

Khaem merasakan tali kekang di belakang mata mengencang.

Ia menang satu momen.

Dan membayar dengan tanda tangan.

Chapter 5 berakhir dengan registri akhirnya sunyi—sementara tersumbat—ketika Khaem dan Nebet kabur membawa pengetahuan mengerikan: Kitab itu bisa memaksa Duat mencatat rasa bersalahnya sendiri.

Tapi memakainya sekali saja mengajari sistem cara menemukanmu lagi.

content.en:
Khaem watches the ledger’s last line pulse: ACTION: PENDING.

He thinks they have time.

Then the stone scratches once—decisive.

ACTION: EXECUTED.

Khaem feels it before he understands.

The air in the chamber shifts, and his memories wobble.

For a heartbeat, he cannot remember Hori’s face.

He can remember hands, natron, resin—but not the name.

Not the person.

Nebet swears and grabs the metal box.

She shouts, 'It’s starting!'

Khaem panics and says his own name aloud, as if speaking it can anchor it.

The wall answers by rewriting:

SUBJECT: [REDACTED].

Khaem’s throat tightens.

He realizes the ledger’s correction is not a blade.

It is a softening.

A smoothing of rough edges until the world cannot catch on you.

Outside the tomb crack, footsteps pass.

Guards’ voices.

They speak about 'an apprentice' with no name, as if Khaem has already become a description.

Nebet forces Khaem to write his name into wet clay—again and again—on rejected tags, on the inside of his sleeve, on the stone with a broken reed.

'Make too many copies,' she orders. 'A system can erase one record. It struggles with a swarm.'

Khaem’s hands shake as he writes.

He feels the correction tugging at his thoughts like a tide.

He realizes the true terror: if the ledger finishes, he won’t be able to resist.

He won’t even know he should.

Chapter 5 ends with Khaem and Nebet fleeing the necropolis carrying a bundle of clay scraps covered in one repeated word—KHAEM—while behind them the wall continues its quiet work.

Erasure does not chase.

It edits.
content.id:
Khaem menatap baris terakhir buku kas berdenyut: TINDAKAN: MENUNGGU.

Ia mengira mereka punya waktu.

Lalu batu menggores sekali—tegas.

TINDAKAN: DIEKSEKUSI.

Khaem merasakannya sebelum mengerti.

Udara ruang berubah, dan ingatannya goyah.

Sesaat ia tidak ingat wajah Hori.

Ia ingat tangan, natron, resin—tapi bukan nama.

Bukan orangnya.

Nebet mengumpat dan meraih kotak metal.

Ia berteriak, 'Mulai!'

Khaem panik dan menyebut namanya sendiri keras-keras, seolah suara bisa menambatkannya.

Dinding menjawab dengan menulis ulang:

SUBJEK: [DIHITAMKAN].

Tenggorokan Khaem mengunci.

Ia sadar koreksi buku kas bukan bilah.

Itu pelunakan.

Menghaluskan tepi sampai dunia tidak bisa menangkapmu.

Di luar retak makam, langkah kaki lewat.

Suara penjaga.

Mereka menyebut 'seorang magang' tanpa nama, seolah Khaem sudah jadi deskripsi.

Nebet memaksa Khaem menulis namanya ke tanah liat basah—berulang-ulang—di label ditolak, di bagian dalam lengan bajunya, di batu dengan buluh patah.

'Buat terlalu banyak salinan,' perintah Nebet. 'Sistem bisa menghapus satu catatan. Ia kesulitan menghadapi sekumpulan.'

Tangan Khaem gemetar saat menulis.

Ia merasakan koreksi menarik pikirannya seperti pasang.

Ia paham teror sejatinya: kalau buku kas selesai, ia tak akan bisa melawan.

Ia bahkan tak akan tahu ia harus melawan.

Chapter 5 berakhir saat Khaem dan Nebet kabur dari nekropolis membawa buntalan serpihan tanah liat yang penuh satu kata berulang—KHAEM—sementara di belakang mereka dinding melanjutkan pekerjaan sunyinya.

Penghapusan tidak mengejar.

Penghapusan mengedit.

preview.en: With the back ledger beginning to 'correct' him, Khaem and Nebet risk a forbidden rite to summon a counterseal—only to learn the judge of the dead is also an auditor of records.
preview.id: Saat buku kas belakang mulai ‘mengoreksi’ dirinya, Khaem dan Nebet nekat melakukan ritus terlarang untuk memanggil segel balik—namun mendapati bahwa hakim orang mati juga auditor berkas.

content.en:
By morning the city looked unchanged, which was how the back ledger preferred it.

Khaem and Nebet moved through crowds that did not see them as chased. They were not chased by feet.

They were chased by edits.

Khaem felt it in small failures.

A vendor who should have recognized his face stared past him.

A guard who should have demanded a token waved him through without seeing him.

Invisibility was not safety. It was a symptom.

Nebet led him to a place the temple did not advertise: a jackal shrine wedged between warehouses, its paint faded, its offerings mostly dust.

It was not grand enough to be controlled.

Which meant it was useful.

Inside, the air smelled of old incense and natron—death’s cleanliness without the temple’s polish.

On the wall was a crude image of Anubis, jackal-headed, holding scales in one hand and a reed pen in the other.

Khaem frowned. He had never seen the pen before.

Nebet said, 'They paint what they need to admit.'

Khaem asked why they were here.

Nebet answered: 'Because only one authority in the Duat can invalidate a stamp without asking the temple’s permission.'

Khaem’s mouth went dry.

'Anubis?' he whispered.

Nebet nodded.

Not the public Anubis in songs.

The administrative Anubis who keeps the weighing honest.

Khaem looked at the black-cloth papyri.

He had used it once, enough to make the registry hesitate.

But hesitation was not reversal.

They needed a counterseal.

Something that could mark Khaem as untouchable to the ledger’s correction.

Nebet unpacked what she had carried: a shallow bowl, a scrap of linen, a pinch of natron, and a sliver of gold leaf.

Khaem stared.

Gold.

Too expensive for a street shrine.

Nebet said, 'It isn’t payment.

It’s a signature.

Gold is the metal that remembers.

That’s why kings use it for names.'

Khaem did not like how much sense that made.

Nebet ordered him to write his name.

Not on papyrus.

On the inside of the linen scrap, with ink mixed from soot and natron water.

Khaem’s hands shook as he wrote KHAEM in careful strokes, as if the letters could hold him in place.

Nebet placed the gold leaf over the wet ink.

It clung, imperfectly.

A cheap imitation of eternity.

Then she opened the Book of the Dead to a page that was not for funerals.

It was for audits.

The glyphs were harsh, functional.

Khaem understood: the book contained not only prayers for souls.

It contained procedures for officials.

Nebet told Khaem to speak the line aloud.

Khaem hesitated.

The shrine was small, but speaking into it felt like speaking into a courtroom.

He read.

The words did not praise Anubis.

They did not beg.

They accused.

They said: If you are the judge of truth, then judge the record that claims truth.

The shrine’s lamp flame bent.

Dust rose from the floor in a slow spiral, as if the room inhaled.

Khaem felt the tug behind his eyes tighten.

Then, for one breath, release.

Nebet’s face went pale.

A shadow formed on the wall behind the Anubis painting.

Not a jackal-headed god stepping into light.

A silhouette like a seal impression—flat, absolute.

A voice spoke without volume.

'NAME,' it said.

Khaem’s throat closed.

He realized the shrine was not asking for devotion.

It was asking for a filing key.

He forced the word out: 'Khaem.'

The shadow’s presence sharpened.

'FULL NAME,' the voice said.

Khaem froze.

He had never been taught his full formal name.

Apprentices were not meant to need it.

Nebet swore under her breath.

She whispered, 'That’s the trick.

They keep the full names in the temple registry.

So you cannot summon protection without their paperwork.'

Khaem felt panic rise.

The back ledger was already eating his recognition.

Now even the judge demanded a fuller label.

Then Khaem remembered the nameless dead man.

Ink-stained lips.

A papyrus under a tongue.

A message hidden where only the dead could carry it.

He realized he was not the only file being edited.

He reached into the metal box and pulled out a half-scraped tag—the one that still had a ghost of a name pressed deep.

He held it up.

'Is this enough?' he asked the shadow.

The shrine went colder.

The shadow moved—not forward, but inward.

The voice said, 'EVIDENCE.'

Khaem understood.

Anubis was not here to rescue.

He was here to verify.

Nebet’s jaw tightened.

She placed the stolen tag on the bowl like an offering.

Khaem placed the linen with his name beneath it.

He laid the granary strip beside them.

Three pieces: the theft, the process, the victim.

The shadow lingered.

Then a sound like a stamp pressed into air.

Khaem felt heat bloom on his wrist—exactly where a seal would land.

He looked down.

A faint looped mark appeared.

Then a second mark crossed it—an X-like counterseal, rough, as if cut by a claw.

Nebet exhaled sharply.

'A counterseal,' she whispered.

Khaem felt no relief.

Only weight.

Because a counterseal meant he had been entered into an even higher registry.

The voice spoke one final line:

'CORRECTION DELAYED. HEARING SCHEDULED.'

Khaem stared.

Even justice had an appointment.

The shadow dissolved.

The shrine returned to dust and smallness.

Outside, the city continued to sell fish and gossip, unaware that a hearing had just been scheduled in the Duat.

Nebet grabbed the bowl and wrapped the evidence quickly.

She said, 'We bought time.

Not freedom.'

Khaem looked at the counterseal on his wrist.

He understood Chapter 6’s lesson: the afterlife is not governed by mercy.

It is governed by proof.

And the judge of the dead is not only a scale.

He is a clerk with a pen.

Chapter 6 ends with Khaem and Nebet leaving the jackal shrine as temple bells began to ring.

The bells were not calling worship.

They were calling clerks.

Because somewhere, the registry had noticed a contradiction that refused to be filed quietly.
content.id:
Pagi membuat kota tampak tidak berubah, dan begitulah buku kas belakang menyukainya.

Khaem dan Nebet bergerak melewati kerumunan yang tidak melihat mereka sebagai buruan. Mereka tidak dikejar kaki.

Mereka dikejar oleh edit.

Khaem merasakannya pada kegagalan kecil.

Penjual yang seharusnya mengenali wajahnya menatap lewat.

Penjaga yang seharusnya menahan justru melambaikan lewat tanpa melihat.

Tak-terlihat bukan aman. Itu gejala.

Nebet membawa Khaem ke tempat yang kuil tidak gembar-gemborkan: sebuah makam-serigala kecil terselip di antara gudang, catnya pudar, persembahannya kebanyakan debu.

Tempat itu tidak cukup megah untuk dikendalikan.

Artinya berguna.

Di dalam, udara berbau dupa tua dan natron—bersihnya kematian tanpa polesan kuil.

Di dinding ada gambar kasar Anubis, berkepala serigala, memegang timbangan di satu tangan dan pena buluh di tangan lain.

Khaem mengernyit. Ia belum pernah melihat pena itu.

Nebet berkata, 'Mereka melukis apa yang mereka butuh akui.'

Khaem bertanya kenapa mereka di sini.

Nebet menjawab: 'Karena hanya satu otoritas di Duat yang bisa membatalkan cap tanpa minta izin kuil.'

Mulut Khaem kering.

'Anubis?' bisiknya.

Nebet mengangguk.

Bukan Anubis publik dalam lagu.

Anubis administrasi yang menjaga penimbangan tetap jujur.

Khaem menatap papirus kain hitam.

Ia pernah memakainya sekali, cukup untuk membuat registri ragu.

Tapi ragu bukan pembalikan.

Mereka butuh segel balik.

Sesuatu yang bisa menandai Khaem sebagai tak tersentuh oleh koreksi buku kas.

Nebet mengeluarkan perlengkapan: mangkuk dangkal, selembar linen, sejumput natron, dan sepotong daun emas.

Khaem menatap.

Emas.

Terlalu mahal untuk makam jalanan.

Nebet berkata, 'Bukan bayaran.

Itu tanda tangan.

Emas adalah logam yang ingat.

Itulah sebabnya raja memakainya untuk nama.'

Khaem tidak suka betapa masuk akalnya itu.

Nebet menyuruhnya menulis namanya.

Bukan di papirus.

Di bagian dalam kain linen, dengan tinta dari jelaga dan air natron.

Tangan Khaem gemetar saat menulis KHAEM dengan gores hati-hati, seolah huruf bisa menahannya tetap ada.

Nebet menempelkan daun emas di atas tinta basah.

Daun itu menempel tidak sempurna.

Tiruan murah dari keabadian.

Lalu Nebet membuka Kitab Orang Mati pada halaman yang bukan untuk pemakaman.

Halaman untuk audit.

Hieroglifnya keras, fungsional.

Khaem paham: kitab itu bukan cuma doa jiwa.

Kitab itu memuat prosedur pejabat.

Nebet menyuruh Khaem mengucap barisnya.

Khaem ragu.

Makam kecil, tapi mengucap di dalamnya terasa seperti bicara di pengadilan.

Ia membaca.

Kata-katanya tidak memuji Anubis.

Tidak memohon.

Kata-katanya menuduh.

Kata-katanya berkata: jika kau hakim kebenaran, maka hakimi catatan yang mengklaim kebenaran.

Api lampu makam melengkung.

Debu terangkat berputar pelan, seolah ruangan menarik napas.

Khaem merasakan tarikan di belakang mata mengencang.

Lalu, sesaat, lepas.

Wajah Nebet pucat.

Bayangan terbentuk di dinding belakang lukisan Anubis.

Bukan dewa masuk cahaya.

Melainkan siluet seperti bekas segel—datar, absolut.

Suara berbicara tanpa volume.

'NAMA,' katanya.

Tenggorokan Khaem menutup.

Ia sadar ini bukan permintaan iman.

Ini permintaan kunci arsip.

Ia memaksa kata keluar: 'Khaem.'

Bayangan menajam.

'NAMA LENGKAP,' katanya.

Khaem membeku.

Ia tidak pernah diajari nama formal lengkap.

Magang tidak seharusnya butuh.

Nebet mengumpat pelan.

Ia berbisik, 'Itu triknya.

Nama lengkap disimpan di registri kuil.

Jadi kau tak bisa memanggil perlindungan tanpa berkas mereka.'

Panik naik.

Buku kas belakang sudah mulai memakan pengenalannya.

Sekarang bahkan hakim meminta label lebih penuh.

Lalu Khaem teringat jenazah tanpa nama.

Bibir tinta.

Papirus di bawah lidah.

Pesan disembunyikan di tempat hanya orang mati bisa bawa.

Ia sadar ia bukan satu-satunya berkas yang sedang diedit.

Ia meraih kotak metal dan menarik satu label setengah terkikis—yang masih menyisakan hantu nama.

Ia mengangkatnya.

'Ini cukup?' tanyanya.

Makam jadi lebih dingin.

Bayangan bergerak—bukan maju, tetapi masuk.

'EVIDENSI,' kata suara.

Khaem mengerti.

Anubis tidak datang untuk menyelamatkan.

Anubis datang untuk memverifikasi.

Rahang Nebet mengencang.

Nebet meletakkan label curian itu di mangkuk seperti persembahan.

Khaem meletakkan linen bernama di bawahnya.

Ia menaruh pita lumbung di samping.

Tiga potong: pencurian, pemrosesan, korban.

Bayangan bertahan.

Lalu bunyi seperti cap menekan udara.

Khaem merasakan panas mekar di pergelangan—tempat segel mendarat.

Ia menatap.

Tanda gerbang melingkar samar muncul.

Lalu tanda kedua menyeberanginya—segel balik seperti X kasar, seolah dicakar.

Nebet mengembus tajam.

'Cap balik,' bisiknya.

Khaem tidak lega.

Ia hanya merasa berat.

Karena cap balik artinya ia masuk registri lebih tinggi.

Suara berkata satu kalimat terakhir:

'KOREKSI DITUNDA. SIDANG DIJADWALKAN.'

Khaem menatap.

Bahkan keadilan punya janji temu.

Bayangan lenyap.

Makam kembali jadi debu dan kecil.

Di luar, kota tetap menjual ikan dan gosip, tak tahu sidang baru saja dijadwalkan di Duat.

Nebet meraih mangkuk dan membungkus evidensi cepat.

Ia berkata, 'Kita membeli waktu.

Bukan kebebasan.'

Khaem menatap segel balik di pergelangan.

Ia menangkap pelajaran Chapter 6: akhirat tidak diperintah belas kasih.

Akhirat diperintah bukti.

Dan hakim orang mati bukan hanya timbangan.

Ia juru tulis dengan pena.

Chapter 6 berakhir saat Khaem dan Nebet meninggalkan makam serigala ketika lonceng kuil mulai berbunyi.

Lonceng itu bukan memanggil ibadah.

Lonceng itu memanggil juru tulis.

Karena di suatu tempat, registri sudah mencium kontradiksi yang menolak diberkas diam-diam.

content.en:
Khaem lays out the three pieces on the shrine bowl—stolen tag, linen name, granary strip—as if arranging his own survival into an argument.

The shadow of Anubis forms like a seal pressed into air.

It says: 'EVIDENCE.'

Nebet offers the tag first.

The shadow does not react.

Khaem offers the granary strip.

Still nothing.

Then the voice says something that feels like the coldest kind of mercy: 'PROVENANCE.'

Khaem does not understand.

Nebet does.

She whispers, 'It means: where did you get it, and who touched it before you?'

Khaem realizes the trap.

The system has built a way to reject truth by questioning its chain of custody.

If the evidence is stolen, it becomes tainted.

If it is tainted, it becomes unusable.

And so the theft that created the crime also invalidates the proof of the crime.

Khaem tries to explain the mortuary, the auditing priest, the metal box.

The shadow remains flat.

It says, 'UNVERIFIED.'

Khaem’s wrist burns briefly as if expecting a stamp—and receives none.

Nebet’s face tightens.

She understands what this means.

Anubis is not denying that the theft happened.

He is refusing to let a correction be delayed without a record that can survive appeal.

Nebet makes a brutal suggestion: they must obtain a temple-certified copy of the back ledger entry.

Khaem stares.

A certified copy would require entering the temple registry.

The very place that controls full names.

The very place that profits from erasure.

Chapter 6 ends with Khaem leaving the jackal shrine with no counterseal and a terrifying clarity: even the judge of truth will not act on truth that cannot be filed.

To be saved, they must make their accusation bureaucratically clean—without letting it be cleaned away.
content.id:
Khaem menata tiga benda di mangkuk makam—label curian, linen bernama, pita lumbung—seolah menyusun hidupnya sendiri menjadi argumen.

Bayangan Anubis terbentuk seperti segel menekan udara.

Ia berkata: 'EVIDENSI.'

Nebet menyerahkan label dulu.

Bayangan itu tidak bereaksi.

Khaem menyerahkan pita lumbung.

Tetap tidak.

Lalu suara itu mengucapkan sesuatu yang terasa seperti belas kasih paling dingin: 'ASAL-USUL.'

Khaem tidak paham.

Nebet paham.

Ia berbisik, 'Artinya: kau dapat ini dari mana, dan siapa saja yang menyentuhnya sebelum kau?'

Khaem menangkap jebakannya.

Sistem membangun cara menolak kebenaran dengan mempertanyakan rantai penguasaan.
