Kalau bukti itu dicuri, bukti itu tercemar.

Kalau tercemar, bukti itu tak bisa dipakai.

Dan pencurian yang menciptakan kejahatan juga membatalkan bukti kejahatannya.

Khaem mencoba menjelaskan rumah mayat, pendeta audit, kotak metal.

Bayangan tetap datar.

Ia berkata, 'TIDAK TERVERIFIKASI.'

Pergelangan Khaem terasa panas sekejap seolah menunggu cap—dan tidak mendapat apa pun.

Wajah Nebet menegang.

Nebet mengerti artinya.

Anubis bukan menyangkal pencurian itu terjadi.

Anubis menolak menunda koreksi tanpa catatan yang bisa bertahan jika diajukan banding.

Nebet mengusulkan hal brutal: mereka harus mendapatkan salinan tersertifikasi dari entri buku kas belakang.

Khaem menatap.

Salinan tersertifikasi berarti masuk ke registri kuil.

Tempat yang menyimpan nama lengkap.

Tempat yang diuntungkan oleh penghapusan.

Chapter 6 berakhir saat Khaem meninggalkan makam serigala tanpa segel balik dan dengan kejernihan mengerikan: bahkan hakim kebenaran tak akan bertindak atas kebenaran yang tak bisa diberkas.

Kalau ingin selamat, mereka harus membuat tuduhan mereka ‘bersih’ secara birokratis—tanpa membiarkannya dibersihkan sampai hilang.

content.en:
Khaem speaks his short name.

The shadow demands the full name.

Khaem cannot provide it.

Nebet steps forward and says, 'Then mark me.'

Khaem whirls on her. 'No.'

Nebet does not look at him.

She looks at the shadow like a person looking at a clerk.

She says, 'I can give my full name. I kept it. That’s why I still have it.'

Khaem realizes, with a hollow shock, that Nebet has lived like a fugitive long enough to keep what he was never taught.

Nebet speaks her full name into the shrine.

The air tightens.

The shadow stamps.

Heat blooms on Nebet’s wrist.

A counterseal crosses the gate-mark.

Khaem feels nothing.

Nebet exhales as if she has been holding her breath for years.

Then the voice says: 'WITNESS PROTECTED. COURIER UNFILED.'

Khaem understands the cruelty.

The Duat has protected the testimony’s holder.

But left the messenger vulnerable.

Nebet tells Khaem this is still a win.

'A protected witness can survive long enough to speak,' she says.

Khaem answers, bitter, 'And an unfiled courier can be erased before the hearing.'

Nebet nods. 'Yes.'

She grabs his sleeve. 'So we move like we’re already dead. Quiet. Fast. Unmemorable.'

Chapter 6 ends with Khaem walking behind Nebet through the morning streets, realizing their roles have reversed: Nebet is now the protected record, and he is the disposable delivery.

If he wants to live, he must make himself harder to file than a ghost.
content.id:
Khaem menyebut nama pendeknya.

Bayangan menuntut nama lengkap.

Khaem tak bisa menyediakannya.

Nebet maju dan berkata, 'Kalau begitu cap aku.'

Khaem berbalik tajam. 'Jangan.'

Nebet tidak menatap Khaem.

Nebet menatap bayangan seperti orang menatap juru tulis.

Ia berkata, 'Aku bisa memberi nama lengkap. Aku menjaganya. Itu sebabnya aku masih punya.'

Khaem tersentak hampa: Nebet hidup seperti buronan cukup lama sampai menyimpan hal yang ia tidak pernah diajari.

Nebet menyebut nama lengkapnya di dalam makam.

Udara menegang.

Bayangan mengecap.

Panas mekar di pergelangan Nebet.

Segel balik menyeberangi tanda gerbang.

Khaem tidak merasakan apa pun.

Nebet mengembus seolah menahan napas bertahun-tahun.

Lalu suara itu berkata: 'SAKSI DILINDUNGI. KURIR BELUM DIBERKAS.'

Khaem menangkap kekejamannya.

Duat melindungi pemegang kesaksian.

Tapi membiarkan pengantar rentan.

Nebet berkata itu tetap kemenangan.

'Saksi terlindungi bisa hidup cukup lama untuk bicara,' katanya.

Khaem menjawab getir, 'Dan kurir yang belum diberkas bisa dihapus sebelum sidang.'

Nebet mengangguk. 'Ya.'

Nebet mencengkeram lengan baju Khaem. 'Jadi kita bergerak seperti kita sudah mati. Sunyi. Cepat. Tidak mudah diingat.'

Chapter 6 berakhir saat Khaem berjalan di belakang Nebet melewati jalan pagi, sadar peran mereka terbalik: Nebet kini catatan yang dilindungi, dan Khaem pengiriman yang bisa dibuang.

Kalau ia ingin hidup, ia harus membuat dirinya lebih sulit diberkas daripada hantu.

content.en:
Nebet chooses the jackal shrine because it is small, neglected, and therefore 'free.'

But neglected does not mean unowned.

Khaem speaks the accusing audit-line from the Book.

The lamp flame bends.

The shadow forms.

It asks for NAME.

Everything feels real.

Then Khaem notices the offering dish.

It is too clean.

Newer than the dust.

And beneath it, a thin thread of copper runs into the wall.

A wire.

A listening cord.

The shrine is not only a shrine.

It is a receiver.

A place where desperate people come to speak their names aloud.

A place where the temple harvests full names without needing to steal them.

Nebet sees it too late.

When the shadow demands FULL NAME, it is not Anubis verifying.

It is the temple fishing.

Nebet grabs the bowl and smashes it.

The copper thread snaps.

The shadow flickers.

Outside, bells ring—not to call clerks.

To call guards.

Khaem and Nebet flee, but the damage is done.

Khaem spoke his name into an instrument.

Now the ledger does not need to find him by tag.

It can find him by sound.

Chapter 6 ends with Khaem realizing the most dangerous shrines are the ones that promise privacy.

And the temple’s greatest power is not force.

It is making people volunteer their own paperwork.
content.id:
Nebet memilih makam serigala karena kecil, terlantar, dan karena itu terasa 'bebas.'

Tapi terlantar tidak berarti tak dimiliki.

Khaem mengucap baris audit yang menuduh dari Kitab.

Api lampu melengkung.

Bayangan terbentuk.

Ia meminta NAMA.

Semua terasa nyata.

Lalu Khaem memperhatikan piring persembahan.

Terlalu bersih.

Lebih baru dari debu.

Dan di bawahnya, seutas tembaga tipis masuk ke dinding.

Kawat.

Tali dengar.

Makam itu bukan hanya makam.

Makam itu penerima.

Tempat orang putus asa datang mengucapkan nama keras-keras.

Tempat kuil memanen nama lengkap tanpa perlu mencuri.

Nebet menyadarinya terlambat.

Saat bayangan menuntut NAMA LENGKAP, itu bukan Anubis memverifikasi.

Itu kuil memancing.

Nebet meraih mangkuk dan memecahkannya.

Kawat tembaga putus.

Bayangan berkelip.

Di luar, lonceng berbunyi—bukan memanggil juru tulis.

Memanggil penjaga.

Khaem dan Nebet kabur, tetapi kerusakan sudah terjadi.

Khaem mengucap namanya ke alat.

Kini buku kas tidak perlu mencarinya lewat label.

Buku kas bisa mencarinya lewat bunyi.

Chapter 6 berakhir saat Khaem sadar makam paling berbahaya adalah yang menjanjikan privasi.

Dan kuasa terbesar kuil bukan kekuatan.

Kuasa terbesar kuil adalah membuat orang menyerahkan berkasnya sendiri.

content.en:
The counterseal burns onto Khaem’s wrist and the shadow speaks:

'CORRECTION DELAYED. HEARING SCHEDULED.'

Khaem demands when.

The shadow answers with the only time the Duat respects:

'SUNDOWN.'

Nebet’s face goes tight.

Sundown is when funerary words are spoken.

Sundown is when the nameless man must be wrapped, processed, and sent.

Khaem realizes the hearing is not in a distant hall of gods.

It is at the threshold where the living finalize paperwork for the dead.

Anubis is not summoning them upward.

He is summoning them back.

Back to the House of Purification.

Back to Hori.

Back to the body that started this.

Nebet says, 'They’re forcing you to bring the evidence into the place where evidence can be confiscated.'

Khaem understands the trap.

A hearing scheduled at sundown is an ambush scheduled in the language of justice.

But a delay has also been granted.

That means they can move openly for a few hours—if they dare.

Khaem decides they must do the unthinkable: return to Hori and tell him everything.

If Hori is loyal, he can protect the body until sundown.

If Hori is compromised, they will learn it in the most dangerous way possible.

Chapter 6 ends with Khaem and Nebet walking toward the House of Purification in daylight, countersealed and carrying stolen tags, as temple bells ring.

They are not running anymore.

They are attending court.

And court, Khaem realizes, is just another mortuary—with better ink.
content.id:
Segel balik terbakar di pergelangan Khaem dan bayangan berkata:

'KOREKSI DITUNDA. SIDANG DIJADWALKAN.'

Khaem menuntut kapan.

Bayangan menjawab dengan satu-satunya waktu yang dihormati Duat:

'SENJA.'

Wajah Nebet mengencang.

Senja adalah waktu kata-kata pemakaman diucapkan.

Senja adalah waktu si tanpa nama harus dibungkus, diproses, dan dikirim.

Khaem sadar sidang itu bukan di aula dewa yang jauh.

Sidang itu di ambang tempat orang hidup memfinalkan berkas orang mati.

Anubis tidak memanggil mereka naik.

Anubis memanggil mereka kembali.

Kembali ke Rumah Pemurnian.

Kembali ke Hori.

Kembali ke tubuh yang memulai semuanya.

Nebet berkata, 'Mereka memaksa kau membawa bukti ke tempat bukti bisa disita.'

Khaem mengerti jebakannya.

Sidang yang dijadwalkan saat senja adalah penyergapan yang dijadwalkan dalam bahasa keadilan.

Namun penundaan juga diberikan.

Artinya mereka bisa bergerak terbuka beberapa jam—kalau berani.

Khaem memutuskan mereka harus melakukan yang tak terpikir: kembali ke Hori dan mengatakan semuanya.

Kalau Hori setia, ia bisa melindungi tubuh itu sampai senja.

Kalau Hori sudah dikompromikan, mereka akan mengetahuinya dengan cara paling berbahaya.

Chapter 6 berakhir saat Khaem dan Nebet berjalan menuju Rumah Pemurnian di siang hari, bercap balik dan membawa label curian, sementara lonceng kuil berbunyi.

Mereka tidak berlari lagi.

Mereka menghadiri pengadilan.

Dan pengadilan, Khaem sadar, hanyalah rumah mayat lain—dengan tinta yang lebih bagus.

preview.en: The counterseal buys Khaem a hearing, not mercy; he and Nebet return to the House of Purification where the dead man’s fate becomes a legal argument, and every spoken name becomes evidence.
preview.id: Segel balik memberi Khaem sidang, bukan belas kasih; ia dan Nebet kembali ke Rumah Pemurnian saat senja, ketika nasib si tanpa nama berubah jadi debat hukum dan setiap nama yang diucapkan berubah jadi bukti.

content.en:
The bells did not sound like worship.

They sounded like timekeeping.

By late afternoon Khaem and Nebet reached the House of Purification from the rear lane where linen was delivered and refuse removed. The place looked unchanged: whitewashed walls, clean floors, the calm cruelty of order.

But Khaem felt the counterseal on his wrist throb faintly, as if reminding him that he was present by permission.

Nebet kept the metal box hidden beneath a bundle of laundry cloth, moving like a woman who belonged to errands.

At the service door a junior attendant tried to stop them.

Nebet did not argue.

She presented a clay tag—blank, official-looking, damp at the edges—and said, 'Replacement inventory. Scribe-master requisition.'

The attendant’s eyes went to the tag.

Then away.

In this city, a tag carried more authority than a face.

They entered.

The embalming rooms smelled of natron and cedar resin. Khaem’s stomach clenched with familiarity.

He expected Hori.

He feared Hori.

They found him in the side chamber where bodies were weighed and documented before the first cut.

Hori was bent over a ledger, reed pen moving, his expression patient as stone.

When he looked up, his gaze did not land on Nebet.

It landed on Khaem’s wrist.

On the counterseal.

Hori’s face tightened—not with anger, but with calculation.

'You brought paperwork into my rooms,' Hori said softly.

Nebet answered, 'We brought proof.'

Hori set the pen down.

'Proof belongs to clerks,' he said.

Khaem stepped forward.

He did not bow.

It felt dangerous not to.

He said, 'They reassigned a dead man’s name. The granary office stamped it. The mortuary prepared it. The back ledger wrote it.'

Hori’s eyes flicked to Nebet’s bundle.

'And you stole tags,' he said.

Nebet did not deny it.

She lifted the laundry cloth and showed the metal box just enough for Hori to see its temple cord cut.

Hori inhaled slowly.

He did not react like a man surprised.

He reacted like a man confirming a suspicion he had been forced to live with.

'You should not have opened that,' he murmured.

Khaem’s throat tightened.

'You knew,' he said.

Hori did not answer.

He glanced toward the corridor.

The House of Purification had many rooms.

It also had many ears.

Hori stood and walked to the door.

He closed it.

Then he did something Khaem had never seen.

He drew a small seal stone from a pouch at his belt.

Not the temple’s public seal.

A private one.

He pressed it into a smear of clay on the doorframe.

The air changed.

The room felt suddenly smaller, more contained.

Nebet’s eyebrows rose.

'A silence seal,' she said.

Hori did not confirm.

He returned to the table and sat.

'You have a hearing,' he said, looking again at Khaem’s wrist. 'The judge does not grant those without expecting a result.'

Khaem asked, 'What result?'

Hori tapped the ledger.

'An outcome that can be filed,' he said.

A filing.

Even in justice.

Hori opened a drawer and pulled out a clay tag.

It was the nameless body’s tag.

Still blank.

Khaem’s anger flared.

Hori held it up.

'Before sundown,' he said, 'this tag must contain a name, or the body becomes unclaimed by law and by ritual.'

Nebet’s voice went sharp.

'By whose law?'

Hori’s smile was thin.

'By the law that keeps the dead moving,' he replied.

Khaem looked at the blank tag and felt the full cruelty of bureaucracy: it did not need to be evil.

It only needed to be continuous.

Nebet placed the granary strip on the table.

Then she placed one rejected tag from the metal box beside it.

The tag still held the faint ghost-press of a name.

Khaem felt the tug behind his eyes.

Hori did not touch it.

He watched it the way a man watches a snake.

'You want me to read stolen clay,' Hori said.

Khaem answered, 'We want you to witness it.'

Hori’s eyes narrowed.

'Witnessing is a stamp,' he said.

Khaem said, 'Then stamp it.'

Silence stretched.

At last Hori took a clean reed pen and dipped it in ink.

He did not write.

He drew a small mark at the edge of the granary strip.

Not an official seal.

A notation.

A clerk’s sign that something has been seen.

Khaem’s counterseal pulsed.

Nebet exhaled.

Hori looked at Khaem and said, 'This is the court you have been granted.'

Khaem frowned.

Hori continued.

'There is no marble hall. No rows of gods. The hearing happens where the paperwork happens.

At sundown, when the dead are finalized.'

Khaem understood.

The House of Purification was the courtroom.

The natron air was the witness stand.

Hori turned to Nebet.

'If the temple learns you are here, you will be corrected before night is complete,' he said.

Nebet’s mouth tightened.

'And if you help us,' she asked, 'what happens to you?'

Hori’s gaze did not soften.

'If I help you, I become visible,' he said.

Khaem felt the weight of that word.

Visible meant vulnerable.

It meant filed.

Hori stood.

He went to the back storage room where confiscated items were kept: oils, knives, charms—anything found on bodies that did not belong in burial.

He returned with a wrapped bundle.

A small jar of ink, sealed.

A second seal stone.

And a strip of papyrus with formal headings.

Khaem’s heart pounded.

Hori placed them on the table and said, 'If you accuse the registry, you must do it in its own format.

You will submit a complaint.'

Nebet laughed once.

Even her laughter sounded tired.

Hori ignored it.

He pointed at the papyrus headings.

'Name of complainant,' he said.

Khaem flinched.

Hori added, 'Name of respondent.'

Nebet’s eyes sharpened.

Khaem asked, 'Who do we write? The temple?'

Hori shook his head.

'No,' he said. 'The registry.'

Khaem stared.

How do you sue a ledger?

Hori answered as if hearing the unasked question.

'You don’t sue it,' he said. 'You force it to acknowledge a contradiction. You file a record that conflicts with its record.

If the judge accepts the conflict, the ledger must hold both.

And if it holds both, it cannot correct cleanly.'

Nebet leaned in.

'And the dead man’s name?' she asked.

Hori pointed at the rejected tag.

'Find it,' he said. 'But do not speak it here until the complaint is written.

A spoken name is usable.

A written accusation is harder to steal.'

Khaem looked at Nebet.

For the first time since the riverbank, there was a path that resembled a plan.

A terrible plan.

Hori moved to the doorway and listened.

The bells outside began to shift tone.

Sundown approaching.

Hori returned and said, 'When the sun touches the horizon, the clerks will come for the final tags.

They will expect compliance.

You will give them a document instead.'

Nebet asked, 'And if they refuse to accept it?'

Hori’s expression did not change.

'Then the hearing becomes physical,' he said.

Khaem swallowed.

He realized the court was not protected by walls.

It was protected by procedure.

If procedure failed, only bodies remained.

Khaem dipped the reed pen into the sealed ink.

He began to write.

Not a prayer.

Not a confession.

A complaint.

In the House of Purification, under the smell of natron, Khaem learned the sharpest form of magic in the city: a document that forces someone else to answer.

Chapter 7 ends with the first line of the complaint drying on papyrus as the bells rang for sundown.

Outside the door, footsteps approached—measured, official.

And Khaem understood that he was about to argue with a system that could erase him mid-sentence.

But he also understood something new.

A system that erases must still read.

And reading, in the end, is a vulnerability.
content.id:
Lonceng itu tidak berbunyi seperti ibadah.

Lonceng itu berbunyi seperti penanda waktu.

Menjelang sore, Khaem dan Nebet tiba di Rumah Pemurnian lewat gang belakang tempat linen masuk dan sampah keluar. Tempat itu tampak tidak berubah: dinding diputihkan, lantai bersih, kekejaman tenang bernama keteraturan.

Namun Khaem merasakan segel balik di pergelangannya berdenyut samar, seolah mengingatkan bahwa ia hadir karena izin.

Nebet menyembunyikan kotak metal di bawah ikatan kain cucian, bergerak seperti perempuan yang urusannya memang urusan kecil.

Di pintu layanan, seorang pelayan muda mencoba menahan.

Nebet tidak berdebat.

Ia menyodorkan sebuah label tanah liat—kosong, tampak resmi, lembap di pinggir—dan berkata, 'Stok pengganti. Permintaan ahli juru tulis.'

Mata pelayan itu jatuh pada label.

Lalu menghindar.

Di kota ini, label lebih berkuasa daripada wajah.

Mereka masuk.

Ruang pembalseman berbau natron dan resin aras. Perut Khaem mengencang oleh akrab.

Ia mengharap Hori.

Ia takut Hori.

Mereka menemukannya di ruang samping tempat tubuh ditimbang dan didata sebelum sayatan pertama.

Hori membungkuk di atas buku kas, pena buluh bergerak, wajahnya sabar seperti batu.

Saat menatap, pandangannya tidak jatuh pada Nebet.

Pandangan itu jatuh pada pergelangan Khaem.

Pada segel balik.

Wajah Hori menegang—bukan marah, melainkan menghitung.

'Kau membawa berkas ke ruanganku,' kata Hori pelan.

Nebet menjawab, 'Kami membawa bukti.'

Hori meletakkan pena.

'Bukti milik juru tulis,' katanya.

Khaem maju.

Ia tidak membungkuk.

Rasanya berbahaya untuk tidak.

Ia berkata, 'Mereka memindahkan nama si mati. Kantor lumbung mengecap. Rumah mayat menyiapkan. Buku kas belakang menulis.'

Mata Hori melirik ikatan Nebet.

'Dan kalian mencuri label,' katanya.

Nebet tidak menyangkal.

Ia menyingkap kain cucian dan memperlihatkan kotak metal sedikit saja agar Hori melihat tali kuilnya sudah putus.

Hori menarik napas pelan.

Ia tidak bereaksi seperti orang terkejut.

Ia bereaksi seperti orang yang mengonfirmasi kecurigaan yang dipaksa ia pelihara.

'Kalian tak seharusnya membukanya,' gumamnya.

Tenggorokan Khaem menegang.

'Kau tahu,' kata Khaem.

Hori tidak menjawab.

Ia melirik koridor.

Rumah Pemurnian punya banyak ruang.

Rumah Pemurnian juga punya banyak telinga.

Hori berdiri dan menutup pintu.

Lalu ia melakukan sesuatu yang belum pernah Khaem lihat.

Ia mengeluarkan batu segel kecil dari kantong ikat pinggang.

Bukan segel publik kuil.

Segel pribadi.

Ia menekannya ke olesan tanah liat di kusen pintu.

Udara berubah.

Ruangan mendadak terasa lebih kecil, lebih tertahan.

Alis Nebet terangkat.

'Cap sunyi,' katanya.

Hori tidak mengiyakan.

Ia kembali duduk.

'Kalian punya sidang,' katanya, kembali menatap pergelangan Khaem. 'Hakim tidak memberi sidang tanpa mengharap hasil.'

Khaem bertanya, 'Hasil apa?'

Hori mengetuk buku kas.

'Hasil yang bisa diberkas,' katanya.

Berkas.

Bahkan dalam keadilan.

Hori membuka laci dan mengeluarkan sebuah label tanah liat.

Itu label jenazah tanpa nama.

Masih kosong.

Marah Khaem menyala.

Hori mengangkatnya.

'Sebelum senja,' katanya, 'label ini harus punya nama, atau tubuh itu resmi jadi tak bertuan—oleh hukum dan ritus.'

Suara Nebet menajam.

'Oleh hukum siapa?'

Senyum Hori tipis.

'Oleh hukum yang membuat orang mati terus bergerak,' jawabnya.

Khaem menatap label kosong dan merasakan kejamnya birokrasi sepenuhnya: ia tidak perlu jahat.

Ia hanya perlu berlanjut.

Nebet meletakkan pita lumbung di meja.

Lalu ia meletakkan satu label 'ditolak' dari kotak metal di sampingnya.

Label itu masih menyimpan hantu nama tertekan.

Khaem merasakan tarikan di belakang mata.

Hori tidak menyentuhnya.

Ia menatapnya seperti orang menatap ular.

'Kalian mau aku membaca tanah liat curian,' kata Hori.

Khaem menjawab, 'Kami mau kau menyaksikannya.'

Mata Hori menyempit.

'Menyaksikan adalah cap,' katanya.

Khaem berkata, 'Kalau begitu cap.'

Sunyi memanjang.

Akhirnya Hori mengambil pena bersih dan mencelupkannya ke tinta.

Ia tidak menulis.

Ia menggambar satu tanda kecil di pinggir pita lumbung.

Bukan segel resmi.

Catatan.

Tanda juru tulis bahwa sesuatu sudah dilihat.

Segel balik Khaem berdenyut.

Nebet mengembus.

Hori menatap Khaem dan berkata, 'Inilah pengadilan yang kalian dapat.'

Khaem mengernyit.

Hori melanjut.

'Tidak ada aula marmer. Tidak ada deret dewa. Sidang terjadi di tempat berkas terjadi.

Saat senja, saat orang mati difinalkan.'

Khaem mengerti.

Rumah Pemurnian adalah ruang sidang.

Udara natron adalah kursi saksi.

Hori menoleh ke Nebet.

'Kalau kuil tahu kau di sini, kau akan dikoreksi sebelum malam selesai,' katanya.

Mulut Nebet mengencang.

'Kalau kau menolong kami,' tanya Nebet, 'apa yang terjadi padamu?'

Tatapan Hori tidak melunak.

'Kalau aku menolong, aku jadi terlihat,' katanya.

Khaem merasakan berat kata itu.

Terlihat berarti rentan.

Berarti diberkas.

Hori berdiri.

Ia pergi ke ruang simpan belakang tempat barang sitaan disimpan: minyak, pisau, jimat—apa pun yang ditemukan pada jenazah dan tak boleh ikut kubur.

Ia kembali membawa buntalan.

Satu guci kecil tinta, tersegel.

Satu batu segel lagi.

Dan satu pita papirus dengan kepala-kepala formal.

Jantung Khaem menghantam.

Hori meletakkannya di meja dan berkata, 'Kalau kalian menuduh registri, kalian harus melakukannya dalam formatnya.

Kalian akan mengajukan pengaduan.'

Nebet tertawa sekali.

Bahkan tawanya terdengar lelah.

Hori mengabaikan.

Ia menunjuk kepala papirus.

'Nama pengadu,' katanya.

Khaem tersentak.

Hori menambah, 'Nama tergugat.'

Mata Nebet menajam.

Khaem bertanya, 'Siapa yang kita tulis? Kuil?'

Hori menggeleng.

'Bukan,' katanya. 'Registri.'

Khaem terpaku.

Bagaimana menggugat buku kas?

Hori menjawab seolah mendengar pertanyaan itu.

'Kalian tidak menggugatnya,' katanya. 'Kalian memaksa ia mengakui kontradiksi. Kalian mengajukan catatan yang bertabrakan dengan catatannya.

Kalau hakim menerima tabrakan itu, buku kas harus memegang dua versi.

Dan kalau ia memegang dua, ia tidak bisa mengoreksi dengan rapi.'

Nebet mencondong.

'Nama si mati?' tanyanya.

Hori menunjuk label ditolak.

'Cari,' katanya. 'Tapi jangan ucapkan di sini sebelum pengaduan ditulis.

Nama yang diucap bisa dipakai.

Tuduhan tertulis lebih sulit dicuri.'

Khaem menatap Nebet.

Untuk pertama kali sejak tepi sungai, ada jalur yang menyerupai rencana.

Rencana yang mengerikan.

Hori mendekati pintu dan mendengarkan.

Di luar, lonceng mulai bergeser nada.

Senja mendekat.

Hori kembali dan berkata, 'Saat matahari menyentuh cakrawala, para juru tulis akan datang untuk label final.

Mereka mengharap patuh.

Kalian memberi dokumen sebagai gantinya.'

Nebet bertanya, 'Kalau mereka menolak menerimanya?'

Ekspresi Hori tidak berubah.

'Maka sidang jadi fisik,' katanya.

Khaem menelan.

Ia sadar pengadilan itu tidak dilindungi tembok.

Pengadilan dilindungi prosedur.

Kalau prosedur runtuh, yang tersisa hanya tubuh.

Khaem mencelupkan pena ke tinta tersegel.

Ia mulai menulis.

Bukan doa.

Bukan pengakuan.

Pengaduan.

Di Rumah Pemurnian, di bawah bau natron, Khaem belajar bentuk sihir paling tajam di kota: dokumen yang memaksa pihak lain menjawab.

Chapter 7 berakhir saat baris pertama pengaduan mengering di papirus ketika lonceng berbunyi untuk senja.

Di luar pintu, langkah kaki mendekat—terukur, resmi.

Dan Khaem mengerti bahwa ia akan berdebat dengan sistem yang bisa menghapusnya di tengah kalimat.

Tapi ia juga mengerti sesuatu yang baru.

Sistem yang menghapus tetap harus membaca.

Dan membaca, pada akhirnya, adalah kerentanan.

content.en:
Khaem writes the complaint with hands that still smell of natron.

The headings stare back like demands.

NAME OF COMPLAINANT.

He writes: Khaem.

The ink dries.

Then the papyrus does something no ordinary papyrus should do.

The letters thin.

Not vanishing—reclassifying.

A neat line appears beneath:

FULL NAME REQUIRED.

Hori’s face goes still.

Nebet whispers, 'The court is the registry. It won’t accept a file without a complete key.'

Khaem’s throat tightens.

He was never taught his full name.

Not because it was unimportant.

Because it was too important.

A full name is leverage.

The door to the side chamber shudders as clerks arrive in the corridor.

Hori says, quietly, 'There is one copy of your full name that the temple trusts.

The one in my master ledger.'

Khaem looks at him.

Hori adds, 'And there is one reason I never showed it to you.

Because once you read it, you can be accused of stealing it.'

Nebet says, 'So let them accuse. A court needs a complainant.'

Hori hesitates, then opens a hidden compartment under the weighing table.

He pulls out a thin slate tablet wrapped in linen.

On it: a list of apprentices, with their formal names, copied for internal audits.

Khaem finds his line.

He reads it.

The counterseal on his wrist throbs.

The letters on the slate feel heavier than any stone.

Because now his full name exists in his mind.

And minds can be searched.

Khaem writes the full name into the complaint.

The papyrus accepts it.

A faint impression appears at the bottom: FILE OPENED.

At the same moment, the corridor goes quieter.

The clerks outside stop talking.

As if they just felt a new file appear with his identity inside.

Chapter 7 ends with Khaem realizing the complaint did not only force the registry to read.

It forced the registry to finally know him completely.

To be heard, he had to make himself legible.

And legible is dangerously close to owned.
content.id:
Khaem menulis pengaduan dengan tangan yang masih berbau natron.

Kepala-kepala formulir menatap seperti tuntutan.

NAMA PENGADU.

Ia menulis: Khaem.

Tintanya kering.

Lalu papirus itu melakukan sesuatu yang tak seharusnya dilakukan papirus biasa.

Huruf-hurufnya menipis.

Bukan hilang—diklasifikasi ulang.

Satu baris rapi muncul di bawah:

NAMA LENGKAP DIPERLUKAN.

Wajah Hori membeku.

Nebet berbisik, 'Pengadilan itu registri. Ia tidak menerima berkas tanpa kunci lengkap.'

Tenggorokan Khaem mengencang.

Ia tidak pernah diajari nama lengkap.

Bukan karena tidak penting.

Karena terlalu penting.

Nama lengkap adalah tuas.

Pintu ruang samping bergetar saat juru tulis tiba di koridor.

Hori berkata pelan, 'Ada satu salinan nama lengkapmu yang kuil percaya.

Yang ada di buku kas indukku.'

Khaem menatap.

Hori menambah, 'Dan ada satu alasan kenapa aku tak pernah menunjukkannya.

Karena begitu kau membacanya, kau bisa dituduh mencurinya.'

Nebet berkata, 'Biarkan mereka menuduh. Pengadilan butuh pengadu.'

Hori ragu, lalu membuka kompartemen tersembunyi di bawah meja timbang.

Ia menarik lempeng tipis dibungkus linen.

Di atasnya: daftar magang, dengan nama formal, disalin untuk audit internal.

Khaem menemukan baris miliknya.

Ia membacanya.

Segel balik di pergelangannya berdenyut.

Huruf di lempeng terasa lebih berat daripada batu mana pun.

Karena sekarang nama lengkapnya ada di kepalanya.

Dan kepala bisa digeledah.

Khaem menulis nama lengkap itu ke pengaduan.

Papirus menerimanya.

Samar muncul jejak di bagian bawah: BERKAS DIBUKA.

Pada saat yang sama, koridor jadi lebih sunyi.

Juru tulis di luar berhenti bicara.

Seolah mereka baru saja merasakan berkas baru muncul dengan identitasnya di dalam.

Chapter 7 berakhir saat Khaem sadar pengaduan itu bukan hanya memaksa registri membaca.

Pengaduan itu memaksa registri akhirnya mengenalnya sepenuhnya.

Agar didengar, ia harus membuat dirinya terbaca.

Dan terbaca itu sangat dekat dengan dimiliki.

content.en:
Hori listens to Nebet’s proof and Khaem’s accusation without moving his face.

He closes the door.

He places the silence seal.

And then he says, 'No.'

Not as a threat.

As a decision.

He pushes the blank tag of the nameless body toward Khaem.

'Fill it,' he says. 'Give the dead man any name that will pass. Do not drag my house into a war with the registry.'

Khaem’s hands tremble.

Nebet’s voice sharpens. 'That is exactly how the registry wins. Everyone chooses a passing lie.'

Hori’s eyes harden.

'And everyone who chooses a heroic truth gets corrected,' he replies.

He points to Khaem’s counterseal.

'Your hearing is not a gift. It is a net.'

Khaem argues that a filed complaint is the only chance.

Hori shakes his head.

'A complaint requires a witness.

I will not be that stamp.'

Nebet steps forward and says, 'Then I will.'

Hori’s gaze flicks to her, and for the first time he looks afraid.

Not of the temple.

Of the consequences of visibility.

Nebet takes the reed pen and draws her witness mark on the granary strip.

The air shifts.

The silence seal on the doorframe cracks—audibly.

A thin snap, like clay breaking.

Outside, the corridor hushes.

Then footsteps multiply.

Hori whispers, 'You just rang the bell.'

Nebet does not flinch.

She tells Khaem to keep writing.

Khaem writes faster as official voices gather.

Chapter 7 ends with Hori standing between the door and the table, realizing he tried to stay neutral and failed anyway.

The registry does not allow neutral.

It only allows compliant or corrected.
content.id:
Hori mendengar bukti Nebet dan tuduhan Khaem tanpa menggerakkan wajah.

Ia menutup pintu.

Ia menekan cap sunyi.

Lalu ia berkata, 'Tidak.'

Bukan ancaman.

Keputusan.

Ia mendorong label kosong jenazah tanpa nama ke arah Khaem.

'Isi,' katanya. 'Beri si mati nama apa pun yang lolos. Jangan seret rumahku ke perang dengan registri.'

Tangan Khaem gemetar.

Suara Nebet menajam. 'Itulah cara registri menang. Semua orang memilih bohong yang lolos.'

Mata Hori mengeras.

'Dan semua orang yang memilih kebenaran heroik akan dikoreksi,' jawabnya.

Ia menunjuk segel balik di pergelangan Khaem.

'Sidangmu bukan hadiah. Itu jala.'

Khaem berargumen pengaduan tertulis satu-satunya kesempatan.

Hori menggeleng.

'Pengaduan butuh saksi.

Aku tidak akan jadi cap itu.'

Nebet maju dan berkata, 'Kalau begitu aku.'

Tatapan Hori melompat ke Nebet, dan untuk pertama kalinya ia tampak takut.

Bukan pada kuil.

Pada akibat dari terlihat.

Nebet mengambil pena buluh dan menggambar tanda saksi di pita lumbung.

Udara berubah.

Cap sunyi di kusen pintu retak—terdengar.

Keretak tipis, seperti tanah liat pecah.

Di luar, koridor mendadak hening.

Lalu langkah kaki bertambah.

Hori berbisik, 'Kau baru saja membunyikan lonceng.'

Nebet tidak gentar.

Ia menyuruh Khaem terus menulis.

Khaem menulis lebih cepat saat suara resmi berkumpul.

Chapter 7 berakhir dengan Hori berdiri di antara pintu dan meja, sadar ia mencoba netral dan gagal juga.

Registri tidak mengenal netral.

Registri hanya mengenal patuh atau dikoreksi.

content.en:
Sundown arrives.

The clerks enter the House of Purification with their baskets of final tags, faces smooth with authority.

Khaem expects refusal.

He expects laughter.

He expects violence.

Instead the lead clerk looks at the complaint and nods.

'Proper format,' he says.

He takes the papyrus as if it is ordinary.

He stamps it with a seal Khaem does not recognize.

Then he hands Khaem a small clay token: a receipt.

'Case logged,' he says. 'Hearing acknowledged.'

Nebet’s eyes narrow.

Nothing bureaucratic is that generous.

Khaem feels his counterseal throb.

The lead clerk smiles politely.

'You have done the correct thing,' he says.

Khaem’s stomach drops.

Correct.

The clerk’s seal is not acceptance.

It is capture.

The receipt is proof that Khaem volunteered his accusation into the registry’s system.

Now the registry owns the timeline.

Owns the hearing.

Owns the file.

After the clerks leave, Hori examines the receipt.

His face tightens.

He says, 'This token is used for retrieval.'

Khaem asks, 'Retrieval of what?'

Hori answers, 'Of the complainant.'

The receipt is not for a document.

It is for a body.

Chapter 7 ends with Khaem realizing the clerks did not fight the complaint because they wanted it.

A filed accusation is a list of names, places, and acts—written neatly by the victim.

It is the easiest map an erasure system can receive.

And he just handed it over in perfect handwriting.
content.id:
Senja datang.

Para juru tulis masuk ke Rumah Pemurnian dengan keranjang label final, wajah mulus oleh otoritas.

Khaem mengira mereka akan menolak.

Ia mengira mereka akan menertawakan.

Ia mengira akan ada kekerasan.

Ternyata juru tulis pemimpin menatap pengaduan dan mengangguk.

'Formatnya benar,' katanya.

Ia mengambil papirus itu seolah itu hal biasa.

Ia mengecap dengan segel yang Khaem tidak kenal.

Lalu ia menyerahkan token tanah liat kecil: tanda terima.

'Kasus tercatat,' katanya. 'Sidang diakui.'

Mata Nebet menyempit.

Tak ada hal birokratis yang semurah hati itu.

Segel balik Khaem berdenyut.

Juru tulis pemimpin tersenyum sopan.

'Kau sudah melakukan hal yang benar,' katanya.

Perut Khaem jatuh.

Benar.

Segel juru tulis itu bukan penerimaan.

Itu penangkapan.

Tanda terima itu bukti bahwa Khaem memasukkan tuduhannya sendiri secara sukarela ke sistem registri.

Kini registri punya garis waktu.

Punya sidang.

Punya berkas.

Setelah para juru tulis pergi, Hori memeriksa tanda terima.

Wajahnya menegang.

Ia berkata, 'Token ini dipakai untuk penjemputan.'

Khaem bertanya, 'Penjemputan apa?'

Hori menjawab, 'Penjemputan pengadu.'

Tanda terima itu bukan untuk dokumen.

Itu untuk tubuh.

Chapter 7 berakhir saat Khaem sadar para juru tulis tidak melawan pengaduan karena mereka menginginkannya.

Tuduhan yang sudah diberkas adalah daftar nama, tempat, dan tindakan—ditulis rapi oleh korban.

Itu peta termudah bagi sistem penghapusan.

Dan ia baru saja menyerahkannya dengan tulisan tangan sempurna.

content.en:
Khaem keeps his promise: he does not speak the dead man’s name aloud.

He searches the rejected tags for the original.

He finds a half-scraped imprint that fits like a missing tooth.

As sundown nears, Hori brings the blank tag for the body.

Khaem presses the rejected tag against it, clay to clay, trying to transfer the ghost of the name without voicing it.

The air in the room tightens.

Natron dust lifts.

The blank tag darkens.

Then, to Khaem’s horror, a voice comes out of it.

Not loud.

Not supernatural in the way stories promise.

Administrative.

A voice that sounds like a man repeating a statement for the record.

It says: 'I object.'

Hori’s pen drops.

Nebet’s eyes widen.

The tag speaks again.

It does not give a name.

It gives a fact.

'I was marked unclaimed while alive.'

Khaem’s skin goes cold.

The dead man is not haunting.

He is testifying.

The back ledger has been editing him, but something in the counterseal’s hearing has opened a channel.

A witness has arrived who cannot be bribed.

Because he is already dead.

Footsteps approach in the corridor.

Khaem realizes the complaint is no longer the only document.

The body itself has become a record.

Chapter 7 ends with the unclaimed dead man’s tag repeating, calmly, 'I object,' as if learning the first sentence of court.

And Khaem understands the most dangerous thing in a bureaucracy is not rebellion.

It is a file that starts speaking back.
content.id:
Khaem menepati janji: ia tidak mengucapkan nama si mati keras-keras.

Ia mencari label asli di antara label ditolak.

Ia menemukan bekas setengah terkikis yang pas seperti gigi hilang.

Saat senja mendekat, Hori membawa label kosong tubuh itu.

Khaem menekan label ditolak itu ke label kosong—tanah liat ke tanah liat—berusaha memindahkan hantu nama tanpa mengucapkannya.

Udara di ruangan menegang.

Debu natron terangkat.

Label kosong menggelap.

Lalu, membuat Khaem ngeri, sebuah suara keluar darinya.

Tidak keras.

Tidak supranatural seperti janji cerita.

Administratif.

Suara seperti orang mengulang pernyataan demi catatan.

Ia berkata: 'Saya keberatan.'

Pena Hori jatuh.

Mata Nebet membesar.

Label itu bicara lagi.

Ia tidak menyebut nama.

Ia menyebut fakta.

'Aku ditandai tak bertuan saat masih hidup.'

Kulit Khaem dingin.

Si mati tidak menghantui.

Ia bersaksi.

Buku kas belakang mengeditnya, tapi sidang segel balik membuka saluran.

Saksi hadir yang tidak bisa disuap.

Karena ia sudah mati.

Langkah kaki mendekat di koridor.

Khaem sadar pengaduan bukan lagi satu-satunya dokumen.

Tubuh itu sendiri menjadi catatan.

Chapter 7 berakhir saat label si mati mengulang tenang, 'Saya keberatan,' seolah sedang belajar kalimat pertama di pengadilan.

Dan Khaem paham hal paling berbahaya bagi birokrasi bukan pemberontakan.

Melainkan berkas yang mulai bicara balik.
